reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 must fix two high-severity blocking issues before this plan can advance. ISS-001 requires replacing flat TF-IDF tokenisation with a longest-non-overlapping-prefix extraction step before vectorisation, so that /api/v1/billing and /api/v1/billing-admin are split at the point of divergence rather than sharing a /api/v1/billing token. ISS-002 requires adding a NetworkPolicy resource to the Jinja2 Helm chart template that restricts OPA sidecar ingress to the owning namespace and egress to the bundle registry URL and Cloud SQL proxy port only. The rest of the round-1 architecture is sound: Workload Identity Federation, pgcrypto encryption, and the five-subcommand CLI structure all pass security review. The plan quality target is moderate — data_model and cost sections provide adequate but not exhaustive detail.

fix_report.action_taken: Added longest-non-overlapping-prefix extraction step to PrefixAnchoredClusterer before TF-IDF vectorisation. The extractor splits each rule's resource path at the deepest prefix that does not overlap with any other domain's prefix set, using a trie over all rule paths. /api/v1/billing-admin rules now anchor to /api/v1/billing-admin rather than sharing the /api/v1/billing token.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Integration test with billing/billing-admin fixture produces two distinct clusters with zero cross-domain rule contamination in 5 of 5 runs.

fix_report.action_taken: Added NetworkPolicy Jinja2 template block to Helm chart skeleton. Every generated chart now includes a NetworkPolicy that allows ingress from the owning namespace only and egress to the OPA bundle registry (port 443) and Cloud SQL proxy (port 5432). All other egress denied by default.
fix_report.expected_outcome: kubectl apply of generated Helm chart in staging shows OPA sidecar pod cannot reach pods outside its namespace or any non-whitelisted external endpoint.

reference.title: ACL Decomposition Tool — Round 2 Plan
reference.executive_summary: This revision fixes the two blocking round-1 issues: TF-IDF clustering now anchors to longest non-overlapping path prefixes via a trie-based extractor, eliminating cross-domain contamination; and every generated Helm chart now includes a NetworkPolicy restricting OPA sidecar traffic to the owning namespace and whitelisted egress only. The five-subcommand Python/Typer CLI architecture, Workload Identity Federation, pgcrypto encryption of ACL blobs, and 7-day shadow evaluation period are confirmed. Plan quality is moderate — adequate for the 3-person platform team scope.
reference.architecture_overview: The ACL decomposition pipeline runs as a Python 3.12/Typer CLI with five subcommands. acl-decomp analyse reads the monolithic ACL YAML from GitLab file API, decrypts the ACL blob, and runs the PrefixAnchoredClusterer: a trie is built over all resource paths, then each rule is anchored to its longest non-overlapping prefix before TF-IDF vectorisation. This fix (ISS-001) ensures /api/v1/billing and /api/v1/billing-admin are split correctly. acl-decomp plan writes per-domain decomposition_plans rows. acl-decomp generate renders Helm charts and Rego bundles per domain via Jinja2; the chart skeleton now includes a NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix) restricting ingress to the owning namespace and egress to bundle registry port 443 and Cloud SQL proxy port 5432. acl-decomp deploy creates a GitLab MR per domain with the generated artifacts, assigning the domain owner as required reviewer. acl-decomp validate runs OPA test suite against Rego bundle and compares shadow-mode decisions against the monolith baseline for 7 days before cutover is permitted.
reference.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12 with Typer 0.12 and Click 8.1 for CLI scaffolding; scikit-learn 1.4 TfidfVectorizer with custom tokeniser for prefix-anchored clustering; Jinja2 3.1 for Helm and Rego template rendering; psycopg3 for PostgreSQL; google-cloud-secret-manager for credential retrieval. Infrastructure: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (eu-west1, db-g1-small) with pgcrypto extension; GCS bucket for Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs; GKE Autopilot cluster for OPA sidecar deployment; GitLab CI with Workload Identity Federation OIDC. Tooling: ruff and mypy --strict in CI; pytest with 80% coverage gate; OPA test runner for Rego policy regression tests.
reference.functional_feature_map: F1 — ACL ingestion: read monolithic ACL file from GitLab repository via REST API; decrypt and parse YAML/JSON into rule objects; store encrypted snapshot in acl_rule_sets. F2 — Prefix-anchored clustering (ISS-001 fix): build trie over all resource paths; assign each rule to longest non-overlapping prefix; run TF-IDF on prefix-anchored token sets with configurable threshold (default 0.65); write cluster assignments to decomposition_plans. F3 — Helm and Rego generation: render chart per domain from Jinja2 templates including NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix), OPA DaemonSet, ConfigMap referencing versioned bundle URL; generate Rego bundle from cluster rule set. F4 — GitLab MR automation: create MR per domain with generated files; assign domain owner as required reviewer; link MR to decomposition_plans record for SOC 2 traceability. F5 — Shadow validation: run acl-decomp validate in 24-hour cron job for 7 days; compare OPA decisions to monolith baseline; block cutover if divergence_count > 0.
reference.system_components: acl-decomp CLI (Python/Typer): five subcommands, each stateless except for PostgreSQL state reads/writes. PrefixAnchoredClusterer: trie-based prefix extraction followed by TF-IDF vectorisation with configurable similarity threshold; produces domain-to-rules mapping. HelmGenerator: Jinja2 rendering of chart skeleton including NetworkPolicy, OPA DaemonSet, and ConfigMap; writes chart archive to GCS. PolicyTestRunner: subprocess wrapper for OPA test runner; captures per-rule pass/fail; writes report to test_coverage_reports. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: stores decomposition_plans, acl_rule_sets (encrypted), migration_batches, test_coverage_reports. GCS bucket (eu-west1): versioned Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs keyed by plan_id/domain. GitLab CI: MR-per-domain pipeline stages gated on platform engineer approval.
reference.workflows: ACL Decomposition Workflow: platform engineer runs acl-decomp analyse --input refs/heads/main:access/acl.yaml --gitlab-project 42; CLI fetches file via GitLab API with short-lived OIDC token, decrypts blob, builds trie over 50k resource paths, anchors each rule to longest non-overlapping prefix, runs TF-IDF clustering, writes 20 decomposition_plans rows with domain/prefix/rule_count; engineer reviews cluster assignment report before proceeding. Helm Generation and MR Workflow: acl-decomp generate --plan-id <uuid> renders Helm chart and Rego bundle for each domain; chart includes NetworkPolicy restricting OPA sidecar to owning namespace; artifacts uploaded to GCS; acl-decomp deploy creates GitLab MR per domain with domain owner as required reviewer and decomposition_plans link in MR description for SOC 2 CC7.1 evidence. Shadow Validation Workflow: after MR merge and OPA sidecar deployment, acl-decomp validate runs daily for 7 days; each run sends test requests to both monolith ACL and OPA sidecar endpoints and records divergence in migration_batches.divergence_count; cutover is permitted only when divergence_count = 0 for 3 consecutive days.
reference.data_model: decomposition_plans (id UUID PK, acl_source_ref TEXT, domain TEXT, resource_prefix TEXT, rule_count INT, similarity_threshold FLOAT, status ENUM planning/generating/deployed/cutover/failed, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). acl_rule_sets (id UUID PK, plan_id UUID FK REFERENCES decomposition_plans, encrypted_blob BYTEA encrypted via pgcrypto pgp_sym_encrypt with GCP KMS-derived key, rule_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). migration_batches (id UUID PK, plan_id UUID FK, gitlab_mr_iid INT, shadow_start TIMESTAMPTZ, shadow_end TIMESTAMPTZ, divergence_count INT DEFAULT 0, cutover_approved_by TEXT, cutover_at TIMESTAMPTZ). test_coverage_reports (id UUID PK, plan_id UUID FK, total_fixtures INT, passed INT, failed INT, opa_version TEXT, report_jsonb JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
reference.api_design: Internal CLI API (Typer subcommands): acl-decomp analyse --input <git-ref:path> --gitlab-project <id> --threshold <float=0.65>. acl-decomp plan --analyse-id <uuid>. acl-decomp generate --plan-id <uuid> --domain <name> (or --all for all domains). acl-decomp deploy --plan-id <uuid> --domain <name>. acl-decomp validate --batch-id <uuid> --compare-endpoint <monolith-url>. All subcommands accept --dry-run flag for no-write preview. GitLab API used internally: GET /api/v4/projects/:id/repository/files/:path, POST /api/v4/projects/:id/merge_requests. No public-facing HTTP API — tool is operator-only.
reference.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity Federation: GKE service account annotated with iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account; CI job presents OIDC token exchanged for short-lived GCP access token; no static SA key files. pgcrypto: acl_rule_sets.encrypted_blob stored with pgp_sym_encrypt using AES-256 key derived from Cloud KMS. NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix): every generated Helm chart includes a NetworkPolicy allowing ingress from owning namespace only and egress to bundle registry FQDN port 443 and Cloud SQL proxy port 5432; all other traffic denied. SOC 2 compliance: CC6.1 — Workload Identity enforces least-privilege; CC6.3 — domain owner MR approval before deployment; CC7.1 — decomposition_plans and GitLab MR IID linked in migration_batches for change evidence. Secret rotation: GitLab token and PostgreSQL DSN rotated quarterly via GCP Secret Manager versions.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline (5 stages): lint (ruff check + mypy --strict, blocks on any type error), unit-test (pytest -x --cov=acl_decomp --cov-fail-under=80, covers clustering and Helm generation modules), integration-test (Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar in CI runner, end-to-end analyse/plan/generate with 500-rule fixture), helm-validate (helm template | kubectl apply --dry-run=client against staging kubeconfig), deploy-dry-run (acl-decomp generate against staging ACL snapshot, verifies Jinja2 rendering and NetworkPolicy presence, does not push MR). Production deployment: acl-decomp deploy creates GitLab MR; after domain owner approval and 7-day shadow period with zero divergence, platform engineer runs acl-decomp cutover --batch-id <uuid> which updates domain router to OPA sidecar endpoint. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo deployment/opa-sidecar-<domain> restores previous image; monolith ACL service remains active as fallback during shadow period. Environments: dev (minikube, local PostgreSQL), staging (GKE staging namespace, Cloud SQL staging eu-west1), production (GKE prod namespace, Cloud SQL prod eu-west1 with automated daily backups to GCS).
reference.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring: structured JSON logs from every Typer subcommand (fields: plan_id, domain, subcommand, actor, duration_ms, outcome). Custom metrics: clustering_duration_seconds (histogram per domain), helm_charts_generated_total (counter), shadow_divergence_count (gauge per batch), opa_policy_eval_latency_ms (histogram from OPA sidecar prometheus endpoint). Alerting: Cloud Monitoring alert if shadow_divergence_count increases after previously reaching zero (potential OPA bundle corruption). GitLab CI pipeline duration tracked; alert if analyse stage exceeds 10 minutes for 50k-rule input.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Cloud SQL db-g1-small (PostgreSQL 16, eu-west1): approximately $30/month during active decomposition cycles; instance paused when no pipeline is running to reduce cost. GCS bucket storage: Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs for 20 domains estimated under 500 MB — negligible cost. GKE Autopilot OPA sidecars: 20 DaemonSets at 0.1 CPU / 128 MB each — approximately $40/month per cluster. Total tool infrastructure cost under $80/month. Scaling: TF-IDF clustering is the compute-intensive step; parallelising analyse across domain subsets via concurrent GitLab CI jobs reduces pipeline duration linearly with job count.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP (Weeks 1-3): CLI scaffold with Typer, PostgreSQL schema and Alembic migrations, ACL file ingestion from GitLab API, PrefixAnchoredClusterer with trie-based prefix extraction (ISS-001 fix), basic Helm skeleton with NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix), unit tests for clustering and Helm generation at 80% coverage. Phase 2 — Pilot (Weeks 4-6): full Jinja2 Rego bundle generation, OPA test runner integration, GitLab MR automation for 5 pilot domains, shadow validation for pilot domains, CI pipeline with all 5 stages. Phase 3 — Full Rollout (Weeks 7-12): generate and deploy remaining 15 domains, cutover orchestration for approved domains, SOC 2 audit trail report generation, documentation and runbook for platform team handoff.
reference.development_guidelines: Typer subcommands must be idempotent: re-running generate for the same plan_id overwrites previous artifacts without duplicating PostgreSQL records. PrefixAnchoredClusterer threshold is configurable at runtime; document the algorithm with a billing/billing-admin worked example in the module docstring. All Jinja2 templates must include a NetworkPolicy block — CI helm-validate stage enforces this via grep check on rendered output. mypy --strict enforced in CI; no Any types in clustering or Helm generation modules. pytest fixtures use real PostgreSQL via testcontainers to avoid mock drift.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Cross-domain rules (ACL rules spanning multiple resource prefixes) cannot be automatically assigned by the trie extractor and require manual domain owner classification — mitigated by acl-decomp analyse flagging ambiguous rules and blocking plan phase until resolved. OPA sidecar cold start latency on GKE Autopilot node provisioning may cause brief policy evaluation gaps during initial deployment — mitigated by pre-warming sidecar pods 24 hours before cutover window. Tradeoff: trie-based prefix anchoring adds 15-20 seconds to the analyse phase for 50k rules versus raw TF-IDF, but eliminates the domain contamination risk that makes raw TF-IDF unsafe for this use case.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Q1 (OPA sidecar egress scope): NetworkPolicy template allows egress to OPA bundle registry FQDN and Cloud SQL proxy port 5432 only; all other egress denied. Q2 (similarity threshold): default 0.65 chosen based on integration tests with billing/billing-admin fixture; configurable via --threshold flag to accommodate domain-specific ACL structures. Q3 (cutover approval authority): cutover approved by domain owner MR approval plus platform engineering lead sign-off in migration_batches.cutover_approved_by field.

parsed.title: Warehouse Operations Monitoring CLI Tool Architecture Plan
parsed.executive_summary: This plan outlines the architecture for a terminal-based CLI tool that provides real-time monitoring of warehouse operations, including live queue depths, worker productivity rates, and conveyor throughput metrics. The tool is designed to be simple, secure, and scalable, with a focus on resolving current focus issues and preserving user-confirmed requirements. It will run on Linux amd64 workstations, with a single-binary deployment approach, and includes future scope considerations for multi-warehouse support and historical data analysis.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture consists of a single Go binary that integrates with a Warehouse Management System (WMS) via Server-Sent Events (SSE) streams. It features a terminal TUI using the Bubbletea framework for styling and Lip Gloss for color-coding, with a Bubbletea model handling the SSE listener and a Cobra CLI for command-line flag parsing. The tool will be deployed on-premises using an Ansible playbook or scp, with a focus on security, scalability, and observability.

technology_stack.frontend_stack: Bubbletea TUI framework for terminal UI
technology_stack.backend_stack: Go 1.22 with Bubbletea TUI framework and Lip Gloss for styling
technology_stack.data_platform: In-memory only, with no local database
technology_stack.security_and_compliance: API key authentication for WMS SSE endpoint, TLS for all HTTP connections, no credential logging, config file permissions restricted to owner
technology_stack.observability: Optional --debug flag for troubleshooting SSE connectivity issues
technology_stack.cost_and_scaling: Single-binary deployment, no cloud hosting, designed for scalability with future multi-warehouse support
technology_stack.phased_implementation: Single iteration delivery with a 2-week field trial before broader rollout
technology_stack.development_guidelines: Use of GitHub Actions for CI/CD, Go vet, golangci-lint, and Go test for quality assurance
technology_stack.risks_and_tradeoffs: Potential WMS SSE endpoint IP allowlisting; version control and binary integrity must be maintained during deployment

backend.api_patterns: Outbound only: WMS SSE consumer, webhook dispatcher
backend.failure_handling: Exponential backoff reconnect, webhook dispatch failure logging and retry

data.entities: In-memory only: pick_queues, pack_stations, conveyor_segments, alert_state, last_updated, reconnecting_state
data.consistency_notes: Atomic updates via Bubbletea message processing

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow, Go vet, golangci-lint, Go test, release workflow with SHA256 checksum verification
devops.deployment_topology: Ansible playbook deployment, config file permissions, optional systemd user service

workflows.security_and_compliance: API key authentication, TLS, no credential logging, restricted config file permissions

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a stateless Go single-binary terminal TUI for warehouse monitoring. The key architectural pattern is a Bubbletea Elm-architecture program with an independent SSE listener goroutine feeding typed MetricUpdateMsg values through a channel into the Bubbletea Update function. The reconnect logic lives entirely in the SSE goroutine, so a 30-second reconnect delay does not block terminal rendering. No database or persistence layer is needed; all state is held in the Bubbletea Model struct.
reference.title: Warehouse Monitor CLI Tool Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A stateless Go 1.22 single-binary terminal TUI for warehouse supervisors to monitor live pick queue depths, pack station productivity rates, and conveyor throughput metrics streamed from a WMS via Server-Sent Events. Built with Bubbletea and Lip Gloss, the tool renders a color-coded dashboard that updates in real time, displays flashing alerts when configurable thresholds are breached, fires webhook escalation POSTs to configured endpoints, and gracefully reconnects to the WMS SSE stream with exponential backoff. Distributed as a Linux amd64 binary with zero shared library dependencies, deployed to warehouse workstations via Ansible playbook.
reference.architecture_overview: Single Go binary with two concurrent execution paths: the Bubbletea TUI program running on the main goroutine managing terminal rendering and keyboard events, and an SSE listener goroutine reading the WMS metric stream and sending typed MetricUpdateMsg values through a buffered channel into the Bubbletea message loop. The Bubbletea Update function processes MetricUpdateMsg values, updates in-memory Model state (pick_queues, pack_stations, conveyor_segments), evaluates configured threshold rules, and triggers AlertMsg values when thresholds are breached. A webhook dispatcher goroutine handles alert HTTP POSTs independently to prevent webhook latency from affecting dashboard rendering. The SSE listener owns reconnect logic with exponential backoff from 5 to 30 seconds, setting reconnecting_state=true in the Model during disconnects to render a visual indicator. Cobra parses startup flags; config file provides API key and threshold rules.
reference.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. TUI: Bubbletea v0.27 (Elm architecture event loop), Lip Gloss v0.10 (ANSI terminal styling for color-coded metric rows and alert indicators). CLI flags: Cobra v1.8. HTTP: net/http standard library for SSE streaming client and webhook POST dispatch (no third-party HTTP client needed). Build: GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build with -ldflags '-s -w' for binary size reduction. CI: GitHub Actions. Distribution: goreleaser for tagged release artifacts with SHA256 checksums.
reference.functional_feature_map: Live Pick Queue Dashboard: Bubbletea View function renders a Lip Gloss table with one row per warehouse zone showing pick_queue_depth with color coding (green below 50, yellow 50-80, red above 80 threshold). Pack Station Productivity: table section showing units/hour per station_id with color band based on target_rate threshold. Conveyor Throughput: items/minute per conveyor_segment_id with red highlight when below minimum_flow threshold. Threshold Alert Row: flashing alert row using Lip Gloss blink style rendered when any threshold is in alert_state=true, with rule name and current value. Webhook Escalation: when alert transitions from false to true, dispatcher goroutine sends HTTP POST to webhook URL with JSON payload containing zone_id, metric_type, current_value, threshold_value, triggered_at. SSE Reconnect Indicator: header row displays 'WMS DISCONNECTED - reconnecting in Xs' with orange Lip Gloss style when reconnecting_state=true.
reference.system_components: warehouse-monitor binary (Go 1.22, Linux amd64, ~8MB stripped). Cobra CLI: parses --config, --zone, --threshold (repeatable, format metric:value), --webhook-url, --refresh, --debug flags. Config loader: reads YAML config file from --config path or default ~/.warehouse-monitor/config.yaml, validates API key presence and config file permissions. SSE listener goroutine: net/http GET to WMS /api/v1/metrics/stream?zone=, reads event-stream lines, unmarshals JSON into MetricUpdateMsg, sends to msg channel, owns exponential backoff reconnect. Bubbletea program: Init/Update/View Elm functions; Update dispatches MetricUpdateMsg to update Model.pick_queues/pack_stations/conveyor_segments and evaluates threshold rules. Threshold evaluator: pure function checking current metric values against configured thresholds, returning AlertMsg slice. Webhook dispatcher goroutine: receives AlertMsg on alert channel, constructs JSON payload, sends POST with optional X-Webhook-Secret header, single retry on failure. Debug logger: writes JSON lines to stderr when --debug flag set, masking API key fields.
reference.workflows: Startup and SSE Connection: operator runs 'warehouse-monitor --zone ZONE-A --threshold pick_queue:80 --webhook-url https://alerts.internal/wh/wms' from terminal. Cobra parses flags, config loader reads API key from WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY env var, validates config file permissions are 0600, creates Bubbletea program and starts SSE listener goroutine. SSE listener opens net/http GET to WMS SSE endpoint with Authorization: ApiKey header and TLS. Bubbletea program renders initial dashboard frame with 'Connecting...' status. On first SSE event received, Model updates and full dashboard renders with live metric values. Threshold Breach and Alert Escalation: WMS SSE stream delivers event with pick_queue_depth=95 for ZONE-A exceeding the configured threshold of 80. SSE listener sends MetricUpdateMsg to Bubbletea channel. Bubbletea Update function processes msg, updates Model.pick_queues['ZONE-A']=95, calls threshold evaluator which returns AlertMsg for pick_queue rule. Update transitions Model.alert_state['pick_queue:ZONE-A']=true and sends AlertMsg to webhook channel. Bubbletea View renders ZONE-A row in red with flashing alert indicator. Webhook dispatcher goroutine receives AlertMsg, constructs JSON body {zone_id:'ZONE-A', metric:'pick_queue_depth', current:95, threshold:80, triggered_at:'2026-03-24T08:15:00Z'}, POSTs to configured URL with X-Webhook-Secret header. SSE Disconnect and Reconnect: WMS SSE connection drops. SSE listener detects EOF on response body, sends ReconnectMsg with wait_seconds=5. Bubbletea Update sets Model.reconnecting_state=true. View renders flashing orange 'WMS DISCONNECTED - reconnecting in 5s' header row. SSE listener sleeps 5s (doubling on each retry up to 30s), re-opens HTTP GET, sends ConnectedMsg on success. Update sets reconnecting_state=false, dashboard resumes normal rendering.
reference.data_model: No persistent data model. In-memory Bubbletea Model struct: ZoneQueues map[string]int (zone_id to pick_queue_depth), StationRates map[string]float64 (station_id to units/hour), ConveyorThroughput map[string]float64 (segment_id to items/minute), AlertState map[string]bool (threshold_rule_id to breach status), Thresholds []ThresholdRule (loaded from flags and config), LastUpdated time.Time, ReconnectingState bool, ReconnectCountdown int. ThresholdRule struct: MetricType string, ZoneOrID string, ThresholdValue float64, WebhookURL string. MetricUpdateMsg struct (Bubbletea Msg): ZoneQueues map, StationRates map, ConveyorThroughput map, EventTimestamp time.Time. AlertMsg struct: RuleID string, MetricType string, CurrentValue float64, ThresholdValue float64, TriggeredAt time.Time.
reference.api_design: No HTTP API exposed. Inbound: WMS SSE stream at GET /api/v1/metrics/stream?zone={zone_id}, event-stream content type, JSON data payloads with fields pick_queues (object), pack_stations (object), conveyor_segments (object), timestamp string. Authorization: ApiKey {api_key} header. Outbound: webhook POST to configured URL, Content-Type: application/json, optional X-Webhook-Secret: {secret} header, body: {zone_id, metric_type, current_value, threshold_value, triggered_at, tool_version}. Expected webhook response: 2xx for success; any non-2xx logged to stderr and retried once after 5s.
reference.security_and_compliance: API key handling: read from WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY env var first, then config file; never printed to stdout; masked as '***' in --debug log output. Config file permission check on startup: if permissions are 0644 or broader, tool logs error to stderr and exits with code 1. TLS: net/http client uses system CA bundle for TLS verification on both WMS SSE and webhook connections; InsecureSkipVerify is not configurable. Webhook security: optional secret_header_value in config file sent as X-Webhook-Secret header to authenticate webhook delivery. No network listening ports opened by the tool; all connections are outbound-only.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Build: GitHub Actions workflow runs go vet, golangci-lint, go test ./..., then GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -ldflags '-s -w' -o warehouse-monitor. Artifact uploaded and tagged release triggers goreleaser producing warehouse-monitor-linux-amd64.tar.gz with SHA256 checksum file. Distribution: Ansible playbook copies binary to /usr/local/bin/warehouse-monitor on target workstations, verifies SHA256 checksum, sets 0755 permissions, creates /etc/warehouse-monitor/ directory with 0700 permissions, copies config.yaml template with 0600 permissions, optionally installs systemd user service unit for auto-start at supervisor login. Environments: dev laptop (run with --debug against WMS staging SSE endpoint), pilot (3 warehouse workstations running against production WMS). No container runtime required on workstations.
reference.observability: No monitoring infrastructure for the tool itself. Operator-facing observability: --debug flag writes JSON log lines to stderr with fields timestamp, level, event (sse_connected, sse_disconnected, metric_update, threshold_breach, webhook_sent, webhook_failed, reconnect_attempt), masking api_key and webhook_secret fields. Debug log can be redirected to a file for post-shift support analysis: 'warehouse-monitor --debug 2>/tmp/monitor-debug.log'. Dashboard header row shows last_updated timestamp and SSE connection latency in milliseconds as passive observability indicators for supervisors.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud hosting cost; runs on existing warehouse workstation hardware. Development cost: estimated 80 person-hours for build and test. Go binary is approximately 8MB stripped; no external runtime dependencies. Scales to any number of warehouse workstations by copying the binary; each instance connects independently to the WMS SSE endpoint. WMS SSE endpoint load scales linearly with number of concurrent tool instances; WMS team should confirm SSE connection limits before broad rollout beyond 20 concurrent supervisors.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Build 2 weeks: SSE listener goroutine with exponential backoff reconnect, Bubbletea TUI with pick queue and pack station rows, Lip Gloss color-coded threshold styling, single threshold alert with webhook dispatch, Cobra CLI flags, config file loader with permission validation, GitHub Actions CI with unit tests for threshold evaluator and webhook payload builder, Linux amd64 binary build. Phase 2 Pilot 2 weeks: deploy to 3 pilot warehouses via Ansible playbook, collect supervisor feedback on dashboard layout and alert thresholds, fix any SSE connectivity or terminal rendering issues, validate webhook delivery to escalation endpoint. Phase 3 Rollout: deploy to remaining warehouses, add conveyor_segments display row, add --zone switching keyboard shortcut for multi-zone warehouses, optional systemd user service installation.
reference.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, and gocritic. go test for all business logic: threshold evaluator (table-driven tests covering all metric types and boundary values), webhook payload construction (verify JSON structure and field presence), config file permission validation (test with temporary files at various permission levels). No CGo usage to preserve zero-dependency binary. Bubbletea Model updates must be pure functions for testability: threshold evaluator takes Model and MetricUpdateMsg and returns new Model plus AlertMsg slice with no side effects. SSE listener goroutine communicates exclusively through typed Bubbletea messages; no shared mutable state.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WMS SSE endpoint enforces IP allowlisting that excludes shift-office workstation IPs. Mitigation: document IP registration requirement in deployment runbook; Ansible playbook validates SSE connectivity before marking installation complete. Risk: terminal width below 80 columns on some workstations causes Lip Gloss table rendering artifacts. Mitigation: tool detects terminal width on startup using term.GetSize() and renders a compact single-column layout below 80 columns. Tradeoff: stateless design with no disk persistence means supervisors cannot review historical threshold breach history from the tool; mitigated by webhook delivering alerts to a persistent escalation system. Tradeoff: single zone per tool instance simplifies the SSE subscription and state model; multi-zone support requires a future enhancement with multiple SSE goroutines and a zone-selector TUI component.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Bubbletea over termui or tview for TUI framework based on Elm-architecture goroutine safety and active maintenance; termui is no longer maintained. Cobra selected for flag parsing over standard library flag package to support repeatable --threshold flags cleanly. Webhook-based alert dispatch selected over direct PagerDuty or Slack SDK integration to keep the tool integration-agnostic; warehouse teams configure their preferred escalation endpoint.

technology_stack.frontend_stack: None - CLI output only with structured JSON and text reports
technology_stack.data_platform: InfluxDB for attack metric time-series; local SQLite for test run metadata
technology_stack.hosting_target: Bare-metal lab server in air-gapped environment
technology_stack.privacy_retention_policy: Vulnerability findings retained indefinitely on encrypted lab storage; replay captures purged after report sign-off
technology_stack.compliance_context: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements for electronic records and audit trails in pharmaceutical research environments; USP 1079 guidelines for temperature monitoring in pharmaceutical supply chains

backend.service_design: Three Python modules: attack_runner (asyncio orchestration of spoofing/replay tasks), packet_engine (Scapy-based MQTT packet crafting and TCP stream capture/replay), report_generator (structured vulnerability finding serialization with HMAC-SHA256 signing)
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API; command-line invocation via argparse with subcommands: spoof, replay, report; JSON output to stdout and structured log files
backend.failure_handling: Attack injection failures logged with packet-level error detail to structured JSON log; test_run status set to FAILED in SQLite with error summary; partial findings preserved for analysis

security.audit_and_logging_controls: All test_run events logged to append-only structured JSON files with timestamp and operator ID; vulnerability_findings signed with HMAC-SHA256 using a lab-local signing key stored in encrypted key file meeting FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature requirements

data.schema_notes: InfluxDB measurements use sensor_node_id and attack_scenario_id tags for filtering injection metric time series; SQLite stores relational test run state and signed findings
data.retention_and_deletion: vulnerability_findings retained indefinitely on encrypted lab storage; replay_captures purged 30 days after report sign-off; raw InfluxDB attack metrics retained 90 days

devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping custom Python metrics endpoint (attack injection rate, replay replay_count, detection latency histogram); Grafana dashboard visualizing attack scenario timelines

workflows.api_design: No HTTP API; command-line invocation via argparse with subcommands: spoof, replay, report; JSON output to stdout and structured log files

workflows.deployment_and_operations: Single bare-metal lab server running InfluxDB, Prometheus, and Grafana on isolated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN; Python tool installed via vendored pip wheel packages
workflows.observability: Prometheus scraping custom Python metrics endpoint (attack injection rate, replay replay_count, detection latency histogram); Grafana dashboard visualizing attack scenario timelines
workflows.cost_and_scaling: Minimal cost due to local deployment; scalability limited by lab server capacity
workflows.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Implement HMAC-SHA256 signing in report_generator; Phase 2: Extend vulnerability_findings schema with severity and affected_sensor_model fields
workflows.development_guidelines: Use vendored Python packages; configure GitLab CI offline runner with no external package pulls; manual test run execution with scripted test suites; automated CI limited to linting and unit tests
workflows.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risks include potential misuse of exploit tooling if lab physical controls fail; tradeoffs include the complexity of implementing HMAC-SHA256 signing and extending the vulnerability_findings schema. However, these are necessary for meeting FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements and enhancing the usefulness of findings to pharmaceutical QA reviewers.

reference.thinking_summary: Two persistent issues need resolution: ISS-002 requires HMAC-SHA256 signing of vulnerability report files to meet FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic record integrity requirements, implemented via a lab-local encrypted key file; ISS-003 requires adding severity ENUM and affected_sensor_model TEXT to the vulnerability_findings SQLite table so pharmaceutical QA reviewers can map findings to specific sensor procurement risk decisions. Both fixes are confined to report_generator and the data model - the core Scapy attack engine is unchanged. This is a weak-quality row since the plan sections are adequate but some remain thin given the prototype scope.

fix_report.expected_outcome: Vulnerability report files satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic record integrity requirements; pharmaceutical QA reviewers can verify report authenticity using the lab signing key without re-running the tool

fix_report.expected_outcome: Pharmaceutical QA reviewers can filter findings by severity and map each finding to specific sensor models under evaluation for procurement, enabling evidence-based sensor selection

reference.title: Cold Chain Sensor Adversarial Research Prototype v3
reference.executive_summary: A Python research prototype running in an air-gapped pharmaceutical lab that simulates MQTT spoofing and TCP replay attacks against IoT cold chain temperature sensors using Scapy. Test runs are logged to InfluxDB (attack metric time series) and SQLite (test run metadata). Vulnerability findings are now stored with severity classification and affected sensor model, and each report carries a HMAC-SHA256 integrity signature meeting FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic record requirements. Resolves two persistent audit findings on report integrity and findings schema completeness.
reference.architecture_overview: Single-host Python application running on a bare-metal lab server isolated on a dedicated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN with no external network egress. Three core modules: packet_engine (Scapy-based MQTT packet crafting and TCP stream capture for spoofing and replay scenarios), attack_runner (asyncio task orchestration executing configured attack scenarios against target sensor nodes), and report_generator (serializes vulnerability_findings to signed JSON report with HMAC-SHA256 block). InfluxDB stores high-frequency attack injection metrics; SQLite stores test_runs, sensor_nodes, attack_scenarios, and vulnerability_findings. Prometheus scrapes a local metrics endpoint; Grafana visualizes attack timelines on the lab network.
reference.technology_stack: Python 3.12 with Scapy 2.5 for packet crafting and TCP capture; asyncio for concurrent attack task orchestration; InfluxDB 2.7 (local) for attack metric time-series; SQLite 3 for relational test run state; argparse CLI with subcommands; HMAC-SHA256 via Python hmac stdlib for report signing; Prometheus client library for metrics endpoint; Grafana 10 and Prometheus on lab network. GitLab CI offline runner for ruff lint, mypy, and pytest. All Python dependencies vendored as wheel files.
reference.functional_feature_map: Spoofing Attack Module: craft MQTT PUBLISH packets with fabricated temperature values targeting specific sensor topic paths; configurable injection rate (readings/sec) and target value range; detection latency measured from injection timestamp to monitoring system anomaly alert timestamp logged in InfluxDB. Replay Attack Module: capture authentic sensor MQTT traffic to pcap file at configurable output_dir; replay captured packets at configurable delay offsets to test nonce validation and timestamp freshness checks; replay result (rejected/accepted) logged per packet. Vulnerability Report Generator: aggregate test_run findings with severity ENUM and affected_sensor_model; compute HMAC-SHA256 signature over serialized finding fields using lab signing key; output signed JSON report to configured path. Lab Isolation Guard: on startup verify active network interface is bound to 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN; abort with error if non-lab interface detected.
reference.system_components: Bare-metal lab server (isolated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN, no egress routes). Python tool process with three modules: packet_engine, attack_runner, report_generator. InfluxDB 2.7 local instance for attack metric time-series (sensor_readings measurement with sensor_node_id and scenario_id tags). SQLite database file for test_runs, vulnerability_findings, and attack_scenarios. Prometheus client HTTP endpoint (localhost:9090) scraped by local Prometheus instance. Grafana dashboard visualizing attack scenario injection rates and detection latency histograms. Encrypted key file storing HMAC signing key, unlocked with operator passphrase at tool startup.
reference.workflows: Spoofing Attack Execution: operator invokes 'python tool.py spoof --sensor-id SENSOR-07 --rate 10 --value-range 45.0-60.0 --duration 300'; attack_runner creates test_run record in SQLite, asyncio tasks call packet_engine to craft MQTT PUBLISH packets at configured rate, each injection timestamped and written to InfluxDB; monitoring system anomaly alert timestamp captured when available; detection latency computed and stored in attack_scenarios record. Replay Attack Execution: operator invokes 'python tool.py replay --capture --sensor-id SENSOR-07 --output-dir /lab/captures/run_042' to capture pcap; then 'python tool.py replay --replay --pcap /lab/captures/run_042/sensor07.pcap --delay 120' to replay with 2-minute offset; replay result (nonce_rejected/timestamp_stale/accepted) logged per packet to SQLite attack_scenarios. Report Generation: operator invokes 'python tool.py report --test-run-id 042 --severity-filter high,critical'; report_generator queries SQLite for findings, populates severity and affected_sensor_model fields, serializes to JSON, computes HMAC-SHA256 over canonical field string using lab key file, appends signature block, writes to /lab/reports/run_042_signed.json.
reference.data_model: test_runs (id INTEGER PK, attack_type TEXT ENUM spoof/replay, target_sensor_model TEXT, operator_id TEXT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME, status TEXT ENUM running/completed/failed, notes TEXT). sensor_nodes (id INTEGER PK, model TEXT, mac_address TEXT, firmware_version TEXT, lab_position TEXT). attack_scenarios (id INTEGER PK, test_run_id FK, scenario_type TEXT, parameters_json TEXT, executed_at DATETIME, outcome TEXT). replay_captures (id INTEGER PK, test_run_id FK, pcap_file_path TEXT, capture_duration_sec INT, captured_at DATETIME). vulnerability_findings (id INTEGER PK, test_run_id FK, severity TEXT ENUM critical/high/medium/low NOT NULL, title TEXT, affected_sensor_model TEXT NOT NULL, finding_detail TEXT, hmac_signature TEXT NOT NULL, generated_at DATETIME). InfluxDB measurement: sensor_readings (sensor_node_id tag, attack_scenario_id tag, value_celsius field, injected BOOLEAN field, timestamp).
reference.api_design: No HTTP API. CLI interface via argparse: 'tool.py spoof --sensor-id ID --rate N --value-range MIN-MAX --duration SECS'. 'tool.py replay --capture|--replay --sensor-id ID --output-dir PATH|--pcap PATH --delay SECS'. 'tool.py report --test-run-id ID [--severity-filter LEVELS] [--output-path PATH]'. 'tool.py list-runs' outputs JSON array of test_run records. 'tool.py verify-report --report-path PATH' verifies HMAC signature of a signed report file. All commands log structured JSON to /lab/logs/tool_{date}.jsonl.
reference.security_and_compliance: Air-gapped network isolation: tool checks at startup that the active network interface IP is within 192.168.100.0/24; aborts with exit code 2 if non-lab interface detected, preventing accidental execution on connected machines. Physical access controls govern lab entry; no application authentication layer. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance for electronic records: vulnerability_findings records carry HMAC-SHA256 integrity signatures computed over canonical concatenation of (id, test_run_id, severity, title, affected_sensor_model, finding_detail, generated_at) using PBKDF2-derived key from operator passphrase and lab-specific salt stored in encrypted key file; signature stored in hmac_signature column and included in report JSON signature block. Signed reports can be independently verified with 'tool.py verify-report' without re-running attack scenarios. USP 1079 alignment: all temperature injection values and detection timestamps logged to InfluxDB with sub-second precision satisfying cold chain monitoring audit trail requirements. Encrypted key file stored with 600 permissions on lab server; passphrase required at tool startup.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Single bare-metal lab server running Ubuntu 22.04 LTS on isolated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN with all external routes removed from routing table. Python tool installed from vendored wheel packages in /lab/vendor/ with no pip network calls. InfluxDB 2.7 installed as systemd service; data directory on encrypted LUKS volume. SQLite database at /lab/data/research.db on same encrypted volume. GitLab CI offline runner executes on lab server: ruff format check, mypy --strict, pytest with mock Scapy and InfluxDB clients (no live packet injection in CI). No Docker; bare Python venv installation. Manual test run execution with human oversight during attack scenarios. Backup: nightly encrypted tar of SQLite database and signed reports to lab NAS.
reference.observability: Prometheus client library in tool exposes metrics endpoint on localhost:9100: injection_packets_total (counter by scenario_type and sensor_model), replay_result_total (counter by outcome enum), detection_latency_seconds (histogram by attack_type), report_generation_total (counter by severity_filter). Grafana dashboard on lab network visualizes injection rate timelines and detection latency distributions per attack scenario. Structured JSON logs to /lab/logs/tool_{date}.jsonl with test_run_id and scenario_id on every event for post-hoc analysis.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; bare-metal lab server amortized over research engagement. InfluxDB retention policy set to 90 days for attack metrics to manage local disk; SQLite database size bounded by finding count. Python tool scales to concurrent attack scenarios via asyncio tasks up to NIC packet injection rate limit; single-host scope is appropriate for targeted sensor model evaluation.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (completed): Scapy spoofing injector and replay capture/replay modules, InfluxDB metric logging, SQLite test run tracking, configurable output_dir for captures (ISS-001 resolved). Phase 2 (this round): HMAC-SHA256 report signing with encrypted lab key file (ISS-002), extended vulnerability_findings schema with severity and affected_sensor_model (ISS-003), 'verify-report' CLI subcommand, Prometheus metrics endpoint, Grafana dashboard. Phase 3 (future): BLE sensor attack module using Scapy BLE extensions, automated finding submission to pharmaceutical QA system via secure lab-internal API.
reference.development_guidelines: ruff lint and mypy --strict enforced in GitLab CI offline runner. pytest unit tests mock Scapy packet engine and InfluxDB client to avoid live packet injection in CI. HMAC signing logic covered by dedicated unit tests with known test vectors. No external package installation during CI; all dependencies vendored. operator_id logged on all test_run and finding records for accountability. Attack scenario parameters validated before execution to prevent accidental out-of-range injection that could damage sensor hardware.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HMAC key file loss or corruption makes historical signed reports unverifiable. Mitigation: key file backed up to encrypted lab NAS with offline copy held by lab security officer. Risk: Scapy MQTT injection technique may not generalize to all sensor firmware versions; some may use proprietary transport layer. Mitigation: sensor_nodes table records firmware_version enabling finding scoping to specific versions. Tradeoff: SQLite over PostgreSQL keeps infrastructure minimal for air-gapped deployment but limits concurrent write performance if multiple researchers run scenarios simultaneously; acceptable for low-concurrency lab use.
reference.open_questions_resolved: HMAC-SHA256 chosen over GPG signing for simplicity of lab-local key management without requiring GPG keyring infrastructure. SQLite retained over InfluxDB for findings metadata because relational queries on severity and sensor_model are simpler in SQL. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirement for audit trail integrity satisfied by HMAC signing combined with append-only structured log files.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to replace a legacy rules-based music recommendation engine with an LLM-powered assistant that generates personalized playlists with mood-based curation for a streaming platform. The plan addresses the core user complaint about opaque recommendations by providing an LLM rationale feature that is under 2 seconds for user experience. The plan ensures that LLM failures degrade to high-quality rule-based results silently. It also includes PII stripping from LLM prompts, prompt injection detection, and a fallback to catalog-based ranking. The architecture is designed to be scalable, secure, and compliant with GDPR, with a focus on maintaining a balance between cost and performance.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented PII stripping middleware
fix_report.expected_outcome: PII no longer appears in LLM prompts sent to OpenAI, mitigating the risk of PII leakage.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented prompt injection detection
fix_report.expected_outcome: Mood inputs are sanitized to prevent jailbreak patterns, enhancing security.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented fallback ranking mechanism
fix_report.expected_outcome: In case of LLM unavailability, the system seamlessly degrades to a high-quality catalog-based ranked playlist without disrupting the user experience.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Redis caching for identical mood+genre prompts
fix_report.expected_outcome: OpenAI spend is contained within the $8000/month cap, improving cost efficiency.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Circuit Breaker for OpenAI client
fix_report.expected_outcome: Fallback to catalog-based ranking is triggered after 3 consecutive OpenAI timeouts, ensuring system resilience.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Redis TTL for prompt caching
fix_report.expected_outcome: LLM refresh is triggered, reducing the frequency of OpenAI calls and staying within the spend cap.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented SSE for streaming response
fix_report.expected_outcome: Progressive playlist rendering provides a seamless user experience without full response buffering.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Redis TTL for recommendation cache
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cache hit rate is maintained above 60%, optimizing OpenAI spend.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Redis TTL for curation_sessions
fix_report.expected_outcome: Latency tracking for curation_sessions is improved for GDPR erasure verification.

parsed.title: LLM-Powered Mood-Based Playlist Curation Architecture for Streaming Platform
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan replaces a legacy rules-based music recommendation engine with an LLM-powered assistant that generates personalized playlists with mood-based curation. It addresses user experience, security, compliance, and cost concerns, ensuring a balance between innovation and operational efficiency. The plan includes a scalable, secure, and GDPR-compliant system with a focus on maintaining a low latency and controlled OpenAI spend.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture is a full-stack AI system with a Next.js 15 frontend, Python 3.12 FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL 16 for data storage, Redis 7 for caching, and AWS EKS for hosting. It features a Cognito-based authentication, PII-stripping middleware, and a fallback ranking mechanism. The system is designed for high-scale, real-time inference with a focus on maintaining a balance between cost and performance.

technology_stack.security: Cognito, TLS 1.3, AES-256, PII minimization, prompt injection detection

workflows.mood_input_to_playlist: Mood input is sanitized, sent to the recommendation service, and a playlist with LLM narrative is returned. If LLM is unavailable, the fallback ranker provides a catalog-based ranked playlist.
workflows.listener_profile_management: Listener profiles are stored and updated with preferences and history. Prompt templates are versioned in the prompt registry for A/B testing.
workflows.engagement_signal_ingestion: Engagement signals are ingested via Kinesis events and stored for analytics.

security_and_compliance.data_protection: PII stripping from LLM prompts, GDPR compliance for data processing
security_and_compliance.audit_trail: Prompt injection detection, abuse prevention, and GDPR erasure capabilities

deployment_and_operations.deployment_topology: EKS cluster with auto-scaling node groups
deployment_and_operations.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions, Helm chart upgrade, canary rollout
deployment_and_operations.observability_stack: Datadog APM, custom metrics, and Kubernetes metrics
deployment_and_operations.cost_controls: Redis cache TTL, OpenAI token limit, EKS auto-scaling

observability.monitoring: Datadog APM, Kinesis Firehose, and S3 analytics

cost_and_scaling.cost_controls: Redis cache TTL, OpenAI token limit, EKS auto-scaling
cost_and_scaling.scaling_strategy: EKS auto-scaling for inference workloads, spot instances for retraining jobs

parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP in 12 weeks, with a focus on core AI capability and A/B testing for model variants.
parsed.development_guidelines: Use TypeScript for React components, FastAPI for backend, and PostgreSQL for data storage. Implement security controls and observability from the start.

tradeoffs: Balancing OpenAI spend with Redis caching
Choosing between EKS and Fargate for hosting

open_questions_resolved: Fine-tuning open-source model in phase 2 to reduce OpenAI dependency
Justifying Kubernetes or simpler Fargate setup for retraining jobs

reference.thinking_summary: Designing an LLM-powered music recommendation system replacing a legacy rules engine. Key decisions: FastAPI async for OpenAI I/O-bound inference, Redis prompt hash caching to stay within $8000/month OpenAI spend cap, PII-stripping middleware as the primary GDPR control before LLM dispatch, SSE streaming from FastAPI through Next.js to browser for sub-2s perceived latency, PostgreSQL prompt_registry for versioned template A/B testing without deploys.
reference.title: LLM-Powered Mood-Based Music Recommendation System
reference.executive_summary: A high-scale AI system replacing a legacy rules-based music recommendation engine with an LLM-powered assistant on AWS EKS. Listeners describe their mood in natural language; the FastAPI inference service dispatches sanitized prompts to GPT-4o, which selects tracks from the catalog and generates a narrative playlist rationale. Redis caches identical mood+genre prompt hashes for 30 minutes to stay within the $8000/month OpenAI budget. GDPR compliance enforced through PII-stripping middleware, 30-day prompt log retention, and automated erasure on account deletion.
reference.architecture_overview: Next.js 15 streaming frontend communicates with a FastAPI inference service on EKS via SSE for progressive playlist rendering. The inference service dispatches sanitized mood prompts to GPT-4o via OpenAI SDK (stream=True), selecting tracks from the PostgreSQL catalog using embedding similarity and returning LLM-generated narrative rationale. Redis 7 caches prompt responses keyed on sha256(mood_hash+genre_context) with 30-minute TTL. A circuit breaker activates a catalog-based fallback_ranker on 3 consecutive OpenAI timeouts, producing a seamless fallback invisible to listeners. Engagement events (play, skip, save) stream to Kinesis Firehose, landing in S3 for Athena-based A/B evaluation of prompt versions stored in the prompt_registry PostgreSQL table. Cognito PKCE handles listener authentication; PII stripping middleware intercepts mood inputs before LLM dispatch to enforce GDPR controls on external API calls.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 15 TypeScript, React Query, Zustand, SSE for streaming. Backend: Python 3.12 FastAPI async, openai-python SDK, Pydantic v2, pgvector for embedding similarity. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension for track embeddings. Cache: Redis 7 for prompt hash cache and token-bucket rate limiting. Auth: Cognito user pools with PKCE. ML: GPT-4o via OpenAI API; fallback rule-based ranker. Infrastructure: EKS us-east-1, Karpenter for node autoscaling, ECR, Kinesis Firehose, S3, Athena. Observability: Datadog APM.
reference.functional_feature_map: Mood Playlist Generation: listener submits natural language mood input; system strips PII, checks Redis cache, dispatches to GPT-4o with versioned prompt template, returns track list with narrative rationale via SSE stream. Listener Profile Management: stores genre_weights, mood_history, and explicit preference overrides; updated by engagement signals. Engagement Signal Pipeline: play, skip, save events written to Kinesis Firehose, partitioned to S3, evaluated via Athena for prompt version A/B testing. Prompt Registry: versioned templates stored in PostgreSQL, activated via feature flag without app redeploy. GDPR Controls: data export endpoint, erasure endpoint deleting all listener data within 30 days. Fallback Ranker: catalog-based energy/genre ranking activates silently on LLM unavailability.
reference.system_components: EKS inference-worker node group (c6g.xlarge 4-20 nodes, HPA on p95 latency), FastAPI inference pods, Next.js on Vercel (API routes proxy to EKS), PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector (listener profiles, playlists, prompt_registry, tracks with embeddings), Redis 7 (prompt hash cache TTL 30min, rate limit counters), Cognito user pool (PKCE for streaming app), Kinesis Firehose (engagement events to S3), S3 + Athena (A/B evaluation queries), OpenAI API (GPT-4o), Karpenter (node autoscaling with overnight scale-to-zero), Datadog agents.
reference.workflows: Mood Playlist Generation Workflow: Listener types mood input ('mellow Sunday morning with rain') into Next.js MoodInput component. Frontend POSTs to FastAPI POST /v1/recommend/playlist with Cognito JWT. FastAPI middleware validates JWT, extracts listener_id, runs PII-stripping NER pass on mood input. Computes sha256(stripped_mood+genre_context), checks Redis cache. On cache miss, fetches active prompt template from prompt_registry, injects mood and top-5 listener genre_weights, dispatches to GPT-4o stream=True with 1500-token budget. Tokens streamed back via SSE to Next.js; frontend renders tracks progressively as they arrive. curation_sessions record written to PostgreSQL with latency_ms. Engagement Signal Workflow: Listener skips track in playlist. Frontend fires POST /v1/engage/event with track_id and action='skip'. FastAPI writes engagement event to Kinesis Firehose. Nightly Glue job aggregates skip rates by prompt_version_id into Athena table. A/B test dashboard in Datadog queries Athena for prompt version relevance scores, enabling ML team to promote winning template in prompt_registry.
reference.data_model: listener_profiles (id UUID PK, cognito_sub TEXT UNIQUE, genre_weights JSONB, mood_history TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). playlists (id UUID PK, listener_id UUID FK, mood_input TEXT, llm_narrative TEXT, track_ids UUID[], prompt_version_id UUID FK, cache_hit BOOL, latency_ms INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). tracks (id UUID PK, title TEXT, artist TEXT, genre TEXT, bpm SMALLINT, energy_score FLOAT, embedding VECTOR(768), catalog_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). mood_contexts (id UUID PK, label TEXT, genre_affinities JSONB, seed_track_ids UUID[]). recommendation_events (id UUID PK, listener_id UUID FK, playlist_id UUID FK, track_id UUID FK, action ENUM play/skip/save/share, event_at TIMESTAMPTZ). prompt_registry (id UUID PK, version TEXT UNIQUE, template TEXT, model TEXT, token_budget INT, active BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). curation_sessions (id UUID PK, listener_id UUID FK, mood_input_hash TEXT, llm_response_cached BOOL, latency_ms INT, openai_tokens_used INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
reference.api_design: POST /v1/recommend/playlist — body: {mood_input, listener_id}, SSE stream response with track_ids and narrative tokens. GET /v1/listener/profile/:id — genre weights and mood history summary. PATCH /v1/listener/profile/:id/preferences — update genre weight overrides. POST /v1/engage/event — body: {listener_id, playlist_id, track_id, action}. GET /v1/prompts/:version_id — prompt template detail for monitoring. GET /v1/gdpr/export/:listener_id — GDPR Article 20 data export. DELETE /v1/gdpr/erasure/:listener_id — GDPR Article 17 erasure request. All endpoints require Cognito JWT; /v1/prompts and /v1/gdpr/erasure require admin scope.
reference.security_and_compliance: Cognito PKCE enforced for all listener-facing endpoints; JWKS cached in FastAPI middleware with 5-minute TTL. PII-stripping middleware: spaCy NER pass removes PERSON, EMAIL, and PHONE entities from mood inputs before LLM dispatch. Prompt injection detection: keyword blocklist (system prompt overrides, role injection strings) plus rate limit 10 requests/listener/minute via Redis token bucket. LLM prompt logs (curation_sessions.mood_input_hash only — no raw text) retained 30 days then purged by scheduled job. GDPR erasure: DELETE cascade on listener_profiles removes playlists, recommendation_events, curation_sessions within 30 days; Kinesis-derived S3 events anonymized via Glue job. Data processing agreement with OpenAI in place; no listener PII in prompt payloads enforced by architecture.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: pytest coverage >85%, ruff, mypy, docker build, ECR push, Helm upgrade to EKS canary (10% traffic first 30 minutes, full rollout on <1% error rate). EKS node groups: inference-workers c6g.xlarge HPA on p95 latency target 1800ms (4-20 nodes), retraining-jobs m6i.2xlarge spot. Karpenter scales inference nodes to minimum 2 overnight. Database migrations via Alembic run as EKS Job pre-deployment. Next.js deployed to Vercel with API routes proxying to EKS ALB. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager injected as EKS pod environment variables via External Secrets Operator.
reference.observability: Datadog APM auto-instrumentation on FastAPI pods with listener_id and prompt_version_id trace tags. Custom metrics: llm_call_latency_p50_ms (alert >2000ms sustained 5min), openai_tokens_per_request (alert >1800), recommendation_cache_hit_rate (alert <60%), fallback_ranker_activation_count (alert >10/min signals LLM degradation), gdpr_erasure_completion_hours (SLA 720h). Kinesis Firehose delivery latency monitored via CloudWatch. A/B prompt version relevance scores queried from Athena nightly into Datadog dashboard.
reference.cost_and_scaling: OpenAI cost controlled via Redis cache hit rate target >60% (estimated $5000-7000/month at scale), 1500-token hard budget per request, and stream=True avoiding buffering charges. EKS inference nodes scale 4-20 on HPA; overnight Karpenter scale-down saves ~40% compute cost. Spot instances for retraining jobs save 70% over on-demand. Estimated total infrastructure: $4000-6000/month including EKS, RDS, Redis, and Kinesis at 500K daily playlist requests.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Mood playlist generation with GPT-4o, PII-stripping middleware, Cognito PKCE auth, listener_profiles and playlists in PostgreSQL, Redis prompt cache, fallback_ranker, SSE streaming frontend, basic Datadog LLM latency and cost metrics. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Prompt registry A/B testing framework, Kinesis engagement pipeline with Athena evaluation, GDPR export and erasure endpoints, genre weight preference overrides, pgvector embedding similarity for track selection improvement. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Open-source model fine-tuning evaluation to reduce OpenAI dependency, collaborative playlist sharing with LLM curation, label promotional campaign integration with mood context seeding.
reference.development_guidelines: Python ruff and mypy in CI with strict type annotations on all FastAPI handlers. Pydantic v2 models for all request/response schemas with explicit PII fields marked for stripping. pytest-asyncio for async handler tests; coverage gate at 85%. Prompt templates reviewed in code review before merge to prompt_registry. No listener PII in application logs — use listener_id only. OpenAI SDK configured with timeout=10s and max_retries=2; never allow default retry behavior to bypass circuit breaker.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OpenAI API cost overrun if cache hit rate falls below 60% on diverse mood inputs. Mitigation: semantic clustering of mood embeddings to expand cache hit surface beyond exact string match. Risk: GPT-4o hallucinating track titles not in catalog. Mitigation: post-processing validates all returned track_ids against PostgreSQL before returning playlist; invalid IDs replaced with catalog-ranked alternatives. Risk: PII leakage via indirect references in mood inputs ('play something like my friend Sarah likes'). Mitigation: NER pass plus fuzzy name matching against listener contact list as additional stripping layer. Tradeoff: Redis cache returns identical playlist for same mood hash — add listener_id salt to cache key to maintain personalization at cost of lower cache hit rate.
reference.open_questions_resolved: GPT-4o selected over GPT-3.5 for narrative quality; cost controlled via caching rather than model downgrade. stream=True chosen for SSE progressive rendering over batch response to meet 2s perceived latency target. Redis prompt cache key uses sha256(stripped_mood+genre_context) without listener_id salt in phase 1; personalized salt evaluated in phase 2 after measuring cache hit rate impact. Cognito PKCE confirmed over API key auth for browser security compliance.

parsed.title: Cholera Risk Forecasting Pipeline for Displacement Camp WASH Coordinators
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan outlines a secure, scalable, and compliant data pipeline for forecasting cholera outbreak risk in displacement camps. It addresses current focus issues, preserves user-confirmed requirements, and improves the architecture without introducing regressions.
