reference.thinking_summary: Daily cholera risk forecasting pipeline integrating four data streams: water quality tests, sanitation ratios, rainfall anomalies, and historical outbreak records. Key architectural decisions: Prefect on Cloud Run Jobs for scheduled batch execution with per-stage service accounts enforcing least privilege on health data; PostgreSQL for risk score persistence and coordinator querying; risk tier system (low/elevated/high/critical) rather than binary threshold; intervention recommendation reports citing indicator drivers for actionable WASH responses. Privacy by design: no personal identifiers ingest by schema constraint.
reference.title: Cholera Outbreak Risk Forecasting WASH Data Pipeline
reference.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Prefect-orchestrated data pipeline on GCP Cloud Run Jobs that ingests daily water quality test results, sanitation coverage ratios, and rainfall anomaly data for displacement camps, engineers WASH features, and runs ML inference to produce per-camp cholera outbreak risk scores and intervention recommendations. WASH sector coordinators consume risk scores via PostgreSQL and receive configurable threshold alerts to prioritize preventive interventions. No personal identifiers are ingested; health data is handled at camp-aggregate level under WHO and GDPR compliance controls.
reference.architecture_overview: Prefect flow cholera_risk_daily runs as a daily Cloud Run Job triggered by Cloud Scheduler at 04:00 UTC. The flow has four sequential task groups: parallel ingestion of water quality, sanitation, and weather data from field monitoring systems and a weather API; feature engineering combining the ingested streams into WASH risk features per camp; batch ML inference loading a scikit-learn gradient boosting model from GCS; and threshold alert dispatch publishing Pub/Sub messages for camps in elevated/high/critical tiers. Results persist to PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL. Three stage-specific GCP service accounts enforce least privilege on GCS and Cloud SQL access. All data encrypted at rest via GCP KMS; weather API credentials stored in Secret Manager.
reference.technology_stack: Orchestration: Prefect 2.x with Cloud Run Job workers. Pipeline language: Python 3.12 with pandas for feature engineering, scikit-learn for model loading and batch inference, psycopg2 for PostgreSQL writes. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL db-g1-small with automated backups; GCS for raw archive and model artifacts. Infrastructure: Cloud Run Jobs, Cloud Scheduler, Pub/Sub for alert dispatch, Artifact Registry, Secret Manager, GCP KMS. CI/CD: GitLab CI, Docker. Observability: Prefect Cloud, Prometheus push gateway, Grafana.
reference.functional_feature_map: Water Quality Ingestion: fetches daily test records (turbidity, e-coli count, chlorine residual, sample source) from field monitoring API per camp, archives raw JSON to GCS, inserts parsed records to water_quality_tests table. Sanitation Ratio Ingestion: reads survey exports from partner file share, computes latrines_per_1000 and open_defecation_rate_pct, inserts to sanitation_ratios table. Rainfall Anomaly Integration: queries weather API for camp coordinates, computes rolling 30-day z-score against 5-year historical baseline, inserts to rainfall_observations with anomaly_z_score. Feature Engineering: joins current-day WASH inputs with 7-day rolling averages and nearest historical outbreak context per camp, produces feature matrix. ML Inference: loads serialized gradient boosting model from GCS, runs batch inference on feature matrix, assigns risk_tier (low/elevated/high/critical) and risk_score per camp, identifies top 3 contributing features via model explain output. Alert Dispatch: queries risk_scores for camps at elevated or above tier, generates intervention_recommendations citing driving indicators, publishes Pub/Sub messages consumed by WASH coordinator notification integration.
reference.system_components: Cloud Scheduler (daily cron at 04:00 UTC triggering ingest Cloud Run Job). Prefect Cloud (flow run orchestration, task retry management, failure alerting to Slack). Cloud Run Job: ingest (Python tasks for water quality, sanitation, weather parallel fetch; sa-ingest service account). Cloud Run Job: engineer-infer (feature engineering + batch inference; sa-inference service account). Cloud Run Job: alert (risk score query, recommendation generation, Pub/Sub publish; sa-alerting service account). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (displacement_camps, water_quality_tests, sanitation_ratios, rainfall_observations, historical_outbreaks, risk_scores, intervention_recommendations tables). GCS wash-pipeline-raw (daily ingestion archives). GCS wash-ml-artifacts (gradient boosting model .pkl, feature scaler .pkl, model version manifest). Secret Manager (weather API key). Pub/Sub topic wash-risk-alerts (consumed by WASH coordinator Slack integration). Prometheus push gateway (pipeline run metrics). Grafana (risk tier distribution dashboards).
reference.workflows: Daily Pipeline Run: Cloud Scheduler fires at 04:00 UTC, triggers ingest Cloud Run Job. Prefect flow starts; three parallel tasks execute: fetch_water_quality_tests (calls field monitoring API, validates records, archives to GCS, inserts to water_quality_tests), fetch_sanitation_ratios (reads partner SFTP export, computes ratios, inserts to sanitation_ratios), fetch_rainfall_data (calls weather API with camp coordinates, computes 30-day z-score, inserts to rainfall_observations). Ingest tasks complete; engineer-infer Cloud Run Job starts. feature_engineer task joins all three sources for each camp using the most recent available record per indicator within a 48-hour recency window, computes 7-day rolling averages, joins nearest historical outbreak features, produces camp_features DataFrame. run_inference task loads gradient_boosting_model.pkl and feature_scaler.pkl from GCS, applies scaler transform, runs model.predict_proba on camp_features, assigns risk_tier by probability threshold (critical >0.85, high 0.65-0.85, elevated 0.40-0.65, low <0.40), extracts top-3 feature importances per camp, writes risk_scores rows. Alert Dispatch: alert Cloud Run Job queries risk_scores where score_date = today and risk_tier in (elevated, high, critical), generates intervention_recommendation text referencing top indicator drivers (e.g., 'e_coli_per_100ml exceeded 10 CFU/100ml threshold for 3 consecutive days at Camp X'), inserts intervention_recommendations rows, publishes Pub/Sub message per camp to wash-risk-alerts topic. Stale Data Handling: if fetch_weather_data fails after 3 retries, engineer_features task uses previous day's rainfall_observations for affected camps and sets stale_weather=true flag in the risk_scores row.
reference.data_model: displacement_camps (id UUID PK, camp_name VARCHAR, admin_region VARCHAR, country VARCHAR, latitude FLOAT, longitude FLOAT, population_estimate INT, last_updated TIMESTAMPTZ). water_quality_tests (id UUID PK, camp_id UUID FK, test_date DATE, turbidity_ntu FLOAT, e_coli_per_100ml FLOAT, chlorine_residual_mg_l FLOAT, sample_source ENUM borehole/surface/trucked, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). sanitation_ratios (id UUID PK, camp_id UUID FK, survey_date DATE, latrines_count INT, population_served INT, latrines_per_1000 FLOAT, open_defecation_rate_pct FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rainfall_observations (id UUID PK, camp_id UUID FK, observation_date DATE, rainfall_mm FLOAT, anomaly_z_score FLOAT, data_source VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). historical_outbreaks (id UUID PK, camp_id UUID FK, onset_date DATE, peak_date DATE, total_cases INT, case_fatality_rate FLOAT, primary_water_source VARCHAR, confirmed BOOL). risk_scores (id UUID PK, camp_id UUID FK, score_date DATE, risk_tier ENUM low/elevated/high/critical, risk_score FLOAT, top_risk_factors JSONB, stale_weather BOOL DEFAULT false, model_version VARCHAR, pipeline_run_id UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). intervention_recommendations (id UUID PK, risk_score_id UUID FK, recommendation_text TEXT, indicator_drivers JSONB, dispatched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Retention: water_quality_tests and sanitation_ratios retained 36 months via scheduled DELETE job. risk_scores retained 60 months. No personal identifiers in any table by schema design. Health surveillance entities: patient_case_reports (id UUID PK, camp_id FK, report_date DATE, suspected_cholera_cases INT, confirmed_cholera_cases INT, clinical_severity ENUM mild/moderate/severe, treatment_center_id UUID, case_fatality_rate NUMERIC, reported_by_health_worker_id UUID), clinical_treatments (id UUID PK, case_report_id FK, treatment_type ENUM oral_rehydration/iv_fluids/antibiotics, patients_treated INT, treatment_center_id UUID, health_worker_id UUID, administered_date DATE).
reference.api_design: No external API. Pipeline produces outputs to PostgreSQL tables and Pub/Sub. WASH coordinators query risk_scores and intervention_recommendations via PostgreSQL connections from their BI tools. Internal pipeline invocation: Cloud Scheduler HTTP target triggers Cloud Run Job via Cloud Run Admin API. Prefect REST API (Prefect Cloud) provides flow run management; not exposed to end users.
reference.security_and_compliance: GCP service account least privilege: sa-ingest has storage.objectCreator on wash-pipeline-raw, cloudsql.client on raw ingestion tables, secretmanager.secretAccessor on wash-weather-api-key only. sa-inference has storage.objectViewer on wash-ml-artifacts, cloudsql.client on feature tables and risk_scores. sa-alerting has cloudsql.client read on risk_scores, pubsub.publisher on wash-risk-alerts. No service account has project-level IAM roles. GCP KMS default encryption for Cloud SQL and GCS. TLS 1.3 for weather API calls. No personal identifiers in ingestion schema; water quality tests stored at camp aggregate level. Pipeline run logs exclude camp resident counts. GDPR: no personal data in pipeline by design; WHO health data standards applied to WASH indicator definitions. Compliance_context: organizational data governance policy for camp health data requires data access log maintained in Cloud Audit Logs; enabled at project level for Cloud SQL and GCS.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline on merge to main: pytest unit tests with 80% coverage gate covering feature engineering and inference modules with mock GCS and PostgreSQL, docker build multi-stage image (build stage installs deps, runtime stage copies wheel), push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run Job update-traffic deploy for each of the three stage jobs. Model artifact promotion: separate manual GitLab CI job requiring WASH data lead approval via GitLab protected environment; copies model .pkl from staging GCS path to production wash-ml-artifacts bucket. Environments: dev (Cloud Run Jobs using dev Cloud SQL instance, dry-run mode skips Pub/Sub publish), production (Cloud Scheduler at 04:00 UTC, full pipeline). Pipeline run monitoring: Prefect Cloud flow run page shows task-level status and duration; Slack webhook notified on flow run failure. Rollback: Cloud Run Job revision pinning allows revert to previous container image via gcloud run jobs update; previous model artifact retained in GCS for rollback via manual promotion job.
reference.observability: Prefect Cloud flow run history: task durations, retry counts, failure tracebacks per daily run. Prometheus push gateway receives metrics at end of each stage: pipeline_ingest_records_total by source and camp_id_count, feature_engineer_camps_with_complete_features, inference_duration_seconds, risk_tier_distribution{tier=low/elevated/high/critical} camp counts, stale_weather_camps_count, alerts_dispatched_total. Grafana dashboards: daily risk tier heatmap by country and camp, 30-day trend of elevated/high/critical camp counts, data freshness per source (days since last record per camp), pipeline run duration trend. Alert: Grafana fires if stale_weather_camps_count exceeds 50 in a daily run or if risk_tier critical count increases by more than 20% day-over-day (potential outbreak cluster signal).
reference.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run Jobs (3 stage jobs, ~1 hour total runtime daily): approximately $15/month. Cloud SQL db-g1-small: $25/month. GCS storage (raw archives + model artifacts): $5/month. Cloud Scheduler, Pub/Sub, Secret Manager, Artifact Registry: $5/month. Prefect Cloud free tier (5 workspaces). Prometheus push gateway on existing Grafana instance: no additional cost. Total approximately $50/month, well under $400 constraint. Scaling: if camp count grows from 500 to 2000, Cloud Run Job memory limit (2GB) accommodates larger pandas DataFrames; Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-1 upgrade at $50/month handles increased query volume.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP Pipeline (4 weeks): PostgreSQL schema and migrations, water quality and sanitation ingestion tasks, rainfall anomaly integration, feature engineering module with 7-day rolling averages, ML inference with pre-trained gradient boosting model loaded from GCS, risk_tier assignment, Pub/Sub alert dispatch, GitLab CI pipeline, Cloud Run Job deployment. Phase 2 - WASH Coordinator Validation (2 weeks): parallel run generating risk scores in dev environment, WASH coordinator review sessions comparing model outputs to expert assessments, threshold calibration adjustments, intervention recommendation text templates reviewed by WASH specialists. Phase 3 - Production Cutover and Observability (2 weeks): production Cloud Scheduler activation, Grafana dashboard rollout to WASH team, Prefect Slack alerting configuration, stale data handling validation, documentation of pipeline data lineage for organizational data governance record.
reference.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type annotations on all pipeline functions. Feature engineering module unit-tested with synthetic camp DataFrames covering edge cases: missing water quality records, zero sanitation survey coverage, no historical outbreak data for new camps. Inference module tested with a minimal model fixture loaded from a test GCS bucket path. No raw SQL strings in pipeline code; all PostgreSQL writes use psycopg2 parameterized queries. GCS paths defined as constants in pipeline/config.py keyed by environment (dev/production). Data schema changes require a corresponding Alembic migration and WASH data lead approval before merge to main.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Gradient boosting model performance degrades as camp population composition changes over time. Mitigation: model_version field in risk_scores enables retrospective performance analysis; retraining pipeline specified in future scope with historical outbreak labels as training signal. Risk: Weather API rate limits during parallel camp coordinate queries cause ingestion failures. Mitigation: batch camp coordinates in groups of 50 per API call and add 10-second sleep between batches to stay within rate limits. Risk: Stale sanitation ratio data (surveys conducted quarterly) produces lagged features between survey cycles. Mitigation: sanitation_ratios recency window extended to 90 days in feature engineering; stale_sanitation flag added to risk_scores if most recent survey >60 days old. Tradeoff: scikit-learn batch inference over a real-time serving endpoint reduces operational complexity at the cost of latency; acceptable for a daily batch pipeline. Tradeoff: Cloud SQL single instance over Spanner chosen for simplicity and cost; must be revisited if camp count scales to 10000+.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed risk tier thresholds (critical >0.85, high 0.65-0.85, elevated 0.40-0.65) with WASH domain specialists based on historical outbreak onset probability distributions. Decided against real-time streaming in MVP in favor of daily batch to reduce infrastructure complexity and cost. Selected Cloud Run Jobs over Cloud Composer (Airflow) to avoid $400/month Composer minimum cost. GCS model artifact path with manual promotion step chosen over SageMaker to stay within GCP stack and budget.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to address the identified focus issues by decoupling the webhook handler from the LLM triage pipeline and ensuring that confidential disclosure content is not logged in structured logs. The plan also includes a background worker for LLM calls, fine-tuning dataset accumulation, and a secure, scalable, and compliant architecture that meets the project's requirements and constraints.

fix_report.action_taken: Decouple webhook handler from LLM pipeline using an asyncio.Queue; handler returns 202 immediately
fix_report.expected_outcome: The webhook handler no longer blocks on LLM responses, reducing the risk of duplicate triage jobs due to LLM latency spikes. This change ensures that the system can handle webhook deliveries asynchronously, maintaining the 60-second latency requirement.

fix_report.action_taken: Remove all structured log statements that include disclosure report body; redirect raw content exclusively to Cloud Storage via the triage worker
fix_report.expected_outcome: Confidential disclosure content is no longer exposed in structured logs, mitigating the risk of sensitive data exposure. Raw reports are now stored in a Cloud Storage bucket with restricted IAM access, ensuring compliance with data sensitivity requirements.

parsed.title: Vulnerability Triage Prototype Architecture
parsed.executive_summary: The revised architecture plan addresses the high-severity focus issues by decoupling the webhook handler from the LLM triage pipeline and securing the storage of confidential disclosure content. The plan includes a background worker for LLM calls, fine-tuning dataset accumulation, and a secure, scalable, and compliant architecture that meets the project's requirements and constraints. The plan is designed to be implementation-grade and security-aware, with a focus on resolving unresolved critical and high-severity issues first.

reference.thinking_summary: Two blocking issues from round 1 both require architectural changes. ISS-001 requires decoupling the webhook handler from the LLM pipeline using an asyncio.Queue so the handler returns 202 immediately. ISS-002 requires auditing all log statements to remove disclosure content and routing raw report text exclusively to Cloud Storage. Both fixes must cascade into the workflows, system_components, and security_and_compliance sections to be coherent.

fix_report.action_taken: Webhook handler now writes report_id and storage_gcs_path to an in-memory asyncio.Queue and returns 202 Accepted within 200ms. A background asyncio task consumes the queue, fetches the report from Cloud Storage, calls Azure OpenAI, and updates the triage_decisions row with status=awaiting_review. Idempotency key (hackerone_report_id) prevents duplicate triage jobs on webhook retry.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Webhook handler responds before HackerOne 60s timeout; duplicate delivery protection via idempotency key prevents duplicate cvss_assessments rows.

fix_report.action_taken: All structured log statements referencing disclosure content replaced with report_id references only. Raw report body written exclusively to Cloud Storage bucket (vuln-reports-archive) by the webhook handler before enqueuing. Triage worker fetches from Cloud Storage using service account credentials. Cloud Logging now contains only report_id, status transitions, and LLM token/latency metrics.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cloud Logging viewer role grants no access to vulnerability report content; disclosure text accessible only via Cloud Storage IAM-controlled bucket.

reference.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Triage Prototype Async Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 FastAPI service on GCP Cloud Run that ingests HackerOne vulnerability disclosure webhooks, archives raw report content to Cloud Storage, and processes CVSS inference and affected asset mapping via Azure OpenAI GPT-4o in a background asyncio pipeline. Security analysts review LLM-generated triage_decisions via a JWT-authenticated REST API, with confirm/override actions recorded as labelled training data for future model fine-tuning. Disclosure content is excluded from all structured logs and accessible only via IAM-controlled Cloud Storage signed URLs.
reference.architecture_overview: Two-stage async architecture on Cloud Run: the webhook handler (POST /webhooks/hackerone) validates HackerOne HMAC signature, writes the raw report body to Cloud Storage, inserts a vulnerability_reports row, and enqueues the report_id to an in-memory asyncio.Queue before returning 202 Accepted. A background coroutine (triage_worker) consumes the queue, fetches the report from Cloud Storage, constructs a sanitised prompt (title and summary, max 2000 tokens), and calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with the cvss_triage JSON schema for structured output. The worker writes cvss_assessments and asset_mappings rows, then sets triage_decisions.status=awaiting_review. Analysts use GET /triage?status=awaiting_review and PATCH /triage/{id}/review to confirm or override; overrides are stored with the analyst-provided rationale in llm_inference_logs as labelled training examples.
reference.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12 with FastAPI and asyncio background tasks on GCP Cloud Run. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with private IP via VPC connector. Storage: Cloud Storage for raw disclosure archives and fine-tuning dataset exports. LLM: Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o (2024-11-20 deployment) with structured output JSON schema mode. Auth: custom HS256 JWT with analyst and admin roles. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with gcloud run deploy. Observability: Google Cloud Monitoring custom metrics, Cloud Logging structured JSON.
reference.functional_feature_map: Webhook Ingestion: HMAC-verified POST /webhooks/hackerone; idempotent by hackerone_report_id; archives raw body to Cloud Storage; returns 202. Triage Pipeline: asyncio background worker; GPT-4o structured CVSS vector inference; affected asset mapping with confidence scores. Analyst Review API: GET /triage with status filter; PATCH /triage/{id}/review for confirm/override with rationale; role-restricted to analyst and admin JWT claims. Dataset Export: GET /triage/export-labels (admin only) produces JSONL of confirmed and overridden triage decisions for fine-tuning. LLM Spend Tracking: custom Cloud Monitoring metric opa_triage.llm_tokens_total with monthly budget alert at $400.
reference.system_components: Cloud Run (triage-api): FastAPI app with webhook ingestion module, triage worker coroutine, and review API module. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: structured triage data with private VPC access. Cloud Storage (vuln-reports-archive): raw HackerOne report bodies; uniform bucket-level access; triage-worker service account read-only. Cloud Storage (label-exports): JSONL fine-tuning snapshots; admin service account write. GCP Secret Manager: Azure OpenAI key, PostgreSQL credentials, JWT signing secret. Azure OpenAI Service: GPT-4o endpoint with data processing addendum covering confidential content.
reference.workflows: Disclosure Ingestion and Triage: HackerOne delivers POST to /webhooks/hackerone with HMAC-SHA256 signature; handler verifies signature, checks idempotency against vulnerability_reports table, writes report body to Cloud Storage at gs://vuln-reports-archive/{report_id}.json, inserts vulnerability_reports row with storage_gcs_path, enqueues report_id, returns HTTP 202. Background triage_worker fetches report from Cloud Storage, truncates to 2000 tokens, calls Azure OpenAI with cvss_triage JSON schema (attack_vector, attack_complexity, privileges_required, user_interaction, scope, CIA impact triad, base_score), writes cvss_assessments and asset_mappings rows, sets triage_decisions status=awaiting_review. Analyst Review and Override: analyst calls GET /triage?status=awaiting_review; selects a pending decision; reviews LLM CVSS vector alongside report summary (title only in API response; full report via signed Cloud Storage URL); calls PATCH /triage/{id}/review with action=confirm or action=override plus corrected_cvss_vector and rationale; action written to llm_inference_logs as labelled example; triage_decisions status set to confirmed or overridden.
reference.data_model: vulnerability_reports (id UUID PK, hackerone_report_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, title TEXT, severity_reported VARCHAR, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ, storage_gcs_path TEXT). cvss_assessments (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, attack_vector VARCHAR, attack_complexity VARCHAR, privileges_required VARCHAR, user_interaction VARCHAR, scope VARCHAR, confidentiality_impact VARCHAR, integrity_impact VARCHAR, availability_impact VARCHAR, base_score_computed NUMERIC(4,1), llm_model_version VARCHAR, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). asset_mappings (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, candidate_service VARCHAR, candidate_team VARCHAR, confidence_score NUMERIC(4,3), llm_reasoning TEXT). triage_decisions (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK UNIQUE, status VARCHAR CHECK (status IN ('pending','awaiting_review','confirmed','overridden')), assigned_analyst_id UUID, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). llm_inference_logs (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT, latency_ms INT, model_version VARCHAR, analyst_action VARCHAR, corrected_cvss_vector TEXT, rationale TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
reference.api_design: POST /webhooks/hackerone: public; HMAC-SHA256 X-HackerOne-Signature header verification; returns 202 Accepted on success, 409 Conflict on duplicate report. GET /triage: JWT auth (analyst+admin); query params: status (pending/awaiting_review/confirmed/overridden), limit, offset; returns array of triage_decisions with associated cvss_assessments. GET /triage/{id}: JWT auth; returns full triage decision including cvss_assessments and asset_mappings plus Cloud Storage signed URL for report body (1h expiry). PATCH /triage/{id}/review: JWT auth (analyst+admin); body: {action: confirm|override, corrected_cvss_vector?: string, rationale?: string}. GET /triage/export-labels: JWT auth (admin only); returns JSONL stream of all confirmed and overridden decisions for fine-tuning.
reference.security_and_compliance: HMAC verification: HackerOne webhook signature verified using X-HackerOne-Signature header before any processing; invalid signatures return 403 immediately. Disclosure content isolation: raw report body written to Cloud Storage (not to PostgreSQL or logs); triage-api service account has write-only access to vuln-reports-archive bucket; only triage-worker task role has read access; analysts receive signed URLs with 1-hour expiry via GET /triage/{id}. JWT auth: HS256 tokens with analyst and admin roles; 8-hour expiry; admin role required for label export endpoint. Secret management: Azure OpenAI API key and PostgreSQL password stored in GCP Secret Manager; mounted as env vars at deploy time. Log hygiene: all Cloud Logging entries reference report_id only; no disclosure text in structured logs; verified in CI via log content assertions. Compliance: Azure OpenAI DPA covers confidential disclosure content; internal responsible-disclosure policy governs retention and access.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run service (triage-api) in us-central1; min-instances=1 in production to prevent cold-start on webhook delivery; max-instances=10 for burst; 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM per instance. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-g1-small) with private IP via Serverless VPC Access connector; automated backups to Cloud Storage with 7-day retention. GitHub Actions CI: ruff, mypy, pytest (>80% coverage), Docker build, push to Artifact Registry; production deploy via gcloud run deploy with --revision-suffix on version tags requiring manual approval. Environments: dev (min-instances=0, Cloud SQL shared-core); production (min-instances=1, db-g1-small). Secrets rotation: Secret Manager versions rotated quarterly; Cloud Run revisions redeploy automatically on new secret version.
reference.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring custom metrics: opa_triage.reports_ingested_total, opa_triage.llm_latency_ms (histogram), opa_triage.llm_tokens_total (labelled by type=prompt/completion), opa_triage.analyst_decisions_total (labelled by action=confirmed/overridden), opa_triage.triage_queue_depth. Alerts: Cloud Monitoring alert when llm_tokens_total monthly spend projection exceeds $400; alert on triage_queue_depth >50 for more than 5 minutes; alert on Cloud Run 5xx error rate >2%. Cloud Logging: structured JSON with fields report_id, status, latency_ms -- no disclosure content. Dashboards: Looker Studio dashboard showing daily ingestion rate, LLM latency percentiles, analyst override rate, and cost-per-report trend.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run: ~$20/month at 1000 reports/month (CPU+memory per request). Cloud SQL db-g1-small: ~$45/month. Cloud Storage: ~$2/month for report archives at average 5KB per report. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o at ~$5/1M input tokens: 1000 reports x 800 tokens average = $4/month well within $500 budget. Total estimated prototype cost: ~$71/month at 1000 reports/month. Scaling ceiling: Cloud Run max-instances=10 supports burst ingestion; Cloud SQL upgrade to db-n1-standard-2 when throughput exceeds 500 concurrent analyst sessions.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Core Pipeline (weeks 1-3): FastAPI app with webhook ingestion (HMAC verification, Cloud Storage archive, idempotency), triage_worker asyncio coroutine calling Azure OpenAI with CVSS JSON schema, PostgreSQL schema migration with Alembic, JWT auth, GET/PATCH /triage review endpoints, CI pipeline. Phase 2 Analyst UX and Dataset Export (weeks 4-6): asset_mappings ingestion from LLM response, signed URL generation for report body access, GET /triage/export-labels JSONL endpoint, Cloud Monitoring custom metrics and budget alert, dev/prod environment separation. Phase 3 Research Evaluation (weeks 7-8): CVSS accuracy evaluation against analyst overrides, override rate dashboard, cost-per-report metric, research findings report comparing LLM base_score to NVD-published scores for historical reports.
reference.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy. ruff for linting. pytest with respx for mocking Azure OpenAI calls; minimum 80% coverage. All log.debug/info calls use report_id as the disclosure identifier, never report body content; enforced via custom pylint rule in CI. Alembic for database migrations; all schema changes reviewed before merging to main. Azure OpenAI responses validated against Pydantic model before writing to PostgreSQL.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o CVSS inference accuracy below acceptable threshold -- mitigated by recording all analyst overrides; if override rate exceeds 40% after 200 reports, halt and evaluate fine-tuning approach. Risk: asyncio.Queue state lost on Cloud Run instance restart -- acceptable for prototype; production upgrade would use Cloud Tasks for durability. Tradeoff: in-memory asyncio.Queue for simplicity over Cloud Tasks; acceptable at prototype scale but documented as a known limitation. Risk: Azure OpenAI rate limiting under burst webhook delivery -- mitigated by exponential backoff with 30s cap and Cloud Run max-instances limiting concurrent LLM callers to 10.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Azure OpenAI over direct OpenAI API due to data processing addendum requirement for confidential disclosure content. Chose asyncio.Queue over Cloud Tasks for prototype simplicity; Cloud Tasks migration path documented. Structured output JSON schema mode chosen over prompt-based JSON extraction to eliminate parsing failures. Asset mapping uses LLM confidence_score field; threshold for high-confidence auto-routing deferred to Phase 3 evaluation.

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a TypeScript SDK with adapter pattern for multi-jurisdiction regulatory feed consumption. Key decisions: FeedAdapter interface per source for extensibility, ChangeEvent as single canonical output type for consumer simplicity, EventEmitter for subscription API in MVP (async generator deferred), in-memory Map for deduplication with content SHA-256 hash. Zero production dependency goal constrained to node-fetch and xml2js per contract.
reference.title: Regulatory Change Feed SDK
reference.executive_summary: A TypeScript npm package exposing a RegulatoryFeedClient that polls EUR-Lex and Federal Register feeds, normalizes jurisdiction-specific XML/JSON payloads into a typed ChangeEvent interface, filters by consumer interest (jurisdiction and instrument_type), deduplicates via content SHA-256 hash, and emits events through an EventEmitter subscription API. Zero production dependencies beyond node-fetch and xml2js.
reference.architecture_overview: SDK follows an adapter pattern: FeedAdapter interface with poll() and parse() methods implemented per source (EurLexAdapter, FederalRegisterAdapter). RegulatoryFeedClient orchestrates polling schedules via setInterval per registered adapter, passes raw items to ChangeEventNormalizer, checks DeduplicationCache, routes passing events through InterestFilter, and emits on the client EventEmitter. No server runtime or database — the SDK runs in the consumer process.
reference.technology_stack: TypeScript 5.x in strict mode. Node.js 20 runtime target. Production dependencies: node-fetch 3 for HTTP polling, xml2js for EUR-Lex SGML/XML parsing. DevDependencies: jest, ts-jest, @typescript-eslint, semantic-release. Distributed as CommonJS and ESM dual-build via tsup. Published to npmjs.com. Changesets for versioned changelog management. vitest for unit tests alongside jest during migration window. Type coverage enforced at >95% via type-coverage CLI in CI.
reference.functional_feature_map: FeedAdapter registration: consumer registers adapters for desired sources. Poll scheduling: RegulatoryFeedClient calls each adapter's poll() on configured pollIntervalMs. ChangeEvent normalization: EurLexAdapter produces ChangeEvents from OJ REST API atom+xml responses; FederalRegisterAdapter produces ChangeEvents from federalregister.gov JSON API. Deduplication: DeduplicationCache checks content_hash before emission, discards duplicates. Interest filtering: InterestFilter routes events to subscriber callbacks matching jurisdiction and instrument_type. Event emission: client.on('change', callback), client.on('error', callback), client.on('debug', callback).
reference.system_components: RegulatoryFeedClient (main class, EventEmitter subclass), FeedAdapter interface, EurLexAdapter (polls EUR-Lex OJ REST API, parses atom+xml via xml2js), FederalRegisterAdapter (polls federalregister.gov JSON API), ChangeEventNormalizer (maps adapter-specific objects to ChangeEvent), DeduplicationCache (in-memory Map keyed by content SHA-256), InterestFilter (matches ChangeEvents against subscriber filter predicates), FeedConfig type (API keys and poll intervals per source).
reference.workflows: Feed Poll Cycle: RegulatoryFeedClient.start() registers setInterval per adapter using pollIntervalMs from FeedConfig; on each tick adapter.poll() calls source API (EUR-Lex OJ endpoint or Federal Register /documents.json), returns RawFeedItem array; ChangeEventNormalizer maps each RawFeedItem to ChangeEvent setting source, jurisdiction, instrument_type, title, publication_date, document_url, change_type, and content_hash; DeduplicationCache checks hash — if seen, item discarded; if new, hash stored and event passed to InterestFilter; InterestFilter evaluates subscriber predicates and emits 'change' to matching subscribers. Error Handling: any adapter.poll() network error emits 'error' event with FeedError containing sourceId and cause; polling continues for other adapters; consumer decides whether to stop or continue.
reference.data_model: ChangeEvent TypeScript interface: {id: string, source: 'eur_lex'|'federal_register'|'uk_legislation', jurisdiction: string, instrument_type: 'regulation'|'directive'|'notice'|'rule'|'order', title: string, publication_date: string, effective_date: string|null, document_url: string, change_type: 'new'|'amendment'|'repeal', content_hash: string}. RawFeedItem: {sourceId: string, rawData: Record<string, unknown>}. FeedConfig: {sources: Record<string, {apiKey: string, pollIntervalMs: number}>}. DeduplicationCache: Map<string, number> keyed by content_hash, value is first_seen_timestamp.
reference.api_design: SDK public API: new RegulatoryFeedClient(config: FeedConfig), client.register(adapter: FeedAdapter), client.subscribe(filter: InterestFilter, callback: (event: ChangeEvent) => void), client.on('change'|'error'|'debug', handler), client.start(), client.stop(). FeedAdapter interface: {sourceId: string, poll(): Promise<RawFeedItem[]>}. EUR-Lex integration: GET https://eur-lex.europa.eu/OJXML/api/ojxmlinterface?type=OJ&dateFrom={date}. Federal Register integration: GET https://www.federalregister.gov/api/v1/documents.json?publication_date[gte]={date}&per_page=100.
reference.security_and_compliance: API keys provided in FeedConfig; RegulatoryFeedClient constructor validates presence of apiKey per configured source. apiKey values redacted before any 'debug' event emission — config is cloned with apiKey replaced by '[REDACTED]'. HTTPS enforced: node-fetch calls to all feed APIs use https:// URLs; HTTP URLs rejected with FeedConfigError at construction. No credential persistence: FeedConfig held in constructor closure, not stored to disk or emitted in events. SDK emits no telemetry to external services.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR: tsc --noEmit, eslint @typescript-eslint strict, jest with 80% coverage gate, npm pack dry-run to verify package contents. On merge to main: semantic-release runs based on conventional commit messages, publishes to npmjs.com with CHANGELOG update. Package built as CommonJS and ESM dual output via tsup for maximum consumer compatibility. Peer dependency declaration: Node.js >=18.
reference.observability: SDK emits DebugEvent on client 'debug' channel: {type: 'poll_complete'|'parse_error'|'dedup_hit'|'filter_miss', sourceId: string, durationMs?: number, eventCount?: number}. Consumer opts in by attaching 'debug' listener. No mandatory telemetry. FeedError type includes sourceId, httpStatusCode (if available), and cause for structured error handling in consumer applications.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost — SDK runs in consumer process. npm package size target under 50KB packed. Polling frequency is consumer-controlled via pollIntervalMs; recommended minimum 60000ms to respect EUR-Lex and Federal Register rate limits. DeduplicationCache grows proportional to unique events seen since start(); consumers calling stop() and recreating the client will clear cache.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): EurLexAdapter and FederalRegisterAdapter, ChangeEventNormalizer, DeduplicationCache, InterestFilter, EventEmitter subscription API, TypeScript strict build, jest unit tests, npm publish. Phase 2 (4 weeks): UK Legislation.gov.uk adapter, async generator subscription interface alongside EventEmitter, webhook receiver helper for push-based sources. Phase 3: Persistent deduplication cache option (consumer-provided storage adapter interface) for long-running processes surviving restarts.
reference.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode; no-explicit-any enforced by @typescript-eslint. All public types exported from index.ts — no barrel files burying types in subdirectories. ChangeEvent interface is the contract; normalizer must produce valid ChangeEvents from all adapters. FeedAdapter implementations tested with recorded fixture responses from each feed source — no live API calls in unit tests. Semantic versioning: breaking ChangeEvent interface changes require major version bump.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EUR-Lex or Federal Register API schema changes breaking normalizer field extraction. Mitigation: RawFeedItem captures full raw response; normalizer logs missing fields as 'debug' events rather than throwing; consumers can inspect raw data via FeedAdapter extension. Risk: In-memory deduplication cache unbounded growth in long-running processes. Mitigation: document cache growth rate in README; phase 2 persistent cache option addresses this. Tradeoff: EventEmitter over async generator for MVP — lower cognitive overhead for most consumer use cases; async generator added in phase 2.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed EventEmitter over async generator for phase 1 subscription API due to broader consumer familiarity. Chose content SHA-256 hash over document URL alone for deduplication because EUR-Lex republishes documents at the same URL with amended content. Selected node-fetch 3 over native Node.js fetch for Node.js 18 compatibility where native fetch is unflagged but not fully stable.

{
  "thinking_summary": "The architecture plan aims to address the unresolved issue of validation of S3 evaluation data before the Gatsby build process to ensure that the static site is always built with complete and up-to-date data. This is crucial for maintaining the reliability of the weekly reports and meeting the stakeholder deadlines. The plan will incorporate a CI validation step that checks each evaluation JSON file against a JSON Schema spec, ensuring that all required fields are present and that the evaluation_date is within the expected window. This step will prevent the silent deployment of incomplete or outdated data, which is a critical requirement for the project's success.",
  "fix_report": [
    {
      "issue_id": "ISS-001",
      "action_taken": "Added a CI valid

reference.thinking_summary: Addressing ISS-001 by adding a JSON schema validation step between S3 sync and gatsby build in the GitLab CI pipeline. The validator checks each evaluation file for required fields (mape_by_category, model_version, evaluation_date, drift_flag) and verifies evaluation_date is within 8 days of the pipeline trigger. On failure the pipeline exits non-zero with a structured error message, preventing silent deploy of stale or incomplete model performance data.

fix_report.action_taken: Added validate_evaluation_data.py script executed as a CI step between S3 sync and gatsby build. Script uses jsonschema to validate each weekly report JSON against a checked-in schema spec; also checks that evaluation_date is within 8 days of the current date. Pipeline fails with exit code 1 and prints which files failed validation and why.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CI pipeline fails fast with a clear error message rather than deploying incomplete or stale demand forecasting charts to stakeholders.

reference.title: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite - Revised Architecture v2
reference.executive_summary: A static Gatsby 5 site auto-generated every Monday by a GitLab CI pipeline from S3 model evaluation JSON files. Displays weekly demand forecasting accuracy (MAPE by SKU category, RMSE trend) and dynamic pricing model performance summaries with Recharts. Served from S3 via CloudFront with IP allowlist restricting access to corporate network. This revision adds a JSON schema validation gate in CI to prevent silent deployment of incomplete or stale evaluation data.
reference.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture with no server-side runtime. GitLab CI pipeline runs every Monday at 5 AM UTC: syncs current week evaluation JSON from s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/, runs validate_evaluation_data.py against JSON Schema spec, and on pass invokes gatsby build which reads local JSON files to pre-render all chart pages. Built artifacts deployed to S3 static hosting bucket behind CloudFront distribution. IP allowlist WAF rule restricts CloudFront access to corporate VPN and office CIDR ranges. Historical weekly report pages archived as static files; no database or server-side compute at any layer.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Gatsby 5, React 18, Recharts 2 for MAPE/RMSE line charts and pricing accuracy bar charts, Tailwind CSS 3. Build tooling: Node 20 LTS, gatsby-cli. CI: GitLab CI with scheduled pipeline. Validation: Python 3.12 with jsonschema for evaluation data quality gate. Infrastructure: AWS S3 (static hosting), CloudFront (OAC, IP allowlist WAF, ACM HTTPS), GitLab CI runner with AWS IAM role for S3 access.
reference.functional_feature_map: Current Week Report Page (/): demand forecasting section with MAPE by SKU category Recharts LineChart (Electronics, Apparel, Home Goods, Grocery categories), RMSE 13-week trend chart, model_version badge, data drift alert banner rendered if drift_flag=true in evaluation JSON. Dynamic Pricing Model Section: price elasticity accuracy percentage and revenue impact bar chart from pricing_model_summaries JSON. Report Archive Page (/archive): table of past 52 weeks with ISO week identifiers, generation timestamps, and links to /report/YYYY-WW/. Historical Report Pages (/report/YYYY-WW/): identical layout to current week, pre-rendered as static pages.
reference.system_components: GitLab CI Pipeline: scheduled job with aws s3 sync stage, validate_evaluation_data.py validation stage, gatsby build stage, aws s3 sync deploy stage, CloudFront invalidation stage. validate_evaluation_data.py: loads weekly_report_schema.json spec; validates each evaluation file; checks evaluation_date freshness (<=8 days); exits 1 with field-level error on failure. Gatsby Build: gatsby-node.js reads ./data/*.json; calls createPages for each weekly report file creating /report/YYYY-WW/ pages; WeeklyReportPage template renders Recharts charts from page context data. S3 Hosting Bucket: public access blocked, versioning enabled, OAC-only access policy. CloudFront Distribution: OAC, WAF IP set allowlist, ACM cert, 7-day cache TTL for archive pages, 1-hour TTL for current week page.
reference.workflows: Weekly Report Generation Workflow: GitLab scheduled pipeline triggers 5 AM UTC Monday; aws s3 sync downloads current/ evaluation JSON to ./data/; validate_evaluation_data.py checks each file against schema - if evaluation_date for demand_forecasting report is 2026-W12 but current week is 2026-W13, pipeline exits 1 with 'demand_forecasting_2026-W12.json: evaluation_date 2026-03-17 is 10 days old, expected within 8 days'; on pass, gatsby build pre-renders WeeklyReportPage with MAPE Recharts LineChart data from JSON; aws s3 sync deploys public/ to S3; CloudFront invalidation clears current week page cache. Archive Access Workflow: stakeholder opens corporate browser, CloudFront WAF checks source IP against allowlist, serves /archive/ static page listing past 52 weekly reports; stakeholder clicks 2025-W48 link to view historical demand forecasting accuracy for Black Friday week.
reference.data_model: model_performance_reports JSON schema: {week_id: str ISO-week format YYYY-WW, model_type: enum demand_forecasting|dynamic_pricing, evaluation_date: date ISO8601, model_version: str, mape_by_category: {Electronics: float, Apparel: float, Home_Goods: float, Grocery: float}, rmse_overall: float, drift_flag: bool, affected_features: list[str]}. pricing_model_summaries JSON schema: {week_id: str, evaluation_date: date, model_version: str, elasticity_accuracy: float, revenue_impact_pct: float}. weekly_report_archives: Gatsby page metadata keyed by week_id with s3_key and generated_at fields for archive listing page.
reference.api_design: No runtime API. Build-time data access: Gatsby reads ./data/demand_forecasting_YYYY-WW.json and ./data/pricing_YYYY-WW.json via filesystem. gatsby-node.js createPages iterates matching files and passes JSON as pageContext to WeeklyReportPage template. No client-side fetch calls in deployed static site; all data embedded in pre-rendered HTML and JS bundles.
reference.security_and_compliance: CloudFront WAF IP set allowlist configured with corporate VPN CIDR and office IP ranges; all requests from non-listed IPs receive 403. S3 bucket policy denies all s3:GetObject except from CloudFront OAC service principal; direct S3 URL access returns 403. HTTPS enforced via CloudFront with ACM certificate; HTTP redirected to HTTPS. CI IAM role has s3:GetObject on s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/ and s3:PutObject on s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ only; no other permissions. CloudFront access logs retained 90 days.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline stages: (1) download_data: aws s3 sync s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/ ./data/ using CI IAM role; (2) validate_data: python3 validate_evaluation_data.py --schema weekly_report_schema.json --max-age-days 8 --data-dir ./data/; exits 1 on validation failure with field-level error output; (3) build: gatsby build; (4) deploy: aws s3 sync public/ s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ --delete; (5) invalidate: aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id $CF_DIST_ID --paths '/index.html' '/archive/*'. Scheduled trigger: Monday 5 AM UTC. Manual trigger available for out-of-cycle model evaluation updates. On pipeline failure, Slack webhook notification sent to #retail-analytics-ops channel.
reference.observability: CloudFront standard access logs delivered to s3://retail-mlops/cloudfront-logs/ with 90-day S3 lifecycle retention. GitLab CI pipeline status visible in GitLab dashboard; Slack webhook on failure. No application-level APM required for a static site. Weekly report generation success tracked via GitLab CI pipeline pass/fail rate.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero hosting cost: S3 static hosting ($0.023/GB/month for ~5 MB weekly site), CloudFront (~$0.01/GB transfer on corporate internal traffic). GitLab CI runner minutes: ~10 minutes per weekly pipeline. Gatsby build scales linearly with archive size; 52-week archive builds in under 3 minutes on standard CI runner.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete - round 1): Gatsby site structure, WeeklyReportPage template, Recharts MAPE and pricing charts, S3/CloudFront IP-restricted hosting, GitLab CI weekly pipeline, archive page. Phase 2 (this revision - round 2): validate_evaluation_data.py CI validation gate with JSON Schema check and evaluation_date freshness check; Slack failure notifications. Phase 3 (Q3): model version comparison view showing MAPE trend across model versions; email digest with report link sent to stakeholder Slack channel on successful Monday deploy.
reference.development_guidelines: Evaluation JSON schema spec (weekly_report_schema.json) is checked into the repo and versioned alongside the site; schema changes require PR review from the data science team. Gatsby pageContext types defined in TypeScript interfaces matching the JSON schema. Local development uses mock evaluation JSON fixtures in ./data/fixtures/. All CI pipeline scripts are Python 3.12 with type hints; validated by mypy in CI.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: data science pipeline delayed past 5 AM UTC; validate_evaluation_data.py freshness check fails and Monday report is not published. Mitigation: Slack alert to #retail-analytics-ops; manual pipeline trigger available once data is ready. Tradeoff: static generation means the site reflects the state of evaluation JSON at build time; intra-week model updates are not reflected until the next weekly build or manual trigger. Risk: CloudFront IP allowlist requires manual update when office CIDR ranges change; undocumented update process causes access outage. Mitigation: IP allowlist managed as Terraform resource with change documented in runbook.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Gatsby over Next.js because static export is the primary use case and no server-side rendering is needed. Confirmed Recharts over D3 for simpler React integration with pre-processed JSON data. GitLab CI selected over GitHub Actions per engineering platform constraint. IP allowlist confirmed as sufficient access control for internal-only site.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to create a secure, scalable, and user-friendly Python SDK for the USDA NASS API, focusing on typed data models, query builders, and rate-limit-aware pagination to assist data scientists and agronomists in crop yield analysis workflows. The plan addresses current focus issues, preserves user-confirmed requirements, and improves the architecture without introducing regressions. It includes concrete architecture, modules, workflows, schemas, APIs, security, deployment, observability, roadmap, and developer guidance, with a phased implementation approach.

fix_report.action_taken: Refactored query builder to use method chaining for better IDE autocomplete and reduce parameter name lookup.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CommodityQueryBuilder methods now support method chaining, enhancing developer experience and reducing potential errors.

fix_report.action_taken: Mapped NASS API snake_case field names to Python-idiomatic names in Pydantic models.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Field names in Pydantic models are now Python-idiomatic, improving readability and consistency.

fix_report.action_taken: Abstracted pagination behind a generator yielding individual Pydantic model instances.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Pagination is now handled through a generator, allowing consumers to stop early with next().

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented rate-limit context with exponential backoff and error handling for rate-limit exceeded.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Rate-limit context manager enforces limits and handles 429 responses with exponential backoff.

fix_report.action_taken: Ensured API key security by redacting the api_key query parameter in logs and configuring httpx client with a custom EventHook.
fix_report.expected_outcome: API key is now securely handled, and logs do not contain sensitive information.

fix_report.action_taken: Added optional pandas dependency with a clear import error message for users who wish to use .to_dataframe() method.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Users are informed about the optional pandas dependency and how to install it if needed.

fix_report.action_taken: Ensured Python 3.10 compatibility by using Optional[X] and Union[X, Y] from typing module instead of match/case statements and X | Y syntax.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SDK is now compatible with Python 3.10 and avoids common type-related errors.

parsed.title: USDA NASS API Python SDK for Crop Yield Analysis Workflows
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan outlines the development of a Python SDK for the USDA NASS API, focusing on typed data models, query builders, and rate-limit-aware pagination to support data scientists and agronomists. The plan addresses current focus issues, preserves user-confirmed requirements, and aims to improve the architecture without introducing regressions. It includes a phased implementation approach with a focus on security, scalability, and developer guidance.
parsed.architecture_overview: The SDK will provide typed data models, query builders, and rate-limit-aware pagination for crop yield analysis workflows. It will be a Python library with no frontend, running in user's local Python environment. The SDK will transform NASS API JSON responses into Pydantic models and pandas DataFrames, with optional caching for repeated queries. It will be distributed via PyPI and hosted on GitHub Actions for CI/CD.

technology_stack.backend: Python 3.10+, httpx for HTTP, Pydantic v2 for response models, pandas for .to_dataframe(), pytest for testing
technology_stack.devops: PyPI distribution via GitHub Actions, optional dependencies: nass-sdk[pandas] installs pandas>=1.5; nass-sdk[async] installs httpx[asyncio]

functional_feature_map.NassClient: Class with typed query builders for commodity statistics, state-level estimates, survey programs, and acreage reports
functional_feature_map.CommodityQuery: Typed query builder for commodity statistics

system_components.api_design: Fluent query builder API with Pydantic models and httpx for HTTP requests
system_components.security_and_compliance: API key security, HTTPS enforcement, no data storage by SDK, no telemetry or usage tracking
system_components.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution, GitHub Actions CI/CD, zero hosting cost
system_components.observability: Python standard library logging with nass_sdk logger, debug-level logs for HTTP requests and rate-limit events
system_components.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost, PyPI free, GitHub Actions free tier sufficient for open-source repo
system_components.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Synchronous HTTP client, CommodityQuery builder, YieldEstimate and AcreageReport Pydantic models, automatic pagination, rate-limit-aware retry with exponential backoff. Phase 2: Async-first client, Redis-backed query cache, CLI wrapper, polars DataFrame integration.
system_components.development_guidelines: TDD approach, tests written before implementation, GitHub Actions for CI on PR and main merge, GitHub Releases for PyPI publish
system_components.risks_and_tradeoffs: Minor tradeoffs for optional pandas dependency, ensuring Python 3.10 compatibility, and maintaining a small core for flexibility.

open_questions_resolved.question: Should the async client be included in the MVP or Phase 2?
open_questions_resolved.resolution: Include in Phase 2, pending community adoption feedback.

reference.thinking_summary: This is a Python library_sdk for the USDA NASS public API -- no hosted service, no database, no frontend. Key design decisions: fluent method-chaining query builder for IDE autocomplete, generator-based pagination so consumers stop early without fetching all pages, optional pandas dependency to avoid forcing heavy imports on non-dataframe users, and httpx EventHook to redact api_key from debug logs. Plan quality is moderate -- some sections are appropriately brief for a library that has no deployment topology, no security hardening beyond API key hygiene, and no cost/scaling concerns.
reference.title: nass-sdk: Python Client for USDA NASS Quick Stats API
reference.executive_summary: A PyPI-distributed Python SDK (Python 3.10+) for the USDA NASS Quick Stats API providing typed Pydantic v2 response models, a fluent method-chaining query builder, generator-based pagination with transparent rate-limit enforcement, and .to_dataframe() output for pandas integration. Designed for data scientists and agronomists automating crop yield, acreage, and survey data retrieval in Python and Jupyter notebook workflows. No hosted infrastructure; distributed on PyPI via GitHub Actions trusted publisher.
reference.architecture_overview: Pure Python library distributed as a PyPI package. Three public modules: nass_sdk.client exposes NassClient (sync) and AsyncNassClient (Phase 2); nass_sdk.query exposes CommodityQueryBuilder, StateQueryBuilder, and SurveyQueryBuilder with fluent method chaining; nass_sdk.models exposes Pydantic v2 models (YieldEstimate, AcreageReport, StateStatistic, SurveyProgram, CommodityQuery). Internal module nass_sdk._http wraps httpx.Client with API key injection, rate-limit backoff, and EventHook-based api_key redaction from debug logs. No network calls are made on import. NassClient instantiation accepts api_key parameter or reads USDA_NASS_API_KEY environment variable. Pagination is abstracted as a generator yielding individual Pydantic model instances across NASS offset-based pages, allowing early stopping via next() or islice().
reference.technology_stack: Python 3.10+. Mandatory dependencies: httpx>=0.27 (HTTP client with sync support), pydantic>=2.6 (response model validation). Optional dependency groups: nass-sdk[pandas] adds pandas>=1.5 for .to_dataframe(); nass-sdk[dev] adds pytest, pytest-recording, vcrpy, ruff, mypy, coverage. Build: hatchling via pyproject.toml. Distribution: PyPI via GitHub Actions OIDC trusted publisher. Type stubs: included in package (py.typed marker, inline annotations).
reference.functional_feature_map: NassClient: instantiation with api_key or USDA_NASS_API_KEY env var; .commodity() returns CommodityQueryBuilder; .state_statistics() returns StateQueryBuilder; .survey_programs() returns SurveyQueryBuilder. CommodityQueryBuilder: .state(alpha_code) filters by state, .year(y) or .year_range(start, end) sets year filter, .unit_desc(ud) filters by unit descriptor, .commodity(name) sets commodity, .fetch() returns List[YieldEstimate], .all() returns Generator[YieldEstimate] paginating until exhausted. Rate-limit context manager: with client.rate_limited(hourly=500, daily=4800): enforces limits, retries on 429 with exponential backoff up to max_retries=3, raises NassRateLimitError if exhausted. .to_dataframe() on any List[PydanticModel]: returns pandas DataFrame with model field names as columns; raises ImportError with install hint if pandas not installed.
reference.system_components: nass_sdk/__init__.py: public exports (NassClient, NassRateLimitError, NassApiError, NassSdkError). nass_sdk/client.py: NassClient class, _http attribute, .commodity()/.state_statistics()/.survey_programs() factory methods. nass_sdk/query.py: CommodityQueryBuilder, StateQueryBuilder, SurveyQueryBuilder with .state()/.year()/.year_range()/.unit_desc()/.commodity()/.fetch()/.all() methods. nass_sdk/models.py: Pydantic v2 models with field validators for NASS string-numeric coercion and suppressed value handling. nass_sdk/_http.py: httpx.Client wrapper, api_key query param injection, rate_limit_state tracking, EventHook for api_key redaction, pagination logic as _fetch_pages() generator. nass_sdk/exceptions.py: NassSdkError, NassApiError, NassRateLimitError.
reference.workflows: Crop Yield Query Workflow: Data scientist instantiates client = NassClient() (reads USDA_NASS_API_KEY from env) -> chains client.commodity().state('IA').year_range(2018, 2023).unit_desc('BU / ACRE').fetch() -> SDK constructs NASS API URL with query parameters, paginates across offset pages internally, validates each record via YieldEstimate Pydantic model, returns List[YieldEstimate] -> researcher calls .to_dataframe() on result for pandas analysis. Rate-Limit-Aware Large Retrieval Workflow: Researcher needs all soybean acreage reports for all states (potentially thousands of records) -> uses with client.rate_limited(hourly=490, daily=4750): for report in client.commodity().commodity('SOYBEANS').all(): process(report) -> SDK yields YieldEstimate instances one by one, transparently fetching next offset page when needed, sleeping and retrying on 429 responses, surfacing NassRateLimitError only when max_retries exhausted. The generator pattern allows early termination if only first N results are needed.
reference.data_model: Pydantic v2 models (no persistent database). YieldEstimate: commodity_desc str, year int, state_alpha str, yield_value Optional[float] (None if NASS suppressed value '(D)'), unit_desc str, cv_pct Optional[float], reference_period_desc str, suppressed bool = False. AcreageReport: commodity_desc, year, state_alpha, planted_acres Optional[float], harvested_acres Optional[float], reference_period_desc, suppressed bool. StateStatistic: state_alpha, state_ansi_code str, state_name, county_code Optional[str], county_name Optional[str], value Optional[float], unit_desc, year, suppressed bool. SurveyProgram: source_desc NASS API literal (CENSUS or SURVEY), sector_desc, group_desc, commodity_desc. CommodityQuery: internal query parameter model used by query builders to construct NASS API request URLs.
reference.api_design: Public Python API (not HTTP endpoints). NassClient(api_key: Optional[str] = None, timeout: float = 30.0, max_retries: int = 3). .commodity() -> CommodityQueryBuilder. CommodityQueryBuilder.state(alpha: str) -> self. .year(y: int) -> self. .year_range(start: int, end: int) -> self. .unit_desc(ud: str) -> self. .fetch() -> List[YieldEstimate]. .all() -> Generator[YieldEstimate, None, None]. RateLimitedContext: client.rate_limited(hourly: int = 500, daily: int = 4800) -> contextmanager. DataFrame output: nass_sdk.utils.to_dataframe(records: List[BaseModel]) -> pd.DataFrame -- also available as .to_dataframe() method on List subclass returned by .fetch(). NassApiError(status_code: int, message: str). NassRateLimitError(retry_after: Optional[int]).
reference.security_and_compliance: API key security: NassClient reads api_key from constructor parameter or USDA_NASS_API_KEY environment variable; never falls back to hardcoded values. Key never included in exception messages, log output, or repr(). httpx EventHook on_request redacts api_key query parameter value to '***REDACTED***' before passing to httpx debug logger. HTTPS enforced: httpx.Client base_url set to https://quickstats.nass.usda.gov/api/get; no HTTP fallback. No credentials stored; each NassClient instance holds key in memory only for its lifetime. USDA NASS data is public domain; no license or compliance requirements on data usage.
reference.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution via hatchling build backend and pyproject.toml. GitHub Actions CI workflow: matrix test on Python 3.10/3.11/3.12, ruff lint, mypy --strict type check, pytest with vcrpy cassettes for deterministic offline NASS API replay (no live API calls in CI), coverage gate at 90%. GitHub Release (semver tag) triggers PyPI publish via OIDC trusted publisher (no API token stored in GitHub secrets). Versioning: semantic versioning; breaking API changes require major version bump per consumer convention. No deployment infrastructure.
reference.observability: Python standard library logging. Logger name: nass_sdk. Log levels: DEBUG -- each HTTP request URL (api_key redacted), page offset, records per page, rate_limit_remaining header value; INFO -- pagination complete with total records fetched; WARNING -- rate limit 429 received, retry attempt N of max_retries. Consumers configure log level via logging.getLogger('nass_sdk').setLevel(logging.DEBUG). No external monitoring, metrics, or telemetry.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. PyPI free tier. GitHub Actions free for open-source. USDA NASS API is free with API key registration (usda.gov). SDK performance scales with NASS API rate limits (not internal throughput). For large bulk queries, the rate_limited context manager serializes requests within configured hourly/daily limits. No horizontal scaling considerations -- library runs in user's Python process.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - PyPI MVP (4 weeks): NassClient sync, CommodityQueryBuilder and StateQueryBuilder with .state()/.year()/.year_range()/.fetch()/.all(), YieldEstimate and AcreageReport Pydantic v2 models with suppressed value handling, automatic offset pagination generator, rate_limited context manager with exponential backoff, .to_dataframe() with optional pandas guard, api_key EventHook redaction, pytest suite with vcrpy cassettes at 90% coverage, PyPI publish via GitHub Actions trusted publisher. Phase 2 - Async and CLI (6 weeks): AsyncNassClient using httpx.AsyncClient, SurveyQueryBuilder for USDA survey program queries, CLI wrapper (nass query --commodity CORN --state IA --year 2023) via click, polars DataFrame support as alternative to pandas output. Phase 3 - Caching and Ecosystem (Q3): Redis-backed query result cache for shared Jupyter Hub environments, SurveyProgram and StateStatistic query builders, integration tests against live NASS sandbox environment.
reference.development_guidelines: Python 3.10 compatibility: no match/case statements, no X|Y union syntax in signatures -- use Optional[X] and Union[X,Y] from typing. Pydantic models use model_validator(mode='before') to coerce NASS string fields before field validation. All public methods and classes must have full type annotations; mypy --strict must pass. pytest fixtures use vcrpy @pytest.mark.vcr decorator to record/replay NASS API responses -- no live API calls in test suite. Optional pandas import guard pattern: def to_dataframe(self) -> 'pd.DataFrame': try: import pandas as pd ... except ImportError: raise ImportError('Install nass-sdk[pandas] or run pip install pandas'). Every new query builder method requires a corresponding pytest test using a recorded cassette.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: NASS API changes field names or pagination behavior without notice (undocumented public API) -- mitigated by vcrpy cassettes failing CI immediately when replay diverges from live response, prompting cassette update. Tradeoff: optional pandas dependency over bundled -- avoids forcing 30MB dependency on researchers using only Pydantic models; accepted complexity is ImportError guidance in .to_dataframe(). Risk: NASS API enforces undocumented rate limits that differ from documented values -- rate_limited context manager parameters are user-configurable to allow conservative settings below documented limits. Tradeoff: sync-only client in MVP over async-first -- synchronous httpx simplifies initial implementation and covers the primary Jupyter notebook use case; async added in Phase 2 for pipeline automation use cases.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed httpx over requests -- httpx supports both sync and async with identical API surface, enabling AsyncNassClient in Phase 2 without rewriting HTTP logic. Confirmed generator-based pagination over page-returning API -- generator allows early stopping with next()/islice() which is critical for exploratory analysis in Jupyter where researchers want to inspect first 20 records before fetching all 5000. Confirmed optional pandas over required -- USDA NASS data is frequently used with polars or directly as Pydantic model lists in automated pipelines; requiring pandas would be inappropriate for non-dataframe users.
