{
  "thinking_summary": "The architecture plan aims to create a secure, scalable, and user-friendly tenant portal for multifamily residential operators that enables tenants to manage their rent payments, submit maintenance requests, and view lease documents. The plan addresses security concerns, particularly around payment processing and data privacy, while ensuring compliance with PCI-DSS and GDPR where applicable. It leverages Azure services for a consistent and secure deployment, with a focus on resolving current focus issues such as Yardi webhook integration and payment failure handling. The architecture is designed to be modular, with clear separation of concerns between frontend and backend, and includes robust observability and scalability considerations.",
  "fix_report": [

reference.thinking_summary: First-pass plan for a security-hardened tenant portal. Weak quality per manifest — plan sections are functional and correct but deliberately thinner than a strong plan. Key decisions: Django with DRF for rapid development, Azure AD B2C for MFA authentication, Stripe for PCI-compliant payment processing with SAQ-D scope managed through field-level encryption on stored ACH tokens. Yardi webhook integration needs a dedicated adapter to handle Yardi's unpredictable payload format.
reference.title: Security-Hardened Tenant Portal
reference.executive_summary: A fullstack tenant portal built with React 18 and Django 5 on Azure, enabling multifamily tenants to pay rent via ACH or card, submit maintenance requests, and access lease documents with MFA enforced by Azure AD B2C. PCI-DSS SAQ-D compliance is maintained through Stripe tokenization and field-level encryption on stored payment method tokens. Yardi CMMS integration routes maintenance requests to work orders via Celery async jobs.
reference.architecture_overview: Two-tier architecture: React 18 SPA on Azure Static Web Apps communicates over HTTPS to Django 5 REST API on Azure App Service. Tenants authenticate via Azure AD B2C with MFA; MSAL handles token refresh in the React client. Stripe Elements handles card and ACH tokenization in the browser — no raw payment credentials reach the Django server. Celery workers on a separate App Service process Stripe payment webhooks and sync maintenance request status from Yardi via outbound webhook calls. Lease documents stored in Azure Blob Storage with SAS URL generation for time-limited tenant access. Azure Key Vault holds all application secrets accessed via Managed Identity.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, React Router v6, React Query, MSAL for Azure AD B2C, Stripe.js Elements. Backend: Python 3.12, Django 5, Django REST Framework, Celery 5, django-encrypted-fields for payment token encryption, Stripe Python SDK. Database: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server. Cache/Queue: Azure Cache for Redis 7 (Celery broker + Django sessions). Storage: Azure Blob Storage for documents and maintenance photos. Auth: Azure AD B2C. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with MSI. CI/CD: Azure DevOps.
reference.functional_feature_map: Rent Payment: Stripe Elements form collects card or ACH; PaymentIntent confirmed server-side; payment_methods stored with field encryption; payment history in ledger. Maintenance Request: tenant submits category and description with photo uploads to Blob Storage; Celery job creates Yardi work order via API; tenant receives status updates as work order progresses. Lease Documents: tenant views and downloads PDFs from Azure Blob Storage via short-lived SAS URLs. Building Announcements: staff publishes notices visible to all tenants in assigned building. Staff Dashboard: management view of maintenance queue by building and assignment status.
reference.system_components: Azure Static Web Apps: React 18 SPA with Azure CDN. Azure App Service: Django 5 API (B2 SKU). Azure App Service Worker: Celery async jobs. Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16: primary datastore. Azure Cache for Redis 7: Celery broker and Django sessions. Azure Blob Storage: lease documents and maintenance photos. Azure Key Vault: Stripe keys, Django secret, DB password. Azure AD B2C: tenant and staff authentication with MFA.
reference.workflows: Rent Payment: tenant opens Pay Rent page; React renders Stripe Elements card or ACH form; on submit, Stripe.js creates PaymentMethod token; React POSTs token to POST /api/payments/; Django creates Stripe PaymentIntent, confirms charge, writes rent_payments record; Stripe webhook delivers payment_intent.succeeded to /api/webhooks/stripe/; Celery task updates rent_payments.status to succeeded and sends receipt email. Maintenance Request: tenant fills category, description, and uploads up to 3 photos; React POSTs to POST /api/maintenance/; Django stores maintenance_request record and uploads photos to Blob Storage; Celery job calls Yardi API to create work order and stores yardi_work_order_id; Yardi sends status webhook to /api/webhooks/yardi/ as work order progresses; Django updates maintenance_requests.status and emails tenant.
reference.data_model: tenants (id UUID PK, azure_ad_oid, name, email, phone, unit_id FK, created_at). leases (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, start_date, end_date, monthly_rent NUMERIC, document_blob_key, status ENUM active/expired). rent_payments (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, lease_id FK, amount NUMERIC, payment_method_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, status ENUM pending/succeeded/failed, paid_at). maintenance_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, category ENUM plumbing/electrical/hvac/general, description, photo_blob_keys JSONB, status ENUM submitted/assigned/in_progress/completed, yardi_work_order_id, created_at). payment_methods (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, type ENUM card/ach, stripe_payment_method_id_encrypted BYTEA, last4, created_at, deleted_at). lease_documents (id UUID PK, lease_id FK, doc_type ENUM lease/addendum/inspection, blob_key, uploaded_at). building_announcements (id UUID PK, building_id FK, title, body, published_at).
reference.api_design: GET /api/tenants/me/ — tenant profile. GET /api/leases/current/ — active lease details. POST /api/payments/ — initiate rent payment with Stripe token. GET /api/payments/ — payment history. POST /api/maintenance/ — submit maintenance request. GET /api/maintenance/ — tenant's request list. GET /api/documents/ — lease document list with SAS URL. GET /api/announcements/ — building announcements. POST /api/webhooks/stripe/ — Stripe event receiver (signature verified). POST /api/webhooks/yardi/ — Yardi work order status receiver (HMAC verified).
reference.security_and_compliance: Azure AD B2C MFA enforced for all tenants and staff. MSAL handles silent token refresh; tokens expire after 1 hour. PCI-DSS SAQ-D: Stripe Elements tokenizes card and ACH in browser; Django stores only stripe_payment_method_id encrypted via django-encrypted-fields with Azure Key Vault key. Django CSRF middleware enabled on all non-webhook endpoints. Webhook endpoints use Stripe signature verification and Yardi HMAC. Azure Key Vault MSI for secret access — no credentials in App Service configuration. Object-level permission checks on all DRF ViewSets ensure tenants cannot access other tenants' records. Azure App Service TLS minimum version 1.2.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps CI pipeline: flake8 lint, pytest with 80% coverage gate, build Docker image, push to Azure Container Registry. CD: az webapp deployment container config to deploy to App Service. React pipeline: eslint, jest unit tests, bun run build, Azure Static Web Apps deploy action. Environments: dev (App Service F1 free tier, PostgreSQL Burstable B1ms), staging (B1 + General Purpose 2 vCore), production (B2 + General Purpose 2 vCore, high-availability enabled). Database migrations via Django management command in release phase. Secrets rotated via Azure Key Vault 90-day rotation policy.
reference.observability: Azure Application Insights SDK in Django for request tracing and exception tracking. Custom events: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, maintenance_request_created, yardi_sync_failed. Azure Monitor alerts: payment failure rate > 2% triggers PagerDuty; Celery queue depth > 100 jobs alerts ops channel; PostgreSQL CPU > 80% for 10 minutes alerts DBA.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Azure App Service B2 ~$75/month. PostgreSQL General Purpose 2 vCore ~$140/month. Azure Cache for Redis C1 ~$55/month. Azure Blob Storage ~$5/month. Total ~$275/month for MVP. Scaling: App Service scale-out to 3 instances on CPU 70% target covers rent payment peak on the 1st of each month.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): React SPA with MSAL auth, rent payment via Stripe, maintenance request submission, lease document viewer, Django API, Yardi webhook adapter, PCI pre-assessment. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Staff management dashboard, building announcements, payment history analytics, Celery monitoring dashboard. Phase 3 (future): Mobile app, smart lock integration, amenity booking.
reference.development_guidelines: Python: black formatter, flake8, mypy strict mode. Django: DRF serializers for all API inputs; no direct model access in views. Field-level encryption applied to all payment_methods.stripe_payment_method_id fields via django-encrypted-fields decorator. PR requires security team review for any payment or auth code changes. No payment data in application logs.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Yardi API reliability — Yardi webhooks are documented inconsistently and sometimes drop events. Mitigation: Celery periodic reconciliation job polls Yardi work order status for open maintenance requests every 15 minutes as a fallback. Risk: PCI SAQ-D scope creep if additional payment methods added beyond Stripe-tokenized card and ACH. Mitigation: all payment method handling routed through Stripe SDK; no direct card data processing. Tradeoff: Django monolith chosen over microservices for MVP development speed; module boundaries maintained for future service extraction.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Yardi webhook HMAC secret obtained from property management team. Stripe ACH tokenization uses Stripe Financial Connections for bank account verification. Azure AD B2C selected over Entra External ID for tenant auth due to existing B2C tenant in org. Lease document SAS URL expiry set to 15 minutes to minimize exposure window.

reference.title: Construction Platform Microservices Decomposition Research Architecture v2
reference.executive_summary: A research prototype on GKE Autopilot decomposing a monolithic construction project management platform into three services (document-control-svc, scheduling-svc, cost-mgmt-svc) communicating over a Kafka 3.6 event bus. An orchestrator-based saga pattern manages cross-domain transactions with compensating events on failure. Per-service PostgreSQL schemas enforce data isolation boundaries. Prometheus metrics capture inter-service event latency, saga transaction duration, and eventual-consistency resolution lag for comparison against a monolith baseline under equivalent synthetic k6 load, producing an architecture decision record for the enterprise committee.
reference.architecture_overview: Three bounded-context services deployed on GKE Autopilot in a research namespace: document-control-svc (Go 1.22) owns document_revisions in document_schema; scheduling-svc (Go 1.22) owns schedule_baselines in scheduling_schema; cost-mgmt-svc (Python 3.12) owns cost_commitments in cost_schema. A dedicated saga-orchestrator-svc (Go 1.22) receives publish_document commands, coordinates the cross-service transaction by emitting document_revised to Kafka (key=doc_id, 3 partitions), awaiting scheduling_ack and cost_ack events within a 30-second window, and marking the saga complete or emitting revert_document_revision on timeout or nack. Kafka 3.6 on Confluent Cloud with Avro schemas managed in Schema Registry. consistency_audit_logs in a shared analytics schema records the resolution_lag_ms from event publish to downstream ack for each service, providing the primary research metric. Prometheus scrapes custom metrics from each service; Grafana renders saga timeline and consistency latency distribution dashboards. A Python 3.12 analytics script aggregates audit logs into weekly benchmark comparison reports against the monolith baseline.
reference.technology_stack: Services: Go 1.22 (document-control-svc, scheduling-svc, saga-orchestrator-svc), Python 3.12 (cost-mgmt-svc, analytics scripts). Event bus: Kafka 3.6 on Confluent Cloud with Avro schema serialisation and Schema Registry. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (four isolated schemas via separate PostgreSQL roles). Orchestration: GKE Autopilot with GitLab CI. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana on GKE, custom metrics per service. Load testing: k6 with synthetic construction project event scripts.
reference.functional_feature_map: Document Revision Publication: saga-orchestrator-svc receives publish_document command, document-control-svc writes document_revision and emits document_revised Avro event. Downstream Consistency Updates: scheduling-svc and cost-mgmt-svc consume document_revised and update task_dependencies and cost_code_mappings respectively, emitting ack events. Saga Rollback: orchestrator emits revert_document_revision if any downstream service times out or returns a nack event; services revert their local changes. Consistency Measurement: consistency_audit_logs records event publish_at and downstream service ack_at timestamps; resolution_lag_ms computed per saga step. Research Benchmarks: k6 load test runs against microservices cluster and monolith baseline.
reference.system_components: document-control-svc (Go, Kafka producer, document_schema owner), scheduling-svc (Go, Kafka consumer for document_revised, scheduling_schema owner), cost-mgmt-svc (Python, Kafka consumer for document_revised, cost_schema owner), saga-orchestrator-svc (Go, Kafka producer of document_revised and revert_document_revision, Kafka consumer of scheduling_ack and cost_ack, saga_schema owner), Kafka 3.6 Confluent Cloud (document_revised 3-partition topic, scheduling_ack, cost_ack, document_revised_dlq), Schema Registry (Avro schemas), PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (four schemas), Prometheus + Grafana, k6 load tester.
reference.workflows: Cross-Service Document Publish: Research engineer calls POST /simulate on saga-orchestrator-svc with a publish_document payload; orchestrator writes saga_transactions record with status pending, calls document-control-svc to create a document_revision, then produces an Avro document_revised event to Kafka with doc_id as partition key. scheduling-svc consumer group processes the event, updates schedule_baselines.task_dependencies to reference the new revision, writes a consistency_audit_log entry (service=scheduling, resolution_lag_ms=event.ack_at - event.publish_at), and emits scheduling_ack to Kafka. cost-mgmt-svc Python consumer similarly updates cost_commitments and emits cost_ack. Orchestrator receives both acks, marks saga_transaction completed. Saga Rollback: if cost-mgmt-svc consumer raises an exception (simulated via a chaos flag in /simulate payload), orchestrator receives no cost_ack within 30 seconds; it emits revert_document_revision event; document-control-svc and scheduling-svc consumers handle the revert by deleting the provisional changes; orchestrator marks saga_transaction rolled_back.
reference.data_model: document_schema.document_revisions (id UUID PK, doc_id UUID, revision_number INT, content_hash TEXT, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ, saga_id UUID). scheduling_schema.schedule_baselines (id UUID PK, project_id UUID, task_dependencies JSONB, last_sync_doc_revision_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cost_schema.cost_commitments (id UUID PK, project_id UUID, cost_code TEXT, amount NUMERIC, linked_doc_revision_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). saga_schema.saga_transactions (id UUID PK, command_type TEXT, status ENUM pending/completed/rolled_back, initiated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, participant_acks JSONB). analytics_schema.consistency_audit_logs (id UUID PK, saga_id UUID FK, service_name TEXT, event_type TEXT, event_publish_at TIMESTAMPTZ, service_ack_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolution_lag_ms INT). Kafka topics: document_revised (Avro: {doc_id: string, revision_id: string, content_hash: string, publish_at: long}), scheduling_ack and cost_ack (Avro: {saga_id: string, service: string, status: enum, ack_at: long}), document_revised_dlq.
reference.api_design: Minimal REST on each service for research tooling: GET /health (liveness check); GET /metrics (Prometheus exposition format); POST /simulate on saga-orchestrator-svc accepts {doc_id: string, inject_failure: bool} to trigger a publish_document saga with optional downstream failure injection for rollback testing. Python analytics script exposes no HTTP API; it queries consistency_audit_logs directly via SQL and outputs Markdown benchmark tables.
reference.security_and_compliance: Service-to-service JWT using a shared HS256 signing key distributed via Kubernetes Secret; each service validates the JWT on incoming REST calls. Kafka consumers use Confluent Cloud API keys stored in Kubernetes Secrets. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricts: document-control-svc can reach PostgreSQL document_schema port only; scheduling-svc to scheduling_schema; cost-mgmt-svc to cost_schema; saga-orchestrator-svc to saga_schema. No external ingress; research engineers access services via kubectl port-forward. Shared PostgreSQL cluster is a prototype simplification; finding will note that production requires separate databases per service to achieve true data isolation.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot with four Deployments and one ConfigMap per service. GitLab CI: go test, golangci-lint, pytest, Docker build, push to GCR, kubectl apply --dry-run in staging then apply in research namespace. Benchmark pipeline: GitLab scheduled job runs k6 against microservices and monolith endpoints weekly, stores JSON results in GCS, triggers Python analytics script to produce Markdown comparison report committed to the research repo.
reference.observability: Prometheus ServiceMonitors scrape /metrics from each service. Custom Prometheus histograms: document_event_publish_latency_seconds, saga_transaction_duration_seconds, consistency_resolution_lag_seconds labelled by service_name. Grafana dashboard panels: Saga Transaction Timeline (Gantt-style per saga step), Consistency Resolution Latency Distribution (histogram by service), DLQ Depth over time. Research output: weekly k6 benchmark comparison showing microservices p50/p99 latency vs monolith baseline under 100 concurrent synthetic document publish events.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Estimated USD 310/month: Confluent Cloud Kafka basic USD 60/month, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL db.f1-micro USD 80/month, GKE Autopilot 4 lightweight services USD 120/month, GCS for benchmark results USD 5/month, Grafana Cloud free tier. Under USD 500/month budget. Load test burst costs covered by budget buffer. Prototype is not designed for scale; GKE Autopilot handles the 4 services without manual node management.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (4 weeks): Scaffold three services, PostgreSQL schema setup, Kafka topics and Avro schemas, document_revised event flow end-to-end, consistency_audit_logs recording. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Saga orchestrator with rollback path, chaos failure injection via /simulate, k6 load test scripts and monolith baseline comparison runs, Grafana dashboards. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Analytics script producing comparative benchmark report, architecture decision record draft, operational complexity findings documented.
reference.development_guidelines: Go services use golangci-lint with errcheck and staticcheck. Python cost-mgmt-svc uses ruff and mypy. All Kafka consumer groups use explicit consumer group IDs to prevent partition assignment conflicts. Each service owns its schema migration files; no cross-schema migrations allowed. Research findings documented in /docs/findings at end of each sprint. Avro schema changes versioned in Schema Registry with backward-compatibility enforcement.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Confluent Cloud Kafka on basic tier has limited partition throughput; insufficient for production-scale document events but adequate for synthetic research load of 100 concurrent events. Risk: shared PostgreSQL cluster undermines true data isolation; findings must note this limitation explicitly. Tradeoff: orchestrator-based saga over choreography chosen because orchestrator state in saga_transactions table makes rollback paths observable and testable, which is more valuable for research insights than choreography's lower coupling. Risk: GKE Autopilot node provisioning latency inflates p99 latency measurements during cold-start periods; pre-warm jobs added to benchmark pipeline.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Orchestrator-based saga chosen over choreography for research observability: saga_transactions table makes every transaction step queryable. Avro with Schema Registry chosen over JSON for schema evolution research as an additional data point. Synthetic data seeded from anonymised production project snapshots; real project data not used. PostgreSQL shared cluster confirmed acceptable for prototype; production recommendation will specify separate databases per service.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to address the unresolved focus issue of the emergency access time-delay timer not surviving app restarts, while preserving all user-confirmed requirements and improving the architecture without introducing regressions. The plan includes a concrete architecture with modules, workflows, schemas, APIs, security, deployment, observability, roadmap, and developer guidance. fix_report.action_taken: Stored emergency_access_requests as rows in audit.db with requested_at DATETIME; on app start query all pending requests and evaluate (now - requested_at) >= approval_delay_hours
fix_report.expected_outcome: The emergency access approval window is enforced without relying on an in-memory timer, ensuring the time-delay security guarantee is maintained even if the app is closed between the request and the approval window expiry.

parsed.title: Hardened Desktop Credential Manager - Round 3
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan addresses the unresolved focus issue of the emergency access time-delay timer not surviving app restarts, while preserving all user-confirmed requirements and improving the architecture without introducing regressions. The plan includes a concrete architecture with modules, workflows, schemas, APIs, security, deployment, observability, roadmap, and developer guidance. It prioritizes unresolved critical and high-severity focus issues and improves weak areas identified by the auditor before adding extra design complexity.

reference.thinking_summary: Resolving ISS-001 by replacing the in-memory emergency access timer with a persistent timestamp-based approach: emergency_access_requests stored as rows in audit.db with requested_at and approval_delay_hours. On every app start, the vault_service queries pending emergency_access_requests and evaluates (now - requested_at) >= approval_delay_hours; if elapsed, the access is auto-approved or expired depending on owner response. This design is restart-safe because the state lives in audit.db, not in memory. Also specifying X25519 ECDH for team vault key handoff to close the key sharing gap from round 2.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced in-memory emergency access timer with persistent emergency_access_requests table in audit.db storing requested_at DATETIME and approval_delay_hours INT. On app start, vault_service queries SELECT * FROM emergency_access_requests WHERE status = 'pending' and evaluates (julianday('now') - julianday(requested_at)) * 24 >= approval_delay_hours. Expired pending requests auto-resolve. Notifications sent to vault owner via OS notification on app start if action required.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Emergency access approval window is enforced correctly even after multiple app restarts between request submission and expiry, preserving the security guarantee of the time-delay mechanism.

reference.title: Hardened Desktop Credential Manager
reference.executive_summary: A Tauri and Svelte desktop application with a Rust Axum IPC backend storing passwords, OTP seeds, and SSH keys in SQLCipher-encrypted local vaults. Master key is sealed to device TPM or Secure Enclave; fallback uses argon2id PIN stretching. Team vault sharing uses X25519 ECDH per-member key encryption. Emergency access requests persist across app restarts via audit.db timestamp tracking. Distributed as signed installers for macOS, Windows, and Linux.
reference.architecture_overview: Local-only desktop architecture: Tauri shell hosts Svelte TypeScript UI and communicates with a Rust Axum command handler via Tauri invoke() over a local Unix domain socket (macOS/Linux) or named pipe (Windows). vault_service module holds in-memory decrypted vault state and owns two SQLCipher databases: vault.db for credential entries and audit.db for append-only audit log and emergency access state. Master key derived from TPM Seal/Unseal (Secure Enclave on macOS, NCrypt TPM 2.0 on Windows) or argon2id from PIN as fallback. No network calls required for core vault; team sync uses direct E2EE blob exchange.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2.x, Svelte 4, TypeScript strict mode, Tauri plugin for biometric and OS keychain. Backend: Rust stable, Axum 0.7, SQLCipher via rusqlite with sqlcipher feature, ring crate (AES-256-GCM), argon2 crate (argon2id), x25519-dalek (team vault ECDH). Build: GitHub Actions cross-platform matrix. Packaging: tauri-bundler (macOS dmg universal, Windows NSIS, Linux AppImage and .deb). Crash reporting: Sentry Rust SDK with data scrubbing.
reference.functional_feature_map: Personal Vault: create/read/update/delete entries of type password, otp_seed, ssh_key with AES-256-GCM per-entry encryption. TOTP Generator: computes TOTP code from decrypted otp_seed with 30s countdown display; clipboard cleared after 30s. SSH Key Viewer: displays public key fingerprint; private key accessible only via copy-to-clipboard. Team Vault: share vault key to member using X25519 ECDH; member roles owner/editor/viewer enforced in Rust. Emergency Access: request access with configurable delay (24-72h); request persisted in audit.db; owner approves or denies on next app open within window. Audit Log Viewer: query audit_log_entries with hash chain integrity check.
reference.system_components: Tauri shell: window management, biometric plugin (macOS LocalAuthentication, Windows Windows Hello), OS notification. Svelte UI: VaultUnlock, EntryList, EntryDetail, TeamVaultPanel, EmergencyAccessPanel, AuditLogViewer. Rust vault_service: SQLCipher pool for vault.db and audit.db, in-memory decrypted entry cache (zeroed on lock), emergency access request evaluator running on startup. ring crate: AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt, HKDF for key derivation. argon2 crate: PIN stretching. x25519-dalek: team vault ECDH key agreement. audit_service: append-only writes with hash chain computation.
reference.workflows: Vault Unlock: Tauri invokes biometric OS dialog; on success calls CryptoKit/NCrypt to unseal master key from TPM; vault_service opens vault.db and audit.db with SQLCipher key derived from master key; verifies vault MAC; queries emergency_access_requests for pending items requiring owner action; emits OS notification if action needed. Emergency Access Request: requestor sends request with requested_at = now() stored in emergency_access_requests table (audit.db) with status=pending and approval_delay_hours; vault owner receives OS notification on next app open; if (now - requested_at) >= approval_delay_hours and owner has not denied, vault_service generates time-limited access token encrypted with requestor's public key. Team Vault Key Share: owner calls EmergencyAccessCommand::share; vault_service generates ephemeral X25519 keypair, derives shared_secret = ECDH(owner_private, member_public), encrypts vault_key with AES-256-GCM using shared_secret, stores encrypted_vault_key in access_grants.
reference.data_model: vault.db - vault_entries (id UUID PK, entry_type ENUM password/otp_seed/ssh_key, title VARCHAR, username VARCHAR, url VARCHAR, nonce BLOB 12 bytes, ciphertext BLOB, created_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME). otp_seeds (id UUID PK, entry_id FK, nonce BLOB, seed_encrypted BLOB, issuer VARCHAR, digits INT DEFAULT 6, period INT DEFAULT 30). ssh_key_pairs (id UUID PK, entry_id FK, nonce BLOB, private_key_encrypted BLOB, public_key TEXT, key_type ENUM ed25519/rsa4096, fingerprint CHAR 47). team_vaults (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, owner_identity VARCHAR, created_at DATETIME). access_grants (id UUID PK, vault_id FK, grantee_identity VARCHAR, role ENUM owner/editor/viewer, ecdh_wrapped_vault_key BLOB, granted_at DATETIME). vault_metadata (key VARCHAR PK, value TEXT) holding vault_mac and schema_version. audit.db - audit_log_entries (id UUID PK, action ENUM, entry_id UUID nullable, actor_identity VARCHAR, timestamp DATETIME, prev_hash CHAR 64, row_hash CHAR 64). emergency_access_requests (id UUID PK, requestor_identity VARCHAR, requestor_public_key BLOB, approval_delay_hours INT, requested_at DATETIME, status ENUM pending/approved/denied/expired, resolved_at DATETIME nullable).
reference.api_design: Tauri invoke commands - vault:unlock(pin_or_biometric) -> VaultStatus. vault:create_entry(entry_type, fields) -> EntryId. vault:read_entry(entry_id) -> DecryptedEntry (zeroed after use). vault:delete_entry(entry_id) -> void. team:share_vault(vault_id, member_identity, member_public_key, role) -> GrantId. team:revoke_member(grant_id) -> void. emergency:request_access(vault_id, requestor_public_key, delay_hours) -> RequestId. emergency:approve_access(request_id) -> EncryptedAccessToken. emergency:deny_access(request_id) -> void. audit:query_log(filters) -> AuditLogPage with chain_valid bool.
reference.security_and_compliance: NIST SP 800-132 compliance: master key derived via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 on PIN fallback with 600,000 iterations (FIPS 140-3 guidance). TPM path: Secure Enclave CryptoKit on macOS, NCrypt TPM 2.0 on Windows. Per-entry encryption: AES-256-GCM with unique 96-bit nonce per write. Vault MAC: HMAC-SHA256 over all (entry_id, updated_at) pairs; recomputed and verified on every vault open. Audit hash chain: row_hash = SHA256(prev_hash || action || entry_id || actor_identity || timestamp); chain verified on every audit.db open. Emergency access timer: persisted in audit.db; app-restart-safe. Team vault key sharing: X25519 ECDH shared secret -> AES-256-GCM encrypted vault key; owner private key never transmitted. Clipboard cleared after 30s. Screen capture protection on sensitive views via Tauri setContentProtection(true).
reference.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions matrix: [macos-14 (aarch64), macos-13 (x86_64)], [windows-2022], [ubuntu-22.04, ubuntu-22.04-arm64]. Steps: clippy lint, cargo test (unit + integration with in-memory SQLCipher), vitest for Svelte components, tauri build, lipo universal binary for macOS. Release tags trigger: Apple notarisation + staple (macOS), Azure Code Signing (Windows), GPG sign AppImage (Linux). Artifacts uploaded to GitHub Releases. Auto-update manifest signed with Tauri updater key pair.
reference.observability: Local audit log in audit.db with hash chain provides tamper-evident access history. Sentry Rust SDK for crash reports: DSN configured in Tauri conf; data scrubbing rules strip any string matching UUID patterns from stack frames. App version and OS version sent with crashes; no vault data. Structured tracing logs via tracing crate (info level) written to local log file with 30-day rotation.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost for core vault; local-only. GitHub Actions free tier covers cross-platform build matrix. Apple Developer Program ($99/yr) required for notarisation. Windows EV certificate for Authenticode (~$300/yr). SQLCipher handles up to 10,000 entries with sub-50ms query time on consumer hardware.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): personal vault unlock via TPM/PIN, password and OTP seed CRUD with AES-256-GCM, local audit.db with hash chain, auto-lock on 15min idle, signed installers for macOS and Windows. Phase 2 (6 weeks): SSH key storage, team vault sharing with X25519 ECDH, emergency access with persistent timer, team RBAC. Phase 3 (4 weeks): browser extension autofill, SSH agent integration, FIDO2 hardware key unlock factor.
reference.development_guidelines: Rust: clippy deny(warnings), cargo test with coverage via cargo-tarpaulin targeting 85% line coverage. Secrets never in Rust String type; use ZeroizeVec from zeroize crate for plaintext buffers. Svelte: TypeScript strict, vitest for component logic. No vault plaintext in Svelte stores; fetch on-demand via IPC. PR requires security team review for any change to vault_service or audit_service modules.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: TPM unavailable in VM environments; argon2id fallback with enforced PIN complexity mitigates brute-force. Risk: vault file loss without backup; mitigated by prompted portable export to encrypted USB. Tradeoff: SQLCipher over age-encrypted flat file chosen for relational query capability needed by audit log and team vault features. Risk: Apple notarisation delay blocking release; mitigated by pre-notarising release candidates before tag.
reference.open_questions_resolved: X25519 ECDH chosen over Signal double ratchet for team vault key sharing; double ratchet is over-engineered for vault key exchange which does not require forward secrecy on every message. Emergency access timer persistence chosen via audit.db rows rather than OS scheduled tasks to maintain single source of truth and audit trail. argon2id parameters set to m=65536, t=3, p=4 following OWASP recommendation for interactive logins.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to address the critical focus issues identified in the previous audit, ensuring compliance with COPPA and DSA while meeting the performance and scalability requirements. The plan will decompose the monolithic app into microservices, with a focus on optimizing feed ranking, upload processing, and social graph components. fix_report.action_taken: Pre-compute content embeddings at upload-complete time via Pub/Sub-triggered job; store in PostgreSQL pgvector; cache cosine similarity scores in Redis at 30s intervals, not per-request
fix_report.expected_outcome: Pre-computed embeddings will be available for feed ranking, reducing latency to meet the p99 <100ms requirement. Redis will cache the similarity scores for quick retrieval during feed ranking.

fix_report.action_taken: Add synchronous Cloud CDN cache invalidation to AdminService.TakedownVideo gRPC handler: call Cloud CDN API within the same transaction that updates video_post.status=taken_down; log cdn_purge_job_id to moderation audit trail for DSA evidence
fix_report.changed_sections: content-moderation service, deployment and operations, security
fix_report.expected_outcome: The CDN purge will be triggered immediately upon takedown, ensuring DSA compliance with a 60-second takedown propagation time. The purge job will be logged for DSA audit trail.

fix_report.action_taken: Add gRPC interceptor to engagement-collector that reads coppa_minor claim from Firebase JWT and drops the engagement event (returns Ack without persisting) for COPPA minor users
fix_report.expected_outcome: Engagement signals from COPPA minor users will be blocked server-side, complying with COPPA requirements and preventing the storage of sensitive data.

parsed.title: Short-Form Video Microservices - Round 2
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan addresses the critical focus issues from the previous audit, ensuring compliance with COPPA and DSA while meeting the performance and scalability requirements. The plan will decompose the monolithic app into microservices, with a focus on optimizing feed ranking, upload processing, and social graph components. The architecture will be designed to be implementation-grade, with a clear separation of concerns, robust security, and compliance with data protection regulations.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture will consist of a Flutter 3.22 mobile app frontend, Go 1.22 microservices backend, and a PostgreSQL/Cassandra/Redis data platform. The backend services will include feed-ranking, upload-processor, social-graph, engagement-collector, content-moderation, and admin-api. The plan will ensure that the feed-ranking service pre-computes content embeddings, the content-moderation service includes a synchronous CDN cache invalidation call, and the engagement-collector service checks the coppa_minor claim from Firebase JWT to block engagement signals from COPPA minor users.

technology_stack.frontend: Flutter 3.22 with Dart, Riverpod for state management, gRPC-Dart for backend communication
technology_stack.backend: Go 1.22, gRPC with protobuf, Pub/Sub for async events
technology_stack.data_platform: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension, Cassandra 4.1, Redis 7
technology_stack.hosting: GKE Autopilot on GCP, Cloud CDN for video delivery, Cloud Storage for uploaded and transcoded video segments
technology_stack.security: Firebase Auth with custom claims, Cloud CDN with Cloud Interconnect API, Datadog for monitoring and alerting

frontend: Vertical video feed with algorithmic ranking

workflows.feed_delivery: Fetch feed-ranking, pre-load top 3 videos, swipe triggers engagement event
workflows.upload_processing: Select clip, trim, add caption, POST to upload-processor, show transcoding progress
workflows.cdn_purge: Plato Flutter video player handles CDN purge, swapping to 'content unavailable' placeholder

security_and_compliance.moderation: Content hash fingerprinting + LLM moderation scoring
security_and_compliance.privacy_controls: GDPR right-to-erasure, COPPA data minimization, no behavioral ad targeting
security_and_compliance.compliance: DSA takedown propagation within 60s, transparency report

deployment_and_operations.hosting: GKE Autopilot on GCP, Cloud CDN for video delivery, Cloud Storage for video segments
deployment_and_operations.ci_cd: Cloud Build per service, Flutter CI, canary deploy with Istio VirtualService
deployment_and_operations.observability: Datadog APM, Datadog RUM, PagerDuty alerts

cost_and_scaling.compute: GKE Autopilot, Cloud Run, Cloud Storage

parsed.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Decomposition and core services setup. Phase 2: Feature rollout, including feed ranking, upload processing, and social graph. Phase 3: Live streaming, AR face filters, web viewer experience.
parsed.development_guidelines: Follow the service-team ownership model, with independent deployment cadence per microservice. Use Cloud Build CI per service repository.

risks: Pre-computation of LLM embeddings may increase initial resource usage
Synchronous CDN cache invalidation may impact user experience during takedown
COPPA minor engagement signal blocking may require additional user verification steps
tradeoffs: Improved feed latency at the cost of increased resource usage for embedding pre-computation
Immediate content takedown at the cost of potential user experience disruption
Stricter COPPA compliance may limit data collection options

open_questions_resolved: Live streaming architecture deferred to Phase 3

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve ISS-001 (LLM in request path) and ISS-002 (CDN purge absent). For ISS-001: embeddings are generated asynchronously by a Pub/Sub-triggered job at upload-complete time and stored in PostgreSQL pgvector; feed-ranking re-scores candidates every 30 seconds using pre-computed embeddings cached in Redis, never during a live GetFeed request. For ISS-002: AdminService.TakedownVideo handler calls Cloud CDN API synchronously within the gRPC handler before returning, logs cdn_purge_job_id to the moderation audit trail for DSA evidence. ISS-003 COPPA: engagement-collector gRPC interceptor reads coppa_minor claim and drops without persisting.

fix_report.action_taken: Moved LLM embedding computation from GetFeed request path to a Pub/Sub-triggered background job: on video_post.status=transcoded event, embedding-generator job calls Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5, stores 768-dim vector in video_posts.content_embedding (pgvector). Feed-ranking service re-scores top-K candidates every 30 seconds via a scheduled worker reading pgvector cosine similarity from PostgreSQL and writing ranked feed to Redis feed_cache:{viewer_id}. GetFeed handler reads only from Redis cache (<5ms), never from Vertex AI
fix_report.expected_outcome: GetFeed p99 latency drops from 200-400ms to <10ms for cached feed reads; LLM embedding freshness lag of up to 30 seconds is acceptable for feed ranking quality

fix_report.action_taken: Added synchronous Cloud CDN cache invalidation to AdminService.TakedownVideo gRPC handler: after updating video_post.status=taken_down in PostgreSQL, handler calls GCP Cloud CDN API cdn.CacheInvalidation.Create with pattern /videos/{video_id}/* before returning gRPC response; cdn_purge_job_id logged to moderation_audit_events table with timestamp; Pub/Sub event emitted post-purge for notification service to alert affected creator
fix_report.expected_outcome: CDN cache invalidation initiated within 1-2 seconds of moderator action; propagation completes in 10-30 seconds on Cloud CDN; DSA 60-second SLA met with buffer; cdn_purge_job_id provides audit evidence for DSA transparency reports

reference.title: Gen Z Short-Form Video Platform - Microservices Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A COPPA/GDPR/DSA-compliant Flutter 3.22 mobile app backed by Go 1.22 gRPC microservices on GKE Autopilot, decomposed from a scaling-bottlenecked monolith. The feed-ranking service delivers personalized vertical video feeds at p99 <100ms by reading pre-scored Redis caches refreshed every 30 seconds using Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 content embeddings computed asynchronously at upload time. Content moderation combines hash fingerprinting and LLM scoring; moderator takedowns synchronously invoke Cloud CDN cache invalidation within the gRPC handler for DSA 60-second propagation compliance. COPPA minor users have engagement signal collection blocked at the gRPC interceptor layer before any Cassandra write.
reference.architecture_overview: Flutter client connects via gRPC-Dart to a GKE Ingress routing to seven Go microservices: feed-ranking, upload-processor, social-graph, engagement-collector, content-moderation, admin-api, and notification. Feed delivery critical path: GetFeed reads Redis feed_cache:{viewer_id} (pre-scored every 30 seconds by a background feed-scorer worker) for sub-10ms response, bypassing Vertex AI entirely at request time. LLM embedding pipeline: upload-processor publishes upload_complete to Pub/Sub; embedding-generator service calls Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API, stores 768-dim vector in video_posts.content_embedding (PostgreSQL pgvector); feed-scorer worker runs cosine similarity queries against cached viewer interest embeddings every 30 seconds and writes results to Redis. CDN takedown path: TakedownVideo gRPC handler updates PostgreSQL video_post status, calls Cloud CDN CacheInvalidation API synchronously, logs cdn_purge_job_id to audit trail. Dual-region GKE (us-central1, europe-west1) with Cloud CDN fronting Cloud Storage video segments globally.
reference.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.22, Dart, Riverpod, gRPC-Dart, video_player package. Backend: Go 1.22, gRPC with protobuf, GCP Pub/Sub for async events, Firebase Admin SDK for JWT validation. Data: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension (creator_profiles, video_posts, social_edges), Cassandra 4.1 (engagement_signals, feed_rankings), Redis 7 (feed_cache, trending_topics). LLM: Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API (768-dim). Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot (us-central1, europe-west1), Cloud Build, Cloud CDN, Cloud Storage, Cloud Run for transcoding jobs. Observability: Datadog APM + RUM.
reference.functional_feature_map: Vertical Feed: Flutter PageView rendering HLS video posts pre-loaded from Redis feed cache, refreshed every 30s by LLM-assisted relevance scoring. Upload Pipeline: creator submits clip via gRPC UploadService, upload-processor stores raw video in Cloud Storage, triggers Cloud Run FFmpeg transcode job, publishes upload_complete event to Pub/Sub which triggers embedding generation. Social Graph: SocialService.Follow/Unfollow writes PostgreSQL social_edges; follower counts maintained with PostgreSQL counter tables. Engagement Collection: streaming gRPC EngagementService.RecordSignals batches like/share/watch_time events to Cassandra; COPPA interceptor drops events for coppa_minor users. Content Moderation: content-moderation service checks PhotoDNA hash + LLM moderation score at upload_complete, routes flagged content to moderation_queues. Moderator Admin Panel: Flutter admin tab (moderator role only) calls AdminService.TakedownVideo which purges CDN within 60 seconds. Trending Topics: Redis sorted set updated hourly by trending-scorer worker based on engagement velocity.
reference.system_components: Flutter app (iOS/Android): Riverpod providers, gRPC-Dart stubs, video_player with HLS. GKE Ingress (gRPC HTTP/2 passthrough). Go microservices: feed-ranking (Redis read + 30s scorer worker), upload-processor (gRPC + Pub/Sub publisher + Cloud Run job launcher), social-graph (PostgreSQL), engagement-collector (Cassandra + coppa_minor interceptor), content-moderation (Pub/Sub consumer, hash + LLM scoring), admin-api (PostgreSQL + Cloud CDN API caller), notification (Pub/Sub consumer + FCM). embedding-generator service (Pub/Sub consumer, Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 API, pgvector write). feed-scorer worker (30s cron, PostgreSQL pgvector cosine similarity, Redis write). PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector. Cassandra 4.1. Redis 7. Cloud Storage (raw video + HLS segments). Cloud CDN. Datadog APM agents on all services.
reference.workflows: Feed Delivery (Critical Path): Flutter calls FeedService.GetFeed via gRPC. feed-ranking handler reads Redis LIST feed_cache:{viewer_id} (pre-populated by feed-scorer worker). Returns top 20 video_post_ids with HLS manifest URLs signed for Cloud CDN. p99 < 10ms from Redis. Background Feed Scoring (every 30s): feed-scorer worker queries Cassandra engagement_signals for each active viewer (last 7 days), fetches top-500 candidate videos, runs pgvector cosine similarity query: SELECT video_id FROM video_posts ORDER BY content_embedding <=> $viewer_interest_embedding LIMIT 500, applies collaborative filter boost from Cassandra follow-graph signal, writes re-ranked list to Redis feed_cache:{viewer_id} with 60s TTL. Upload and Embedding Pipeline: Creator uploads clip via UploadService.StartUpload. upload-processor stores raw file in Cloud Storage, launches Cloud Run FFmpeg transcode job (HLS at 720p/1080p). On transcode complete, publishes {video_id, hls_manifest_url} to upload_complete Pub/Sub topic. embedding-generator consumes event, calls Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API with video thumbnail + caption text, stores 768-dim vector in video_posts.content_embedding via pgvector. content-moderation consumes same event, checks hash, runs LLM moderation score, routes to moderation_queues if score > 0.7. Moderator Takedown (DSA SLA): Moderator clicks takedown in Flutter admin panel. Flutter calls AdminService.TakedownVideo(video_id, reason). admin-api gRPC handler: UPDATE video_posts SET status='taken_down'; calls GCP Cloud CDN CacheInvalidation.Create API with url_map pattern /videos/{video_id}/*; receives cdn_purge_job_id; INSERTs moderation_audit_event row with cdn_purge_job_id and initiated_at timestamp; returns TakedownAck with cdn_purge_job_id to Flutter. Flutter shows confirmation with purge job ID. CDN propagation completes in 10-30s; total takedown propagation under 60s. Pub/Sub notification event emitted to notify creator.
reference.data_model: creator_profiles (id UUID PK, firebase_uid VARCHAR, username VARCHAR UNIQUE, region VARCHAR, age_verified BOOL DEFAULT false, coppa_minor BOOL DEFAULT false, follower_count INT DEFAULT 0, following_count INT DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMP) - PostgreSQL. video_posts (id UUID PK, creator_id UUID FK, caption TEXT, hls_manifest_url TEXT, thumbnail_url TEXT, duration_seconds FLOAT, status TEXT CHECK IN ('uploading','transcoding','active','taken_down'), content_embedding vector(768), moderation_score FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMP) - PostgreSQL with pgvector. social_edges (follower_id UUID, followee_id UUID, created_at TIMESTAMP, PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id)) - PostgreSQL. engagement_signals (viewer_id UUID, video_id UUID, signal_type TEXT, watch_time_ms INT, occurred_at TIMESTAMP, PRIMARY KEY (viewer_id, occurred_at)) - Cassandra MV partition. feed_rankings (viewer_id UUID, bucket_key TEXT, ranked_video_ids LIST<UUID>, scored_at TIMESTAMP) - Cassandra. moderation_queues (id UUID PK, video_id UUID FK, queue_type TEXT, assigned_moderator_id UUID, status TEXT, cdn_purge_job_id TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP) - PostgreSQL. trending_topics stored in Redis ZSET trending:topics with score = engagement_velocity_score.
reference.api_design: gRPC services (proto): FeedService.GetFeed(ViewerFeedRequest{viewer_id, page_token}) returns FeedResponse{video_posts[], next_page_token}. UploadService.StartUpload(UploadRequest{creator_id, duration_seconds, caption}) returns UploadSession{upload_url, session_id}. EngagementService.RecordSignals(stream EngagementEvent{viewer_id, video_id, signal_type, watch_time_ms}) returns Ack. SocialService.Follow(FollowRequest{follower_id, followee_id}) returns FollowAck. AdminService.TakedownVideo(TakedownRequest{video_id, moderator_id, reason}) returns TakedownAck{cdn_purge_job_id, initiated_at}. AdminService.GetModerationQueue(QueueRequest) returns ModerationQueueResponse. REST (admin panel DSA reporting): GET /admin/dsa-report?period=monthly (returns takedown counts and avg propagation times for DSA transparency report).
reference.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth custom claims: coppa_minor (set on DOB < 13 at registration), age_verified (parental consent received), moderator (granted by admin). gRPC interceptors on all services: JWT validation, and on engagement-collector: coppa_minor=true drops event without Cassandra write. COPPA: no behavioral signal collection for minors; watch-time not persisted; no ad targeting. GDPR: deletion-coordinator receives DELETE /users/:id, publishes user_delete_request to Pub/Sub; each service purges user rows within 72 hours; PostgreSQL social_edges and video_posts cascade on creator_profiles delete. DSA: TakedownVideo handler calls Cloud CDN CacheInvalidation synchronously; cdn_purge_job_id and timing logged to moderation_audit_events for quarterly DSA transparency report; GET /admin/dsa-report endpoint for regulatory submissions. Content hash fingerprinting: PhotoDNA hash checked at upload_complete before video becomes active.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Dual-region GKE Autopilot: us-central1 (global traffic default), europe-west1 (EU user affinity via Cloud Load Balancing geo-routing for GDPR residency). Each region deploys all Go microservices via Cloud Build. HPA on CPU + custom Datadog metrics: feed-ranking scales on feed_request_queue_depth, upload-processor scales on upload_queue_depth. Cloud Build pipeline per service: go test ./..., golangci-lint, ko build, kubectl apply with Workload Identity. Flutter CI: flutter test, flutter build appbundle --release, Firebase App Distribution for beta. Canary: Istio VirtualService 5% traffic split before full rollout. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo per service.
reference.observability: Datadog APM gRPC interceptor on all Go services with distributed trace propagation via gRPC metadata. Key traces: GetFeed (Redis read latency), TakedownVideo (CDN purge latency), embedding-generator (Vertex AI call duration). Custom Datadog metrics: feed_cache_hit_rate (target >99%), embedding_generation_lag_seconds (p95 < 120s post-upload), cdn_purge_latency_seconds (p99 < 55s for DSA SLA), engagement_signal_drop_rate_coppa (count of COPPA-dropped signals for audit). PagerDuty alerts: feed_cache_hit_rate <95%, cdn_purge_p99 >55s, upload_failure_rate >3%. Datadog RUM in Flutter for crash rate and video player error rate.
reference.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot scales to zero for low-traffic services; estimated $2,400/month at 500K daily active users: GKE compute ($900), Cloud CDN egress for 5PB/month video ($1,100), PostgreSQL CloudSQL ($250), Cassandra (self-managed GKE StatefulSet, $150). Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API: ~$0.0001 per 1K tokens, negligible for upload volume. Redis cluster ($120/month). Datadog ($200/month at this scale). Feed-scorer worker cost negligible (Go goroutine pool, <1 CPU continuous).
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (12 weeks): Decompose monolith feed-ranking and upload-processor services; Flutter gRPC client with Riverpod; Cassandra engagement_signals; Redis feed cache with 30s scorer; Vertex AI embedding pipeline (async); Cloud CDN with synchronous TakedownVideo purge; COPPA engagement interceptor; GDPR deletion coordinator; Datadog APM. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Social graph service with follower/following feed boost; trending topics Redis ZSET scorer; push notifications via FCM; content-moderation LLM scoring with PhotoDNA; DSA transparency report endpoint. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Ad placement service (excluding COPPA minors); creator analytics dashboard; live streaming architecture; AR face filters via TensorFlow Lite on-device.
reference.development_guidelines: Proto-first API design: all service interfaces defined in .proto files in the shared proto repository before implementation. Go: golangci-lint, unit tests for all business logic (80% coverage), gRPC interceptor tests with mock JWT tokens including coppa_minor=true test cases. Flutter: flutter_test for widget tests, integration tests for feed load and upload flow. DSA audit trail: every TakedownVideo must include cdn_purge_job_id in response - tested in admin-api integration tests. Each service deployed independently; no cross-service database writes (services own their data stores). Datadog trace annotations on all gRPC handlers with feed_cache_hit span tag.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: 30-second feed re-scoring lag means newly uploaded viral content appears in feeds up to 30 seconds after embedding generation. Mitigation: hot-content detector publishes forced re-score event when video engagement velocity exceeds threshold. Risk: PostgreSQL pgvector cosine similarity query over 10M video_posts at 30s interval becoming a bottleneck. Mitigation: partition pgvector queries by content category tag; pre-filter candidates by creator follow graph before similarity scan. Tradeoff: synchronous CDN purge in TakedownVideo vs. async job - chosen synchronous to meet DSA 60s SLA guarantee; adds ~2s to moderator API response time which is acceptable. Risk: Cassandra fan-out read for feed-scorer on viral videos followed by millions: Mitigation: cap fan-out at top-500 followed creators per viewer; use Cassandra materialized view for pre-aggregated engagement counts.
reference.open_questions_resolved: LLM embedding computed asynchronously at upload time, not at request time; GetFeed reads only Redis cache. CDN purge called synchronously in TakedownVideo gRPC handler with cdn_purge_job_id logged for DSA evidence. COPPA engagement blocking enforced via gRPC interceptor in engagement-collector service. pgvector in PostgreSQL chosen over Pinecone/Weaviate to reduce external service dependencies at current scale.

parsed.title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI v2
parsed.executive_summary: The revised architecture plan addresses the unresolved high-severity focus issue of rate limiter inaccuracy at high EPS by implementing per-goroutine rate limiting using golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter and enhances security by implementing a YAML startup scan for PEM headers and SASL password patterns. The dry-run flag for YAML validation without connecting to Kafka/Redis is also included to prevent accidental cluster saturation.

reference.thinking_summary: Round 2 fixes the shared Ticker rate limiter (ISS-001) with per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter and adds YAML startup credential scan (ISS-002). The flawed plan_quality introduces an intentional inconsistency: the workflows section describes a web dashboard contradicting the cli_tool project class.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced shared time.Ticker with per-goroutine rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/workerEPS), 1) where workerEPS=totalEPS/numWorkers. Each goroutine calls limiter.Wait(ctx) before producing, yielding accurate per-goroutine rate control. Dropped-message counter now only increments on Kafka produce error or context deadline.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Accurate EPS delivery at target rates up to 50000 events/second; dropped-message count reflects actual Kafka failures not rate limiter artifacts

fix_report.action_taken: Added validateConfigCredentials(rawYAML []byte) function called at startup before viper parsing. Scans raw YAML bytes for '-----BEGIN', 'password:', 'sasl_password:', 'auth_token:'. Any match triggers log.Fatal with matched line number.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Startup scan prevents engineers running benchmarks with credential-containing YAML configs

reference.title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI v2
reference.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool enabling sports data platform engineers to benchmark Kafka ingestion pipelines by simulating burst traffic at configurable events-per-second rates. Per-goroutine rate limiters using golang.org/x/time/rate deliver accurate EPS targeting, while an HDRHistogram records p50/p95/p99 latency. Results persist to a local DuckDB database for historical comparison and optionally push to Prometheus pushgateway. Startup YAML validation scans for credential patterns and aborts with a fatal error on detection.
reference.architecture_overview: cobra CLI with run, compare, and config validate subcommands. BenchmarkRunner spawns N producer goroutines using a semaphore-bounded pool; each goroutine holds an independent golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter initialized at totalEPS/numWorkers, calling limiter.Wait(ctx) before each produce call. An HDRHistogram recorder goroutine receives latency samples via unbuffered channel. DuckDB via CGo stores run results. No network server; pure CLI binary.
reference.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. CLI: cobra and viper. Rate limiting: golang.org/x/time/rate per-goroutine Limiter. Kafka: confluent-kafka-go with librdkafka. Histograms: hdrhistogram-go. Result storage: DuckDB via marcboeker/go-duckdb. Logging: zerolog JSON. CI: GitHub Actions with goreleaser for multi-platform releases. pprof profiling endpoint exposed via net/http on --pprof-port flag for heap and goroutine dump capture during high-throughput benchmark runs. go test -benchmem tracks allocations per message to detect GC pressure regressions in hot-path serialisation code.
reference.functional_feature_map: sports-bench run: load and validate YAML config, scan for credential patterns, spawn N rate-limited producer goroutines, collect HDR latency samples, print p50/p95/p99 report, write JSON result, append to DuckDB. sports-bench compare: SQL query DuckDB for two run IDs, render side-by-side diff table. sports-bench config validate: parse YAML, apply schema and credential scan, print resolved config or error. --dry-run: validate config without connecting to cluster.
reference.system_components: BenchmarkRunner (goroutine pool orchestrator), ProducerGoroutine (per-worker Limiter and confluent-kafka-go producer), LatencyRecorder (HDRHistogram via channel), DropCounter (atomic counter), ResultWriter (DuckDB append and JSON file), ConfigLoader (viper YAML and validateConfigCredentials scan), PrometheusExporter (push to pushgateway on completion).
reference.workflows: Benchmark Execution: Engineer runs sports-bench run --config match_day_feed.yaml --eps 5000 --duration 60 --workers 20; ConfigLoader reads YAML and scans for credential patterns (fatal on match); BenchmarkRunner initializes 20 ProducerGoroutines each with rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/250), 1); goroutines call limiter.Wait(ctx) then produce to Kafka; LatencyRecorder collects percentiles; after 60 seconds SIGALRM triggers graceful drain; HDRHistogram printed and JSON written. INTENTIONAL FLAW: A web dashboard is available at http://localhost:8080 after running sports-bench serve to view benchmark history — this contradicts the cli_tool project class and local_only exposure.
reference.data_model: benchmark_runs (run_id VARCHAR PK, config_file_hash, target_type, eps_target INT, worker_count INT, duration_seconds INT, started_at TIMESTAMP, total_messages_sent BIGINT, dropped_messages BIGINT, p50_latency_us DOUBLE, p95_latency_us DOUBLE, p99_latency_us DOUBLE, max_latency_us DOUBLE, throughput_msg_per_sec DOUBLE). feed_configurations (config_hash VARCHAR PK, config_yaml TEXT, parsed_at TIMESTAMP). throughput_samples (run_id VARCHAR, sample_second INT, messages_in_window BIGINT). latency_histograms (run_id VARCHAR, percentile_bucket DOUBLE, count BIGINT). All in ~/.sports-bench/results.duckdb.
reference.api_design: CLI interface only. sports-bench run [flags]: --config, --target kafka/redis-streams, --eps, --duration, --workers, --dry-run, --push-gateway, --verbose. sports-bench compare --run-id (repeatable). sports-bench config validate --config. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 config error, 2 cluster connection error, 3 credential detection error.
reference.security_and_compliance: Kafka credentials via KAFKA_BROKERS, KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH, KAFKA_SASL_USERNAME, KAFKA_SASL_PASSWORD env vars only. Redis AUTH via REDIS_AUTH. validateConfigCredentials scans for '-----BEGIN', 'password:', 'sasl_password:', 'auth_token:' — fatal on match with line number. Kafka TLS via security.protocol SASL_SSL. Prometheus pushgateway URL validated for localhost or RFC1918 range.
reference.deployment_and_operations: goreleaser: linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64. CGo enabled; librdkafka pre-installed in GitHub Actions matrix. PR CI: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test -race. Release on git tag: goreleaser release --clean, GitHub Release with checksums. go install supported via module proxy. GitHub Actions matrix builds across linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64 with CGO_ENABLED=1 and pre-installed librdkafka via apt/brew/choco. Release checksums verified against SLSA provenance attestation generated by goreleaser's --provenance flag for supply-chain integrity.
reference.observability: zerolog JSON to stderr on --verbose with run_id, worker_id, eps_target, total_sent, dropped, p50_us, p95_us, p99_us. Prometheus metrics on --push-gateway completion: sports_bench_messages_total, sports_bench_dropped_total, sports_bench_p99_latency_us, sports_bench_throughput_mps labeled by run_id. DuckDB result file includes a run_metadata table (run_id, kafka_broker, topic, target_eps, duration_s, cli_version) for longitudinal regression analysis across benchmark versions using a simple SELECT with JOIN.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — local binary. GitHub Actions free tier. goreleaser OSS tier. DuckDB file grows at ~1KB per benchmark run. DuckDB file stored on local NVMe for maximum write throughput during high-frequency benchmark runs; results exported to CSV for sharing. No cloud egress costs; self-hosted Kafka required — tool assumes engineer provides their own broker.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (v1, complete): Kafka target, shared Ticker (known-flawed), DuckDB, JSON output. Phase 2 (v2, current): Per-goroutine rate.Limiter, validateConfigCredentials, --dry-run, goreleaser multi-platform release. Phase 3: Redis Streams adapter, HTML report from DuckDB, GitHub Actions step summary mode.
reference.development_guidelines: Go modules, no global state. gosec in CI with zero high-severity findings. Rate limiter accuracy regression test: assert p99 EPS error <5% at 10000 EPS with 50 workers. Credential scan unit test: assert validateConfigCredentials returns error on YAML containing 'password: secret123'.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: confluent-kafka-go CGo complicates cross-compilation — mitigated by platform-native GitHub Actions runners. Risk: HDRHistogram memory grows with very long runs — max_duration_seconds: 3600 cap. Tradeoff: DuckDB CGo over pure-Go SQLite — DuckDB chosen for SQL aggregation power on histogram data.
reference.open_questions_resolved: golang.org/x/time/rate chosen over custom token bucket — standard library, well-tested. DuckDB confirmed over pure-Go SQLite for compare subcommand aggregation. HDRHistogram channel-based recorder avoids contention on hot produce path.

The focus is on addressing the current focus issues, preserving all user-confirmed requirements, and improving the architecture without introducing regressions.       "issue_id": "1",

reference.thinking_summary: This is a first-pass plan for a minimalist artisan bakery static site. The critical design decisions are: Eleventy with YAML data files to meet the self-service baker update constraint, image optimization via eleventy-img to meet the 2-second mobile load constraint on a gallery-heavy site, and Netlify Forms to eliminate all server-side form infrastructure. The biggest technical risk is unoptimized imagery; the plan must address this explicitly.
reference.title: Artisan Bakery Static Website Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A zero-server static website for a boutique artisan bakery built with Eleventy 3.x and deployed to Netlify free tier. The baker updates daily product availability by editing a YAML file in GitHub, triggering an automated site rebuild. The photo gallery is optimized at build time for sub-2-second mobile loads. Wholesale inquiry submissions route via Netlify Forms directly to the baker's inbox with no backend infrastructure required.
reference.architecture_overview: Fully static Eleventy site: HTML generated at build time from Nunjucks templates and YAML data sources. No client-side JavaScript except a deferred Plausible Analytics snippet. Daily availability data lives in _data/availability.yaml, edited by the baker in GitHub and triggering a Netlify redeploy via webhook. The photo gallery is processed at build time via eleventy-img, generating WebP variants at three responsive breakpoints. Netlify serves the static output globally via its CDN with automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Eleventy 3.x, Nunjucks templating, custom CSS with CSS custom properties. Image optimization: eleventy-img plugin (WebP output, responsive srcset). Forms: Netlify Forms with Akismet spam filtering. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cookieless, GDPR-compliant). Hosting: Netlify free tier with custom domain. CI: GitHub Actions (YAML lint, image size validation, Eleventy build check). Version control: GitHub.
reference.functional_feature_map: Daily Availability Page: renders product_listings from _data/availability.yaml sorted by category (bread, pastry, cake, seasonal); each item shows name, quantity available, and price display. Baker Story: static HTML/CSS page with baker biography, years of craft, and portrait photo. Photo Gallery: responsive CSS grid of baked goods images processed via eleventy-img with lazy loading; filtered by category. Wholesale Inquiry: Netlify Forms-powered contact form capturing business name, contact name, email, and inquiry text, submitted to baker inbox with spam filtering. Home Page: hero with daily availability summary showing today's featured items plus gallery preview grid.
reference.system_components: Netlify CDN (global edge delivery of static HTML, CSS, and optimized images). GitHub repository (source of truth for all content, templates, and YAML data). eleventy-img (build-time image processing pipeline). Netlify Forms (form submission handler with Akismet spam filtering, email notification to baker). Plausible Analytics (cookieless page view tracking hosted on Plausible's CDN). GitHub Actions (pre-merge validation: YAML lint, image size check, Eleventy build smoke test).
reference.workflows: Daily Availability Update: Baker opens _data/availability.yaml in GitHub web editor, updates product quantities and availability notes, commits to main branch → Netlify detects push, triggers Eleventy build (approx 20-30 seconds) → updated availability page live globally via Netlify CDN. Wholesale Inquiry Flow: Prospective wholesale buyer fills /wholesale form → Netlify Forms validates honeypot field and Akismet check → submission forwarded to baker's email with full form contents → baker replies directly; no data persists beyond Netlify Forms dashboard (baker clears periodically). Photo Gallery Update: Baker (or developer) adds optimized source images to src/assets/gallery/, commits to main → eleventy-img processes new images at build time into WebP variants → gallery page updated on next deploy.
reference.data_model: product_listings (YAML: name STRING, category ENUM bread/pastry/cake/seasonal, available_qty INTEGER, price_display STRING, notes STRING optional). daily_availability (YAML: published_date DATE, items ARRAY of product_listing references). gallery_images (filesystem metadata: filename, alt_text STRING, category ENUM bread/pastry/cake/seasonal, display_order INTEGER). baker_profiles (YAML: baker_name, bio_text, years_baking INTEGER, portrait_filename). wholesale_inquiry_submissions (Netlify Forms: contact_name, business_name, email, product_interest, inquiry_text — stored in Netlify dashboard only, not in repository or self-managed storage).
reference.api_design: No self-hosted API. External integrations only: Netlify Forms POST https://www.netlify.com/form-handler (browser-native form action, no JavaScript required). Plausible Analytics script GET https://plausible.io/js/script.js (deferred). Netlify Build Webhook: Netlify listens on main branch push events from GitHub to trigger rebuilds. GitHub Actions invokes Eleventy CLI (npx @11ty/eleventy --dry-run) for build validation.
reference.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify with auto-renewed Let's Encrypt certificate. CSP header via netlify.toml: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://plausible.io; img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; frame-ancestors 'none'. Netlify Forms spam filtering via Akismet integration plus HTML honeypot field. Plausible is cookieless — no GDPR consent banner required. No cookies set by the site. YAML data files contain no personal information — only product names and availability counts.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Netlify build command: npx @11ty/eleventy. Publish directory: _site. Build triggered on push to main branch and on Netlify Forms integration events. Deploy previews automatically generated for all open pull requests with a unique URL shared in PR comments. Baker workflow: edit availability.yaml via GitHub web UI → commit to main → site rebuilds in ~25 seconds → live. Rollback: Netlify one-click rollback to any previous successful deploy from the Netlify deploy history dashboard. Custom domain: configured via Netlify DNS or external DNS CNAME to Netlify's load balancer.
reference.observability: Plausible Analytics dashboard showing daily unique visitors, top pages (availability, gallery, wholesale), and referral sources (Instagram, Google, direct). Netlify deploy notifications via email on build failure. GitHub Actions status checks visible in PR view. No APM or error tracking required for a zero-JavaScript static site. Netlify Forms dashboard shows submission count and allows baker to review and delete stored submissions.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Total monthly cost: $0. Netlify free tier includes 100GB bandwidth, 300 build minutes, 100 form submissions/month — all sufficient for a local artisan bakery's expected traffic. Plausible Analytics: $9/month for the paid plan if the baker wants more than 30-day data retention (optional upgrade). Custom domain registration ~$15/year via Namecheap or Google Domains. If traffic grows beyond Netlify free tier limits, Netlify Pro ($19/month) provides 400GB bandwidth.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Launch (1 sprint, 5 days): Eleventy project setup with Nunjucks layouts, YAML data files for availability and baker profile, gallery with eleventy-img optimization, wholesale inquiry form via Netlify Forms, Plausible Analytics integration, CSP headers via netlify.toml, GitHub Actions CI, deploy to Netlify with custom domain. No phase 2 required for MVP — the full feature set is the initial delivery. Post-launch enhancements (if requested): Instagram embed, pre-order YAML form, seasonal menu archive page — all additive static content changes.
reference.development_guidelines: All product and availability data in YAML files — no hardcoded content in Nunjucks templates. Image optimization required via eleventy-img; raw img tags forbidden. CSS custom properties for bakery brand colors defined in :root to enable easy rebranding. GitHub branch protection on main: require PR review from developer before baker-initiated commits trigger production deploy. YAML lint (yamllint) enforced in GitHub Actions. No npm packages with node_modules committed to repository.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Baker commits invalid YAML syntax causing a failed build. Mitigation: GitHub Actions YAML lint step catches errors before merge; Netlify atomic deploys mean the previous version stays live on build failure. Risk: Unoptimized source images committed to repository bloating clone size. Mitigation: GitHub Actions checks that no source image in src/assets/ exceeds 3MB; eleventy-img handles all output optimization. Tradeoff: YAML-in-GitHub update workflow requires baker to have basic GitHub familiarity. Accepted because alternative (CMS) adds cost and complexity that is not justified for this scope.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Eleventy over Hugo for more familiar Nunjucks templating for the developer. Chose Plausible over GA4 to avoid GDPR cookie consent banner complexity on a simple bakery site. Confirmed Netlify Forms over Formspree for tighter Netlify hosting integration. No CMS needed — YAML data files are sufficient for this content volume.

These resolutions ensure that the architecture can handle the complexities of device posture evaluation and secure network communication without relying on unspecified or potentially problematic implementations.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified EDR detection for Windows using WMI Win32_Service query for known EDR service names and for macOS using launchctl list filtered against EDR process name list.
