reference.thinking_summary: Resolving ISS-002 by adding server-side reCAPTCHA v3 token verification in the POST /api/inquiry route via Google siteverify API before any Salesforce forwarding. reCAPTCHA v3 script loaded with IntersectionObserver only when inquiry form enters viewport to protect Lighthouse score. Also specifying Salesforce Lead field mapping to unblock the CRM team's configuration. The landing_page class means no persistent backend; the API route is a thin serverless function.

fix_report.action_taken: POST /api/inquiry now calls https://www.google.com/recaptcha/api/siteverify with RECAPTCHA_SECRET_KEY Vercel environment variable and submitted token; requests with score below 0.5 silently return 200 without Salesforce forward (bot feedback prevention); RECAPTCHA_SECRET_KEY stored in Vercel encrypted environment variables never exposed to client. reCAPTCHA v3 script loaded with async defer via IntersectionObserver triggered when inquiry section enters viewport, protecting Lighthouse performance score.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Bot form submissions blocked server-side; Lighthouse score maintained >= 95 with deferred reCAPTCHA load; no exploitable bot feedback channel via error responses

reference.title: Pumped Hydro Investment Landing Page - v2 Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static landing page presenting the 800 MW Mountain Creek Pumped Hydro Storage project to institutional energy infrastructure investors. Features a construction timeline visualization driven by YAML milestone config, technical specifications, team profiles, and an investor inquiry form with server-side reCAPTCHA v3 verification and Salesforce Lead API integration. Deployed to Vercel Edge; Plausible cookieless analytics tracks form conversion and PDF download events.
reference.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 statically generated site deployed to Vercel Edge. All page content generated at build time from Zod-validated YAML config files (milestones.yaml, project_specs.yaml) and static assets. Single serverless API route POST /api/inquiry handles form submissions: validates reCAPTCHA token server-side via Google siteverify API, applies IP-based rate limiting via Vercel Edge middleware token bucket backed by Vercel KV, validates form body with Zod InquirySchema, then maps and forwards validated lead data to Salesforce Lead API. No persistent backend service; no database; no server-side sessions. Vercel handles HTTPS, CDN distribution, and automatic preview deployments on PR branches.
reference.technology_stack: Next.js 14 with TypeScript (static generation via getStaticProps). Tailwind CSS for responsive styling. Framer Motion for timeline animation. Plausible Analytics self-proxied via Next.js rewrites. reCAPTCHA v3 with async defer and IntersectionObserver load. Zod for form and YAML schema validation. js-yaml for YAML parsing at build time. Vercel Edge runtime for API route and middleware. Vercel KV for rate limit token bucket state. GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI and axe-core for CI.
reference.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: project name, 800 MW and 8-hour storage highlights, primary CTA scrolling to inquiry form. Project Specifications: capacity_mw, storage_hours, annual_generation_gwh, reservoir details from project_specs.yaml. Construction Timeline: animated horizontal timeline from milestones.yaml with status indicators and date labels. Team Profiles: static cards from team_profiles.yaml. Investment Thesis: narrative section with downloadable project summary PDF tracked as Plausible goal event PDF_Download. Investor Inquiry Form: name, email, organization, interest description, honeypot field, server-side reCAPTCHA v3, Salesforce Lead API.
reference.system_components: Next.js static pages: index page with all sections as scroll-linked components. Serverless API route pages/api/inquiry.ts: reCAPTCHA siteverify, Zod InquirySchema validation, honeypot check, Salesforce Lead API POST with field mapping. Vercel Edge middleware middleware.ts: rate limit check using Vercel KV token bucket (5 POSTs per IP per 10 minutes). YAML config loader lib/config.ts: reads and validates milestones.yaml and project_specs.yaml against Zod schemas in getStaticProps. Plausible Analytics: self-proxied via Next.js rewrites to /p/js/script.js to prevent ad-blocker interference. reCAPTCHA v3: IntersectionObserver loads script only when inquiry section enters viewport.
reference.workflows: Investor Inquiry Submission: investor fills form and submits; Next.js client validates required fields; reCAPTCHA v3 executes and appends token to POST body; POST /api/inquiry sent; Vercel Edge middleware checks rate limit token bucket in Vercel KV; API route calls Google siteverify with RECAPTCHA_SECRET_KEY; if score >= 0.5, validates body with Zod InquirySchema, maps to Salesforce Lead fields (FirstName+LastName from name, Email, Company from organization, Description from investment_interest_text, LeadSource='Mountain_Creek_Landing_2026', Campaign_Tag__c='series_a_2026'), POSTs to Salesforce Lead API with Bearer token; returns {success: true} regardless of Salesforce outcome to prevent information leakage. Milestone Update: project team updates milestones.yaml via GitHub PR; Vercel creates preview deployment with updated timeline; PM reviews at preview URL; PR merged to main; production deployment live within 2 minutes.
reference.data_model: milestones.yaml: [{id: str, label: str, date: str ISO8601, status: 'completed'|'in_progress'|'upcoming', description: str}] validated by MilestoneSchema Zod. project_specs.yaml: {capacity_mw: number, storage_hours: number, reservoir_capacity_mwh: number, upper_reservoir_volume_af: number, head_elevation_ft: number, turbine_units: number, expected_annual_generation_gwh: number, permitting_status: str, target_cod: str, project_name: str, location: str} validated by ProjectSpecsSchema Zod. team_profiles.yaml: [{name, title, bio, photo_filename}]. No database. Salesforce Lead fields: FirstName, LastName, Email, Company, Description, LeadSource, Campaign_Tag__c.
reference.api_design: POST /api/inquiry request body: {name: string, email: string, organization: string, investment_interest_text: string, recaptcha_token: string, hp_field: string (honeypot, must be empty)}. Responses: 200 {success: true} always returned regardless of outcome; 429 returned by Vercel Edge middleware for rate limit exceeded before reaching route. Internal logic: honeypot non-empty returns 200 without forward; reCAPTCHA score < 0.5 logs score and returns 200; Salesforce API error logs payload to Vercel log drain and returns 200. Plausible goal events: PDF_Download and Inquiry_Submitted tracked via plausible custom events.
reference.security_and_compliance: Server-side reCAPTCHA v3 verification via Google siteverify API; RECAPTCHA_SECRET_KEY in Vercel encrypted environment variables. Honeypot field hp_field validated server-side; non-empty returns 200 silently. Rate limiting via Vercel Edge middleware token bucket in Vercel KV: 5 POST /api/inquiry per IP per 10 minutes. Salesforce access token stored in SALESFORCE_ACCESS_TOKEN Vercel environment variable; never exposed to client bundle. HTTPS enforced by Vercel; HSTS and CSP headers in next.config.js headers config: script-src 'self' www.google.com/recaptcha plausible.io 'nonce-{build_nonce}', img-src 'self' data:, frame-src www.google.com. No cookies; Plausible is cookieless.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project linked to GitHub; automatic preview on PR push; production deploy on main push. GitHub Actions: npm ci, next build, Lighthouse CI performance >= 95, axe-core WCAG 2.1 AA check. Custom domain with Vercel-managed SSL. Vercel KV on Hobby tier sufficient for landing page rate limit traffic. Rollback: Vercel dashboard instant rollback to any previous deployment retained 30 days.
reference.observability: Plausible Analytics goals: Inquiry_Submitted (POST /api/inquiry success callback), PDF_Download (onclick on project summary link), CTA_Click (hero button). Plausible dashboard: page views, inquiry conversion rate, referrer sources (LinkedIn, direct, investor newsletters). Vercel deployment logs: API route outcomes logged via console.error with structured JSON including Salesforce API response code. No APM needed; Vercel Edge function invocation metrics available in Vercel dashboard.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Approximately $20/month: Vercel Pro $20 for custom domain, team members, Vercel KV for rate limiting. Plausible Analytics $9/month up to 10K pageviews. Salesforce Lead API within existing CRM subscription. GitHub Actions free tier for CI. Scales automatically via Vercel Edge CDN during investor campaign traffic spikes.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (5 weeks): design and copy finalization (weeks 1-2), Next.js component build with YAML config and Zod validation (weeks 2-4), server-side reCAPTCHA and Salesforce Lead API integration, Lighthouse and axe-core CI, Vercel production launch for Series A campaign. Phase 2 (4 weeks after Series A close): password-protected investor data room via Next.js middleware auth, multilingual support (Japanese, Korean) via next-i18next, construction progress photo gallery with S3 source.
reference.development_guidelines: reCAPTCHA secret key and Salesforce access token must never appear in client-side code or console logs; enforced via pre-commit secret scan hook. YAML config changes require Zod schema validation to pass in CI before merge. Lighthouse CI gate blocks merge if performance score drops below 95. axe-core accessibility checks block merge on WCAG 2.1 AA violations. All user-visible strings in en.json locale file for future i18n compatibility.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: reCAPTCHA v3 score thresholds may block legitimate investors submitting from corporate proxy networks with atypically low behavioral scores. Mitigation: log borderline scores (0.4-0.5) without blocking during first week; adjust threshold based on observed investor traffic patterns. Risk: Salesforce API rate limits at 100 calls/minute on standard edition may cause inquiry drops during campaign launch spike. Mitigation: silent 200 response with Vercel log drain backup allows manual CRM entry for any missed leads. Tradeoff: returning 200 on all form outcomes prevents bot feedback but requires active monitoring of Vercel logs for genuine Salesforce integration failures.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Vercel KV over Redis for rate limiting due to Vercel-native integration simplicity for landing page traffic. Selected reCAPTCHA v3 over hCaptcha for Salesforce form tracking compatibility. Salesforce Lead API chosen over Web-to-Lead form to enable custom field mapping and campaign source tagging for Series A attribution.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to address the current focus issues by prioritizing unresolved critical and high-severity issues, preserving all user-confirmed requirements, and improving the architecture without introducing regressions. The plan focuses on resolving the unresolved issue of OAuth token expiry not being surfaced to the user before marketplace API failures, which is a medium-severity issue identified in the latest round. fix_report.action_taken: Added token expiry monitoring with in-app warning at 24 hours before expiry and automatic refresh attempt before each poll cycle
fix_report.expected_outcome: The app will now detect and warn users about expiring OAuth tokens 24 hours before they expire, and attempt an automatic refresh before each poll cycle. This will prevent silent marketplace API failures and improve user experience.

parsed.title: Marketplace Reseller Desktop Repricing Tool v2: Enhanced OAuth Token Lifecycle Management
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan enhances the Marketplace Reseller Desktop Repricing Tool by addressing the unresolved issue of OAuth token expiry not being surfaced to the user before marketplace API failures. The plan preserves all user-confirmed requirements and improves the architecture without introducing regressions. It includes concrete architecture, modules, workflows, schemas, APIs, security, deployment, observability, roadmap, and developer guidance. The plan also addresses the issue of DuckDB file size growth for high-SKU resellers by adding a 90-day retention cleanup job and a 2 GB size warning.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture plan is for a desktop application that allows independent marketplace resellers to manage their listings across eBay, Amazon, and Walmart. It includes a frontend built with Electron 32, Vue 3, and Tailwind CSS, a backend with Python 3.12 and FastAPI, and a data platform with DuckDB. The application uses OAuth2 tokens stored in the OS keychain via Electron safeStorage for security, and polls competitor prices every 15 minutes. The plan also addresses the issue of DuckDB file size growth for high-SKU resellers by adding a 90-day retention cleanup job and a 2 GB size warning.

technology_stack.data_platform: DuckDB for local columnar analytics on listing and price snapshots

functional_feature_map.marketplace_connection_management: OAuth2 per marketplace stored in OS keychain via Electron safeStorage
functional_feature_map.listing_snapshot_import: Listings table virtualized with vue-virtual-scroller for resellers with 10,000+ SKUs
functional_feature_map.margin_rule_builder: Margin profile CRUD: floor price, target margin %, ceiling price
functional_feature_map.reprice_queue_with_diff_ui: Diff-style reprice queue with margin impact column and bulk-approve flow
functional_feature_map.batch_reprice_submission: Batch submit reprices to marketplace APIs respecting rate limits
functional_feature_map.analytics_dashboard: Margin trend chart per ASIN and reprice action history
functional_feature_map.settings: Marketplace connections, poll frequency, token expiry warnings

system_components.frontend: Electron 32 main process, Vue 3 renderer process, preload script
system_components.backend: FastAPI sidecar, marketplace API clients, poller, repricer, analytics, settings
system_components.data: DuckDB file in OS app data directory

workflows.api_design: OAuth2 per marketplace, token storage via Electron safeStorage, TLS 1.3 for API calls
workflows.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 tokens stored in OS keychain via safeStorage, no credentials in DuckDB, TLS 1.3 for API calls
workflows.deployment_and_operations: Self-hosted update server, GitHub Actions for CI/CD, Sentry for crash reporting
workflows.observability: Sentry for crash reporting, local rotating log file in app data dir, no server-side monitoring for v1
workflows.cost_and_scaling: Self-hosted update server on low-cost VPS, DuckDB file size monitoring with 2 GB warning threshold and purge guidance
workflows.phased_implementation: 2-week sprints with beta reseller testers from week 4 onward
workflows.development_guidelines: Use Electron Forge for build, GitHub Actions for CI/CD, Vitest for Vue 3 components, Electron Forge build on macOS and Windows runners, auto-update server deploy on tag push
workflows.risks_and_tradeoffs: OAuth2 tokens for marketplace accounts are high-value targets; token storage and expiry management are critical for security and user experience. The plan addresses these issues with Electron safeStorage and token lifecycle management.

reference.thinking_summary: The key fix in round 3 is ISS-003: OAuth token expiry lifecycle management. I need to add a token expiry tracking field to marketplace_credentials, implement 24-hour pre-expiry warnings in the settings UI, and ensure the poller checks token freshness before each poll cycle. Amazon LWA, eBay OAuth 2.0, and Walmart each have different refresh token TTLs so the expiry monitoring must be per-marketplace. I will also document Walmart Marketplace rate limits which were flagged as missing.

fix_report.action_taken: Added token_expires_at field to marketplace_credentials; poller module now checks token freshness before each poll cycle and attempts refresh if within 2 hours of expiry; Settings page shows expiry countdown per marketplace with amber warning at 24 hours and red warning at 2 hours; Sentry alert raised if automatic refresh fails
fix_report.expected_outcome: Resellers will see proactive expiry warnings before marketplace API failures occur; automatic refresh will silently renew tokens in the happy path; manual re-authentication flow is clearly surfaced when refresh fails

reference.title: Marketplace Reseller Desktop Repricing Tool
reference.executive_summary: An Electron 32 desktop application with Vue 3 frontend and embedded Python 3.12/FastAPI sidecar, backed by a local DuckDB database, that automates competitor price monitoring and bulk repricing across Amazon, eBay, and Walmart Marketplace. OAuth2 tokens for each marketplace are stored in the OS keychain via Electron safeStorage with proactive expiry monitoring and automatic token refresh. A 15-minute poller refreshes competitor price points; the RepricingEngine evaluates each listing against margin rules and populates a diff-style approval queue showing current price, proposed price, and expected margin before batch submission.
reference.architecture_overview: Electron 32 main process manages the app lifecycle, OS keychain access via safeStorage, and auto-updater. The Vue 3 renderer process handles all UI via a typed IPC bridge exposed through a preload script. A Python 3.12/FastAPI sidecar is spawned as a child process on app start and listens on loopback 127.0.0.1:8923 — the Electron preload script proxies API calls to the sidecar via HTTP. DuckDB stores all listing snapshots and competitor price point history locally with no cloud dependency. The Poller module runs APScheduler jobs per connected marketplace on 15-minute intervals, checking token freshness before each cycle and refreshing tokens within 2 hours of expiry. The RepricingEngine processes each listing_snapshot against the active margin profile and pushes candidates to the reprice queue. The BatchSubmitter groups approved items by marketplace and submits reprices in rate-limit-compliant batches.
reference.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 32, Vue 3, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, PrimeVue, Pinia, Vue Query. Backend sidecar: Python 3.12, FastAPI, APScheduler, sp-api-python, ebaysdk, DuckDB Python SDK, cryptography library for keychain IPC. Data: DuckDB (columnar, local file). Distribution: Electron Forge with NSIS (Windows) and DMG (macOS); code-signed; self-hosted auto-update server. Observability: Sentry Electron SDK; local rotating log files.
reference.functional_feature_map: Marketplace Connections: connect Amazon, eBay, and Walmart via OAuth2 flows; settings UI shows token expiry countdown per marketplace with amber/red warnings; automatic background token refresh at 2-hour threshold. Listing Snapshot Import: fetch active listings from each connected marketplace via API, upsert into DuckDB listing_snapshots table. Competitor Price Polling: APScheduler 15-minute jobs per marketplace fetch competitor prices for all tracked SKUs and upsert competitor_price_points. Reprice Queue: RepricingEngine evaluates each listing against active margin profile, computes proposed_price, and populates queue with current vs. proposed diff and expected_margin_pct. Batch Reprice Submission: user approves or overrides items in queue; BatchSubmitter groups by marketplace and submits in rate-limit-compliant batches. Analytics Dashboard: DuckDB queries powering margin trend per ASIN, reprice action history, competitor price history charts in Vue renderer.
reference.system_components: Electron 32 main process (app lifecycle, safeStorage, auto-updater); Vue 3 renderer (SPA via preload IPC bridge); Python 3.12 FastAPI sidecar child process on loopback:8923; DuckDB file in OS app data directory; APScheduler for marketplace pollers; Sentry Electron SDK; self-hosted Electron Forge auto-update server.
reference.workflows: Token Expiry Monitoring and Refresh: On each Poller tick, the sidecar checks marketplace_credentials.token_expires_at for the target marketplace; if within 2 hours of expiry, attempts token refresh using the stored refresh token via the marketplace OAuth2 token endpoint; on success, updates token and token_expires_at in keychain and database; on failure, sets token_status=refresh_failed and emits an IPC event to the renderer causing the Settings page to display a red re-authenticate banner. The poller aborts the current cycle and logs the failure to avoid sending requests with an expired token. Settings page polls token status every 60 seconds and shows per-marketplace countdown: green (>24 hours), amber (2-24 hours), red (<2 hours or refresh_failed). Reprice Queue Approval and Batch Submit: User opens Reprice Queue page, views diff table with columns: SKU, marketplace, current_price, proposed_price, expected_margin_pct; clicks approve on individual rows or 'Approve All' for bulk; approved items submitted to BatchSubmitter; BatchSubmitter groups by marketplace, respects rate limits (Amazon SP-API: 1 req/sec with burst 5; eBay Sell API: 5 req/sec; Walmart Marketplace: 10 req/sec); submission results written to reprice_run_logs with status submitted or failed; failed items surfaced as retryable in queue.
reference.data_model: marketplace_credentials (marketplace ENUM amazon/ebay/walmart, token_ref VARCHAR keychain key, token_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, refresh_token_ref VARCHAR keychain key, token_status ENUM active/expiring_soon/refresh_failed, connected_at). listing_snapshots (id, marketplace, seller_sku, asin_or_item_id, title, current_price_cents INT, cost_price_cents INT, polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ). competitor_price_points (id, asin_or_item_id, marketplace, competitor_seller_id, price_cents INT, condition, polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE per polled_at). repricing_rules (id, name, floor_price_cents INT, target_margin_pct DECIMAL(5,2), ceiling_price_cents INT, applies_to ENUM all/category/sku_list, active BOOLEAN). reprice_run_logs (id, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ, marketplace, seller_sku, old_price_cents INT, proposed_price_cents INT, expected_margin_pct DECIMAL(5,2), status ENUM pending/approved/submitted/failed/overridden, submitted_at). channel_performance_reports (id, marketplace, report_date DATE, total_listings INT, repriced_count INT, avg_margin_pct DECIMAL).
reference.api_design: FastAPI sidecar on loopback:8923. GET /listings?marketplace=amazon&min_margin_gap=5 (listings with margin below threshold), GET /queue (pending reprice items with diff data), POST /queue/approve (body: {item_ids:[...]} or {approve_all: true}), POST /queue/submit (trigger BatchSubmitter for approved items), GET /analytics/margin-trend?sku=:sku&days=30 (DuckDB query), GET /analytics/reprice-history?days=7, GET /settings/connections (marketplace status + token expiry_at + token_status), POST /settings/connect/:marketplace (initiate OAuth2 flow), POST /settings/refresh/:marketplace (manual token refresh trigger), GET /health.
reference.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 token storage: all access tokens and refresh tokens stored via Electron safeStorage (OS AES-256 keychain) referenced by key name only in DuckDB marketplace_credentials; actual token values never written to DuckDB or log files. Token lifecycle: token_expires_at tracked in DuckDB (non-sensitive metadata); automatic refresh attempt at 2-hour threshold; refresh_failed status triggers UI warning and polling abort. Authorization headers redacted in sidecar debug logs via FastAPI middleware. DuckDB file stored in OS app data directory with user-only file permissions (chmod 600 on macOS/Linux, restricted ACL on Windows). TLS 1.3 enforced for all outbound marketplace API calls. Marketplace API rate limits enforced by token bucket per marketplace to comply with ToS.
reference.deployment_and_operations: Electron Forge builds DMG (macOS, notarized with Apple Developer ID) and NSIS installer (Windows, EV code-signed) via GitHub Actions matrix runners. Binaries uploaded to self-hosted Electron auto-update server on tag push. GitHub Actions CI: pytest for FastAPI sidecar (Poller token refresh logic, RepricingEngine margin calculation, BatchSubmitter rate limit compliance tests at 90% coverage), Vitest for Vue 3 components (queue table, settings page), Electron Forge build on macOS-latest and windows-latest runners. Environments: dev (npm run dev with hot reload), beta (code-signed build distributed to 10 reseller testers), release (full distribution via auto-update). DuckDB file stored in OS app data; no server infrastructure required for v1.
reference.observability: Sentry Electron SDK captures renderer and main process crashes with stack traces; per-version Sentry projects for regression tracking. Local rotating log file in OS app data directory (7-day retention): structured JSON logs from FastAPI sidecar with traceId per poll cycle and per reprice run. Key log events: poll_started, poll_completed (with SKU count), token_refresh_attempted, token_refresh_failed, reprice_submitted (with marketplace and count), reprice_failed (with error code). No server-side monitoring for v1; Sentry alert rules for token_refresh_failed error spike.
reference.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero infrastructure cost for v1: self-hosted auto-update server on 10 USD/month VPS; no cloud DB. DuckDB handles 10,000 SKUs with 90-day competitor price history (approx 260 million rows at 15-minute intervals) in under 500 MB; queries complete in <500ms via columnar scan. Growth path: multi-seat v2 will require central DuckDB or MotherDuck cloud sync; current local architecture scales to single-user workload without modification.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Amazon SP-API OAuth2 connection with token expiry monitoring and auto-refresh, listing snapshot import, 15-minute competitor price poller, margin rule builder (floor/ceiling/target margin), reprice queue with diff table and approve/approve-all flow, batch reprice submission to SP-API, basic analytics (margin trend per ASIN), DuckDB 90-day retention cleanup job, Electron Forge macOS and Windows builds with code signing, Sentry integration. Phase 2 (8 weeks): eBay and Walmart Marketplace connections with their respective OAuth2 and rate limit profiles, cross-marketplace analytics dashboard, channel performance reports, Parquet archive export for DuckDB purge. Phase 3 (future): multi-seat licensing with central sync server, AI margin rule suggestions, supplier cost import from CSV/API.
reference.development_guidelines: Python sidecar uses mypy strict mode and Ruff linting; RepricingEngine and Poller modules have 90% pytest coverage. Vue 3 components use TypeScript strict; Vitest for component unit tests. Electron preload IPC types generated from FastAPI OpenAPI spec via openapi-typescript. Marketplace API credentials never appear in logs — custom Sentry before-send hook strips Authorization headers. PR review required for all changes; changelog maintained per release for auto-updater release notes.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Amazon SP-API throttling during batch reprice submission for resellers with large catalogs. Mitigation: BatchSubmitter implements token bucket (1 req/sec default, configurable) with request queue; user sees progress bar during submission. Risk: DuckDB file corruption on crash during write. Mitigation: DuckDB WAL mode; Sentry crash report triggered on abnormal exit; user prompted to run CHECKPOINT on next launch if WAL file detected. Tradeoff: local DuckDB removes cloud egress costs but limits multi-device sync for v1 — accepted by target single-seat resellers; multi-seat sync deferred to v2 with MotherDuck or central Postgres.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Token expiry monitoring: per-marketplace token_expires_at stored in DuckDB; auto-refresh at 2-hour threshold; UI warning at 24 hours — ISS-003 resolved. Walmart Marketplace rate limits: 10 req/sec per credential, confirmed in Walmart Marketplace API docs. eBay rate limits: 5 req/sec for Sell Inventory API. DuckDB file size warning threshold set at 2 GB with archive-and-purge guidance in settings.

parsed.thinking_summary: The architecture plan aims to address the unresolved high-severity focus issues of operator data isolation at the database layer and the asynchronous design of the NPC portability flow, ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements and maintaining a carrier-grade SLA. The plan includes a robust security model with mTLS, JWT, and Redis for idempotency, and a scalable, fault-tolerant deployment on AWS ECS Fargate. The architecture is designed to be implementation-grade with a focus on security, compliance, and operability.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policy definitions scoped to operator_id in the data model and query-level enforcement.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Operator data is now isolated at the database level, ensuring compliance with CPNI regulations and interconnect agreements.

fix_report.action_taken: Designed the porting endpoint as asynchronous with a 202 response and implemented GET /v1/port-requests/:id polling for status updates.
fix_report.expected_outcome: The NPC portability flow now correctly reflects the regulatory requirement for asynchronous processing, with the API returning a 202 status upon completion and allowing the client to poll for the status.

parsed.title: MVNO SIM Lifecycle Management API v1.1
parsed.executive_summary: The revised architecture plan addresses the previously unresolved high-severity focus issues by ensuring operator data isolation at the database level and redesigning the NPC portability flow to be asynchronous. The plan maintains a carrier-grade SLA, complies with CPNI regulations, and is scalable and fault-tolerant.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture is a Go-based carrier REST API for MVNO SIM lifecycle operations with HLR integration and operator-scoped auth, deployed on AWS ECS Fargate with PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7, using mTLS and JWT for security, and designed for scalability and fault tolerance.

technology_stack.data_platform: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security, Redis 7 for idempotency keys and rate limiting
technology_stack.hosting: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with multi-AZ deployment
technology_stack.security: mTLS, JWT with operator-scoped claims, AWS Secrets Manager for secrets management
technology_stack.observability: Datadog APM, PagerDuty for SLA breach alerts

reference.thinking_summary: Resolving ISS-001 by adding explicit PostgreSQL RLS policy definitions ensuring operator_id-scoped query isolation at the database layer. Resolving ISS-002 by redesigning the number portability endpoint as async with 202 Accepted and a dedicated status polling resource, reflecting NPC regulatory processing latency. mTLS certificate lifecycle and CPNI audit logging get explicit treatment given the high risk level and CPNI compliance context.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit PostgreSQL RLS policy definitions to data_model section: CREATE POLICY operator_isolation ON sim_cards USING (operator_id = current_setting('app.operator_id')::uuid). All query paths now set app.operator_id from JWT claims before execution. api_design and security_and_compliance sections updated to describe enforcement mechanism.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Auditor confirms database-layer operator isolation is demonstrably enforced with RLS policy syntax present

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned portability endpoint: POST /v1/port-requests returns 202 Accepted with Location: /v1/port-requests/:id header. Added GET /v1/port-requests/:id returning status ENUM pending/validated/completed/rejected and NPC reference. NPC polling background job described. Workflow section updated with async portability state machine.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Portability flow correctly reflects NPC regulatory async processing; no more synchronous completion assumption

reference.title: MVNO SIM Lifecycle Management API — Carrier Grade
reference.executive_summary: A carrier-grade Go REST API enabling MVNO operators to manage full SIM card lifecycles including activation with HLR provisioning, suspension, async number portability via NPC, and data plan assignment. Deployed on AWS ECS Fargate with mTLS partner authentication, operator-scoped PostgreSQL RLS, Redis idempotency, and CPNI-compliant audit logging meeting FCC Part 64 requirements.
reference.architecture_overview: Go 1.22 Gin service on ECS Fargate (us-east-1, multi-AZ) sitting behind an ALB that terminates mTLS from MVNO operator systems. Service decomposes into sim_lifecycle, porting, data_plans, webhooks, hlr_client, and audit domain packages. HLR provisioning calls are wrapped by an HLRClient interface supporting circuit breaking and timeout handling. PostgreSQL 16 enforces operator data isolation via row-level security policies binding queries to operator_id from JWT claims. Redis 7 provides idempotency key deduplication and rate limit counters. Webhook worker delivers quota exhaustion callbacks to operator endpoints with exponential backoff retry.
reference.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, pgx/v5, go-redis/v9, otelgo for distributed tracing. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies, partitioned activation_events table. Cache: Redis 7 for idempotency TTL and rate counters. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB with mTLS termination, AWS Secrets Manager, CloudWatch, Datadog APM. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with golangci-lint, Go race detector, ECR, ECS rolling deploy.
reference.functional_feature_map: SIM Activation: ICCID validation, HLR provisioning, MSISDN assignment, idempotency enforcement. Suspension/Reactivation: atomic SIM state transitions with HLR sync. Number Portability: async NPC submission, regulatory validation polling, atomic subscriber migration on approval. Data Plan Assignment: quota configuration and rate plan binding with HLR update. Quota Webhook Delivery: operator endpoint callbacks with retry. Operator Audit Log: CPNI-compliant subscriber access logging.
reference.system_components: ALB with mTLS listener (operator certificate trust store); ECS Fargate cluster (4-20 tasks, CPU 60% target autoscale); Go API service with Gin router and operator_id middleware; HLRClient module with circuit breaker (10s timeout, 5-failure threshold); PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies and monthly activation_events partitions; Redis 7 for idempotency keys (24h TTL) and rate limit counters; SQS-backed webhook delivery worker with exponential backoff; Secrets Manager for HLR credentials and NPC API keys.
reference.workflows: SIM Activation: MVNO POST /v1/sims/activate with ICCID and operator JWT; idempotency key checked in Redis; ICCID validated against sim_cards table; HLRClient provisions SIM on HLR within 30s timeout; MSISDN assigned from operator pool; sim_cards row inserted with RLS operator_id binding; activation_event logged; 201 response with assigned MSISDN. Number Portability (Async): MVNO POST /v1/port-requests with ICCID and donor MSISDN; port_requests row created with status=pending; NPC API called to register portability request; 202 Accepted returned with Location: /v1/port-requests/:id; NPC polling job tracks status transitions pending->validated->completed; on completion subscriber record atomically migrated and HLR updated; webhook notifies operator. Quota Exhaustion Webhook: Policy enforcement node publishes quota event; webhook worker reads from SQS; operator endpoint called with event payload; exponential backoff on failure up to 72h.
reference.data_model: sim_cards (iccid VARCHAR PK, msisdn VARCHAR UNIQUE, operator_id UUID NOT NULL, status ENUM active/suspended/ported/cancelled, data_plan_id UUID FK, hlr_provisioned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). PostgreSQL RLS: CREATE POLICY operator_isolation ON sim_cards USING (operator_id = current_setting('app.operator_id')::uuid); policies identical on port_requests and activation_events. subscribers (id UUID PK, iccid VARCHAR FK, operator_subscriber_id VARCHAR, created_at). port_requests (id UUID PK, iccid VARCHAR FK, donor_msisdn VARCHAR, npc_reference VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/validated/completed/rejected, initiated_at, completed_at). data_plans (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID, name VARCHAR, quota_mb INT, rate_plan_id VARCHAR, validity_days INT). activation_events (id UUID PK, iccid VARCHAR FK, operator_id UUID, event_type VARCHAR, payload_json JSONB, created_at) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at). rate_plans (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID, plan_code VARCHAR, per_mb_rate NUMERIC).
reference.api_design: RESTful /v1 with Idempotency-Key header on all mutations. POST /v1/sims/activate {iccid, operator_subscriber_id, data_plan_id} -> 201 {msisdn, iccid, status}. POST /v1/sims/:iccid/suspend -> 200 {status}. POST /v1/sims/:iccid/reactivate -> 200 {status}. POST /v1/port-requests {iccid, donor_msisdn, target_msisdn} -> 202 {id, status, location}. GET /v1/port-requests/:id -> 200 {id, status ENUM, npc_reference, completed_at}. PUT /v1/sims/:iccid/data-plan {data_plan_id} -> 200 {quota_mb, validity_days}. GET /v1/audit-log?operator_id=&from=&to= -> 200 paginated CPNI audit entries. All endpoints enforce operator_id from JWT claims against RLS context variable.
reference.security_and_compliance: mTLS: ALB listener configured with operator CA bundle; operator certificates rotated every 365 days with OCSP stapling; revocation via OCSP responder URL embedded in certs. JWT: RS256, operator_id and allowed_operations claims; 1-hour expiry; Go middleware sets app.operator_id session variable for RLS before each query. CPNI: subscriber_access_log captures operator_id, iccid, action, requesting_user, justification, timestamp; append-only with no UPDATE/DELETE privileges; forwarded to Datadog SIEM. IP allowlist: per-operator CIDR ranges enforced at ALB security group. Rate limiting: 1000 req/min per operator enforced via Redis sliding window. FCC Part 64: subscriber data access restricted to authorized operator scope; CPNI disclosure procedures documented in runbook.
reference.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1a/1c/1d; ALB with mTLS, WAF (SQLi/XSS rules), and access logs to S3. GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint, go test -race, 85% coverage gate, docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy (minimum 2 healthy tasks). Staging SLA gate: automated p99 latency check against 200ms threshold before production promotion. Secrets Manager: HLR credentials, NPC API keys, DB password — 90-day rotation with Lambda rotator. Blue/green rollback via ECS service rollback to previous task definition. Aurora Multi-AZ with automated failover for PostgreSQL. Redis ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover.
reference.observability: Datadog APM with Go otel tracer; trace IDs propagated in all HLR and NPC calls. Custom metrics: sim_activation_duration_ms (p50/p95/p99), hlr_circuit_breaker_state, port_request_age_hours (histogram), webhook_delivery_attempt_count, operator_rate_limit_rejections. PagerDuty P1: HLR circuit breaker open >60s, activation p99 >200ms, ECS task count <2. PagerDuty P2: webhook delivery failure rate >5%, port request stuck in pending >4 hours. Structured JSON logs with correlation_id, operator_id, iccid fields for log-based alerting.
reference.cost_and_scaling: ~$800/month baseline for 4 Fargate tasks + Aurora Multi-AZ + Redis. HLR call volume drives primary scale; ECS autoscaling to 20 tasks at 1000 concurrent activations. Redis idempotency keys capped at 1M entries before LRU eviction. Activation_events monthly partitioning limits index bloat; S3 Glacier archival reduces hot storage cost at year 5.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (10 weeks): SIM activation with HLR integration, suspension/reactivation, operator mTLS auth, RLS enforcement, CPNI audit logging, CI/CD pipeline, Datadog integration. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Async number portability with NPC integration and polling endpoints, data plan assignment with quota sync. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Quota webhook delivery worker, operator audit log export API, bulk activation batch endpoint, eSIM groundwork.
reference.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 strict mode; golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, gosec enabled in CI. HLRClient always mocked in unit tests; integration tests use containerized HLR simulator. RLS policies tested with operator_id cross-contamination test suite. All database migrations via golang-migrate with rollback scripts. ADR required for any HLR vendor change.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HLR vendor outage during activation surge. Mitigation: circuit breaker queues requests for retry; operators receive 503 with Retry-After header. Risk: NPC regulatory changes affecting portability SLA. Mitigation: NPC adapter abstracted behind interface for swap without API contract change. Tradeoff: RLS over application-layer operator filtering chosen for defense-in-depth at cost of session variable overhead per query. Risk: CPNI audit log growth exceeding PostgreSQL performance at high volume — mitigation: forward to Datadog SIEM and archive to S3 after 90 days.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Async portability confirmed: 202 + polling pattern selected over webhook-only to support operators without public callback endpoints. RLS selected over schema-per-operator: schema-per-operator rejected due to migration complexity at 50+ MVNO operator scale. Certificate rotation period set to 365 days with OCSP; shorter rotation deemed operationally disruptive for carrier-grade partners.

parsed.title: Livestock Health Tracking Mobile App v2
parsed.executive_summary: The revised architecture plan addresses the previously unresolved high-severity issues by correcting the technology stack to Flutter 3 with Dart, incorporating SQLCipher for device-level encryption, and updating the conflict resolution policy to prevent the silencing of concurrent health event edits. This ensures compliance with the confirmed requirements and security baseline, while maintaining the offline-first approach and ensuring data integrity for health event records.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture plan for the Livestock Health Tracking Mobile App is designed to provide a robust, secure, and user-friendly solution for livestock producers to track animal health events, vaccinations, and weight measurements. It features a mobile app with offline capabilities for field use, cloud sync for data integrity, and comprehensive traceability reporting. The plan includes a Flutter 3 frontend, Node.js backend, PostgreSQL and SQLite databases, and GCP hosting, with a focus on security, compliance, and operability.
parsed.technology_stack: Flutter 3 with Dart for the mobile frontend, Node.js 20 with Express for the backend, PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL for cloud storage, SQLite with SQLCipher for offline storage, Redis 7 for session cache and report job queue, and Firebase Auth for authentication.

reference.title: Livestock Health Tracking Mobile App
reference.executive_summary: Flutter 3 offline-first mobile app for livestock producers logging individual animal health events, vaccinations, and RFID ear tag scans in remote paddocks without cellular connectivity. Local Drift/SQLite storage with SQLCipher encryption queues records for batch sync to a Node.js Express Cloud Run API backed by PostgreSQL 16. Firebase Auth with operation_id row-level security enforces multi-operation tenancy. Conflict resolution keeps both concurrent records with conflict_flag rather than silently discarding, honoring the traceability completeness constraint.
reference.architecture_overview: Flutter 3 mobile app with Drift ORM managing local SQLite (SQLCipher-encrypted) for offline-first data entry. Riverpod 2 providers implement optimistic local write: health events, vaccination records, and RFID scan results written immediately to device SQLite SyncQueue, then batch-uploaded to Cloud Run Node.js Express API on connectivity restore. PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL on GCP stores the authoritative animal health record with operation_id row-level security for multi-operation tenancy. Sync conflict policy: server creates both records with conflict_flag=true when concurrent edits detected, never discarding either. Firebase Auth issues JWT with operation_id custom claim; Express middleware validates JWT and sets PostgreSQL session variable for RLS enforcement. Bull queue on Redis Memorystore handles background traceability PDF report generation.
reference.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.22 with Dart 3, Drift 2.x SQLite ORM, SQLCipher 4.x for device database encryption, Riverpod 2 for state management, flutter_blue_plus for BLE RFID reader (Allflex RS420) pairing, Firebase Auth SDK for JWT token management. Backend: Node.js 20, Express 4, firebase-admin for JWT validation, Bull 4 with Redis for report job queue, pg driver for PostgreSQL. Cloud: GCP Cloud Run (min 1 instance), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16, Redis Memorystore, Firebase Auth, Google Cloud Monitoring, Firebase Crashlytics. CI/CD: Bitrise for Flutter iOS/Android builds and TestFlight/Play Store distribution; GitHub Actions for Node.js API.
reference.functional_feature_map: RFID Ear Tag Scan: flutter_blue_plus connects to Allflex RS420 BLE reader, receives tag number, looks up or creates animal record locally. Health Event Entry: offline-capable form with event_type selector (illness/treatment/vaccination/death/observation), symptom multi-select from species-specific symptom library, treatment drug picker with automatic withdrawal period calculation from drug database. Vaccination Record: records vaccine product, dose, route, and computes withdrawal_date from drug withdrawal_days. Lot and Pen Management: assign animals to lots, view lot summary with active animal count and pending health events. Sync Status: SyncNotifier watches connectivity, shows pending upload count in app bar, executes batch upload on connectivity restore. Market Shipment Traceability Report: links all animals in a lot to their complete health history and validates antibiotic withdrawal compliance before shipment (Phase 2).
reference.system_components: Flutter App: Drift database (SQLCipher key from platform secure storage), SyncQueue table (local_id, entity_type, payload_json, sync_status, attempt_count), AnimalRepository (Drift queries + cloud sync overlay), HealthEventRepository (offline write + queue), SyncCoordinator (connectivity watch via connectivity_plus, batch upload to /sync endpoint). Node.js Express API: authMiddleware (Firebase JWT validation, operation_id extraction), syncRouter (POST /sync/health-events batch upsert with conflict detection), animalsRouter (CRUD with RLS), reportRouter (Bull job enqueue for PDF generation). PostgreSQL 16: animals, health_events (with conflict_flag), vaccination_records, lot_assignments, market_shipments tables with operation_id RLS policies. Redis Memorystore: Bull job queue for report generation, Firebase session cache. Firebase Crashlytics: mobile crash reports with device model and OS version tags.
reference.workflows: Offline Health Event Logging Workflow: Field worker scans RFID tag with Allflex BLE reader via flutter_blue_plus. Flutter app looks up animal by rfid_tag_number in local Drift SQLite. Worker selects event type (e.g., vaccination), picks vaccine product from local drug list, enters dose and route. Drift writes health_event and vaccination_record to local SQLite with sync_status=pending. SyncNotifier detects no connectivity; event remains in SyncQueue. When paddock connectivity restores, SyncCoordinator batches pending events (up to 50 per request) and POSTs to /sync/health-events with idempotency key. Server inserts records scoped to operation_id, checks for concurrent edits within 60s window, creates conflict_flag=true duplicates if found. Returns local_id to server_id mapping; Drift updates sync_status to synced. Market Shipment Traceability Workflow (Phase 2): Ranch manager selects a lot for shipment, app generates traceability report request. Bull job fetches all animals in lot, joins health_event history and vaccination_records, validates withdrawal dates are cleared for all treatment records, generates PDF via PDFKit, uploads to Cloud Storage, pushes download link notification to manager.
reference.data_model: animals: {id UUID PK, operation_id UUID FK, rfid_tag_number VARCHAR, species VARCHAR, birth_date DATE, sex CHAR(1), dam_id UUID nullable FK, sire_id UUID nullable FK, current_lot_id UUID FK, status VARCHAR}. health_events: {id UUID PK, animal_id UUID FK, operation_id UUID FK, event_type VARCHAR, event_date DATE, notes TEXT, recorded_by UUID FK, conflict_flag BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, client_created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, server_created_at TIMESTAMPTZ}. vaccination_records: {id UUID PK, health_event_id UUID FK, vaccine_product VARCHAR, dose_ml NUMERIC, route VARCHAR, withdrawal_days INT, withdrawal_date DATE}. lot_assignments: {animal_id UUID FK, lot_id UUID FK, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, released_at TIMESTAMPTZ}. Local SQLite SyncQueue (Drift): {local_id TEXT PK, entity_type TEXT, payload_json TEXT, sync_status TEXT, attempt_count INT, created_at INTEGER}. NOTE: data_model uses generic VARCHAR types rather than proper ENUM constraints — this is an intentional quality flaw in this revision demonstrating a plan that fixes the blocking contract violations but still has a data model quality gap.
reference.api_design: POST /sync/health-events — accepts {idempotency_key, events: [{local_id, animal_id, event_type, event_date, notes, client_created_at, vaccination_payload?}]}. Returns {synced: [{local_id, server_id}], conflicts: [{local_id, conflict_server_id}]}. GET /animals/:id — returns animal with latest health event summary. POST /animals — create animal with rfid_tag_number (unique per operation_id). GET /lots — list lots with animal counts for operation. POST /reports/traceability — enqueue PDF report Bull job for lot_id. All routes require Firebase JWT Authorization header; operation_id extracted from custom claim.
reference.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth JWT validated in Express authMiddleware; operation_id custom claim used to set PostgreSQL session variable app.operation_id before each query, enabling RLS enforcement. PostgreSQL RLS: all tables include operation_id column with policy USING (operation_id = current_setting('app.operation_id')::uuid). Device encryption: SQLCipher 4.x on local Drift SQLite database. Key derivation: iOS uses SecKeyCreateRandomKey in Keychain with kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclave binding; Android uses Android Keystore AES-256-GCM key. Key stored in platform secure storage via flutter_secure_storage; database re-keyed on password change. USDA APHIS traceability: operation_id isolation ensures farm operations cannot access other producers' animal records. Firebase Crashlytics configured with data redaction to exclude animal health event content from crash reports.
reference.deployment_and_operations: GCP Cloud Run Node.js service: min-instances=1 (prevent cold start on morning sync burst), max-instances=20, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 via Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar. Redis Memorystore for Bull queue. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, Jest tests with Cloud SQL emulator, docker build, Cloud Run deploy on main merge. Bitrise Flutter CI: flutter analyze, flutter test, flutter build ios (--release), flutter build appbundle, upload to TestFlight (iOS) and Play Store internal track (Android) on main merge. Bi-weekly field testing cycle: internal track promoted to production track after QA sign-off on offline sync and RFID BLE pairing. Firebase Crashlytics integrated in Flutter app for production crash visibility. Google Cloud Monitoring alerts: Cloud Run error rate >1%, sync endpoint P99 latency >3s.
reference.observability: Firebase Crashlytics: mobile crash reports with Flutter stack traces, device model, OS version, and Flutter version tags. Custom Crashlytics keys: sync_queue_depth (pending upload count at time of crash), last_sync_status. Google Cloud Monitoring: Cloud Run request count, error rate, P99 latency. Custom metrics logged via Cloud Logging: sync_batch_size (events per batch upload), sync_conflict_count (per-operation daily count), ble_connection_success_rate (RFID reader pairing success ratio). Alert: sync_conflict_count >10/day for any operation triggers investigation; high conflict rates indicate clock skew between worker devices.
reference.cost_and_scaling: GCP estimated $380/month: Cloud Run min-instances=1 $45, burst scaling to 20 instances during morning/evening sync $80, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 db-standard-2 $190, Redis Memorystore Basic 1GB $40, Firebase Auth free tier, Bitrise Team plan $25/month. Scales to 500 concurrent farm operations without architecture changes; Cloud Run auto-scales sync burst load; Cloud SQL read replica added when analytics queries degrade sync endpoint performance.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Flutter iOS and Android app with RFID ear tag scan, health event and vaccination logging, offline SQLite SyncQueue with SQLCipher, Firebase Auth, Node.js sync API, PostgreSQL with RLS, Bitrise CI/CD to TestFlight and Play Store internal track. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Weight measurement logging with Bluetooth scale integration, lot management and pen assignment, market shipment traceability PDF report generation via Bull queue, antibiotic withdrawal compliance validation. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Herd analytics dashboard (death rate, treatment frequency by lot), packer buyer portal integration for electronic traceability record submission, bulk import from legacy paper records via CSV.
reference.development_guidelines: Flutter: Dart null safety enforced, Riverpod 2 for all state (no setState in feature widgets), Drift migrations versioned and tested with in-memory SQLite in unit tests. SQLCipher key must never be logged or included in crash reports — Crashlytics redaction configured. Node.js: ESLint with no-console rule (use structured logging via pino), all PostgreSQL queries use parameterized statements (no string interpolation), operation_id extracted from JWT claim never from request body. Sync idempotency: SyncCoordinator includes idempotency_key (UUID v4 generated per batch) in POST /sync/health-events; server stores processed idempotency keys in Redis for 24 hours to handle retry deduplication.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Allflex RS420 BLE connection unreliable on certain Android device models due to manufacturer-specific BLE stack variations. Mitigation: manual RFID tag number entry fallback always available in UI; field test with target device hardware before GA release. Risk: SQLCipher key loss (device wipe without backup) results in unrecoverable local SQLite data. Mitigation: sync pending queue items before enabling device wipe; warn users if sync_queue_depth >0 when logout initiated. Tradeoff: keep-both-with-conflict-flag policy creates duplicate health event records requiring manual review by ranch manager for conflicting entries; accepted because data completeness for USDA APHIS traceability is more important than clean records. Risk: Cloud Run cold start (when min-instances not warm) causes 3-5s first-sync delay for early-morning field workers; mitigated by min-instances=1 baseline.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Conflict resolution confirmed as keep-both-with-conflict-flag (not last-write-wins) per explicit constraint. SQLCipher 4.x selected over Android-native Room encryption because Drift SQLite ORM has first-class SQLCipher support via sqlcipher_flutter_libs. RFID reader confirmed as Allflex RS420 BLE; flutter_blue_plus supports BLE central mode on both iOS and Android.

parsed.title: CLI Tool for Food Truck Operators and Small Restaurant Owners to Manage Digital Menu Boards
parsed.executive_summary: This architecture plan outlines the development of a CLI tool for food truck operators and small restaurant owners to manage digital menu boards. The plan addresses current focus issues, preserves user-confirmed requirements, and improves the architecture without introducing regressions. The plan includes a CLI framework, backend REST API, security measures, data platform, hosting target, privacy retention policy, future scope, constraints, observability, cost and scaling considerations, phased implementation, and development guidelines. The plan also addresses risks and tradeoffs, and resolves open questions.
parsed.architecture_overview: The architecture plan includes a CLI tool built on Node.js 22 with TypeScript and Commander.js for CLI commands, a REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform, JWT authentication, PostgreSQL 16 for data storage, and deployment on DigitalOcean App Platform with npm package distribution. The plan prioritizes security, scalability, and user-friendly operations.

technology_stack.backend_stack: Node.js 22 with TypeScript, commands implemented via Commander.js
technology_stack.hosting_target: DigitalOcean App Platform for the API backend; CLI distributed as npm package
technology_stack.security_and_compliance: JWT tokens with 24-hour expiry, HTTPS-only API calls, credentials stored in OS keychain or encrypted local config file
technology_stack.observability: Datadog APM on the backend API, structured JSON logs, CLI error reporting with request IDs for support
technology_stack.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform handles auto-scaling adequately for this traffic profile
technology_stack.phased_implementation: Iterative development with weekly releases via npm publish CI pipeline
technology_stack.development_guidelines: Use GitHub Actions for CI/CD, npm for CLI distribution, and TypeScript for type safety.
technology_stack.risks_and_tradeoffs: The plan balances simplicity and security, with tradeoffs in API complexity and development effort. Risks include potential scalability issues and security vulnerabilities if not properly managed.

system_components.backend_api: REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform
system_components.security_and_compliance: JWT tokens with 24-hour expiry, HTTPS-only API calls, credentials stored in OS keychain or encrypted local config file
system_components.observability_stack: Datadog APM agent on App Platform
system_components.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform with 2 replicas, managed PostgreSQL database, and npm package deployment
system_components.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions with npm publish on tag, Datadog APM agent on App Platform

workflows.menu_item_operations: CRUD operations for menu items, availability toggle, price update
workflows.display_board_operations: List, status, zone management, availability window CRUD
workflows.authentication_workflow: JWT issued by /auth/login endpoint, stored in OS keychain via keytar npm package
workflows.rate_limiting_workflow: Rate limiting on the 86 endpoint, retry with exponential backoff on 5xx responses
workflows.error_handling_workflow: CLI prints human-readable error messages with request IDs for support escalation

authentication.storage: OS keychain or encrypted local config file

deployment_and_operations.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions with npm publish on tag, Datadog APM agent on App Platform

reference.thinking_summary: Designing a simple CLI tool for food truck operators with a companion REST API backend. Key decisions: Commander.js for typed command definitions with auto-generated help, JWT in OS keychain for secure credential storage without asking operators to manage files, PostgreSQL for board/item state with an append-only item_updates table for audit history. Keeping the API surface minimal — four core commands map to four API endpoints — so the MVP can ship in 8 weeks and be validated with operators before adding scheduling and web UI.
reference.title: Digital Menu Board CLI Architecture
reference.executive_summary: A Node.js 22 TypeScript CLI tool (menuboard-cli) for food truck operators and small restaurant owners to manage cloud-connected digital display boards from the command line. Operators run commands to 86 items, update prices, manage display zones, and configure scheduled availability windows. A companion REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform persists board state in PostgreSQL and pushes changes to display hardware. The CLI is distributed via npm and works on macOS and Linux terminals with JWT-based authentication stored securely in the OS keychain.
reference.architecture_overview: Two-component architecture: a Node.js 22 CLI package (menuboard-cli on npm) and a companion REST API backend on DigitalOcean App Platform. The CLI wraps authenticated HTTPS calls to the API; no logic executes locally beyond argument parsing, output formatting, and credential retrieval from the OS keychain. The API backend manages all persistent state in a PostgreSQL 16 managed database — menu items, board configurations, display zones, and availability schedules. Display boards poll the API on a configurable TTL (default 10 seconds) to refresh their displayed content, ensuring 86 status changes are visible on screens within one poll cycle. Board online/offline status is tracked via last_seen_at heartbeat timestamps.
reference.technology_stack: CLI: Node.js 22, TypeScript 5.4, Commander.js for command/flag parsing, keytar for OS keychain credential storage, chalk for terminal output formatting, ora for progress spinners. API: Node.js 22, TypeScript 5.4, Express 4, pg driver with pgBouncer connection pooling. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Databases. Infrastructure: DigitalOcean App Platform (2 replicas), GitHub Actions CI/CD. Observability: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging. Distribution: npm package registry.
reference.functional_feature_map: menuboard item:eighty-six <item-id|name> — marks item as sold out, pushes to all configured boards immediately. menuboard item:restore <item-id|name> — marks item as available again. menuboard item:price <item-id> --price <cents> — updates displayed price in cents to avoid floating-point issues. menuboard board:list — shows all operator boards with online/offline status and item counts. menuboard board:status <board-id> — displays current zone layout and all item availability states. menuboard schedule:set <item-id> --start 07:00 --end 11:00 --days mon,tue,wed,thu,fri — configures automatic show/hide windows. menuboard auth:login --email — initiates authentication and stores JWT in OS keychain.
reference.system_components: menuboard-cli npm package: Commander.js command tree, HTTP client with retry logic, keytar credential vault integration, chalk-formatted tabular output for board status. REST API: Express router with versioned /api/v1 prefix, JWT middleware for auth, operator ownership validation middleware, menu-items module, boards module, schedules module. PostgreSQL 16: display_boards, menu_items, display_zones, availability_schedules, item_updates (audit log). DigitalOcean App Platform: 2 API replicas with health check on /health, App Platform Managed DB connection, Datadog APM agent sidecar.
reference.workflows: Item 86 Workflow: Operator runs 'menuboard item:eighty-six tacos'. CLI retrieves JWT from keychain, calls PATCH /api/v1/items/tacos/availability with body {availability: 'eighty_six'}. API validates operator owns the board containing the item, updates menu_items.availability, inserts record to item_updates audit log, returns updated item. CLI prints 'Tacos marked as 86d on Truck Board 1'. Board polls API within 10 seconds and updates display. Price Update Workflow: Operator runs 'menuboard item:price breakfast-burrito --price 1295'. CLI calls PATCH /api/v1/items/breakfast-burrito/price. API validates ownership, updates price_cents field, logs change to item_updates with previous_price_cents for rollback reference. CLI confirms with formatted output showing old and new price.
reference.data_model: display_boards (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, location_label VARCHAR, status ENUM online/offline/error, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). menu_items (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_id UUID FK nullable, name VARCHAR, price_cents INT, category VARCHAR, availability ENUM available/eighty_six, display_order INT, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). display_zones (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_name VARCHAR, zone_type ENUM main/specials/drinks, position INT). availability_schedules (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, days_of_week SMALLINT, active BOOLEAN). item_updates (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, operator_id UUID FK, change_type ENUM price/availability/schedule, previous_value JSONB, new_value JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
reference.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. Authentication: POST /auth/login (issue JWT), POST /auth/refresh. Items: PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/availability {availability: 'eighty_six'|'available'}, PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/price {price_cents: INT}, GET /api/v1/items?board_id=:id. Boards: GET /api/v1/boards (list operator boards with status), GET /api/v1/boards/:id/status (full zone and item state). Schedules: POST /api/v1/schedules {item_id, start_time, end_time, days_of_week}, DELETE /api/v1/schedules/:id. All responses: {data, meta} envelope. Errors: {error: {code, message, request_id}}.
reference.security_and_compliance: Authentication: JWT HS256 signed with 512-bit secret, 24-hour expiry. CLI stores token via keytar in OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux Secret Service, Windows Credential Manager) — never written to disk as plaintext. Operator ownership enforcement: every board/item mutation runs SELECT board_id FROM display_boards WHERE operator_id = $jwt_sub before executing; returns 403 if mismatch. Transport: HTTPS only; HTTP connections refused. Rate limiting: 60 requests/minute per JWT on all mutation endpoints via express-rate-limit. Input validation: Joi schemas on all request bodies to prevent injection. API deployed with Helmet.js security headers.
reference.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform: 2 replicas of the Node.js API, auto-deploy on push to main via GitHub integration. Managed PostgreSQL 16 on same DO datacenter (NYC3) with automatic daily backups and point-in-time recovery. pgBouncer connection pooling at 20 connections per replica. Health check endpoint GET /health returns DB ping status. GitHub Actions pipeline: on push to main — tsc --noEmit type check, Jest unit tests (70% coverage gate), Docker build, App Platform deploy via doctl CLI. On git tag v* — run full test suite, npm publish --access public.
reference.observability: Datadog APM on API with trace sampling at 100% for write operations (item availability/price changes). Structured JSON logs on every request: operator_id, board_id, command, duration_ms, status_code, request_id. Custom Datadog metrics: menuboard.item_eighty_six_count (counter by board), menuboard.price_updates_per_hour, menuboard.board_offline_duration_minutes. Alert: board offline > 30 minutes triggers Datadog monitor notification to operator email.
reference.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform Basic plan (~$24/month for 2 replicas) and Managed PostgreSQL Basic plan (~$15/month) totals approximately $40/month for the backend. npm package hosting is free. At 1000 daily CLI commands across all operators, PostgreSQL handles load comfortably on the smallest instance class. Scaling path: upgrade to DO App Platform Professional tier and add read replicas when operator count exceeds 500.
reference.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Core four commands (item:eighty-six, item:restore, item:price, board:list), JWT auth with keychain storage, PostgreSQL schema with item_updates audit log, REST API on App Platform, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, npm package published. Phase 2 (3 weeks): menuboard board:status with zone breakdown, schedule:set for availability windows, board heartbeat and online/offline tracking, Datadog APM integration. Phase 3 (future): Web dashboard UI for operators preferring GUI, multi-location chain support with board grouping, image upload for menu items, webhook push to boards replacing polling.
reference.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout CLI and API. Commander.js command files colocated with their HTTP client calls — one file per command group (items.ts, boards.ts, schedules.ts). API route handlers thin — business logic in service modules, not Express handlers. Jest unit tests for all service module functions with PostgreSQL queries mocked via pg-mock. Integration tests against a real Postgres test container in CI. Semantic versioning for CLI; breaking flag changes require major version bump. CLI --help output tested as part of CI to prevent accidental interface regressions.
reference.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Board polling interval creates up to 10-second delay between CLI 86 command and screen update. Mitigation: document this to operators; Phase 2 can add WebSocket push for instant updates if delay is unacceptable. Risk: keytar dependency requires native OS keychain access, which can fail in some CI or container environments. Mitigation: fallback to encrypted local config file with user warning. Tradeoff: Commander.js over Ink/React-based TUI — simpler implementation and faster startup time at the cost of no interactive prompts in MVP. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL over Redis for item state — durability and relational queries justify the cost over a cache-only approach.
reference.open_questions_resolved: Selected Commander.js over yargs for better TypeScript ergonomics and built-in help generation. Chose npm distribution over Homebrew tap for faster release iteration. Selected OS keychain via keytar over environment variable credentials to avoid operators accidentally committing secrets to dotfiles.
